title: Prevention of Epithelial Ovarian Cancer
journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Medicine
summary: This article reviews prevention strategies for epithelial ovarian cancer, emphasizing both risk reduction and treatment approaches across different patient populations. Surgery, chemoprevention, and lifestyle factor modifications all effectively reduce epithelial ovarian cancer incidence, representing the primary preventive interventions available. Women carrying inherited major gene mutations that substantially elevate ovarian cancer risk represent a critical population for intervention, as surgical approaches have demonstrated favorable outcomes in managing this heightened risk. The multifaceted prevention framework combines surgical intervention—which serves dual roles in both cancer prevention and treatment—with pharmacological chemoprevention and modifiable lifestyle factors. Together, these strategies provide a comprehensive approach to ovarian cancer prevention tailored to individual risk profiles, with particular emphasis on identifying and managing patients with genetic predispositions. The article underscores that effective ovarian cancer prevention requires risk stratification and personalized selection among these complementary intervention modalities.

title: Early Ovarian Cancer Detection in the Age of Fallopian Tube Precursors: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines emerging biomarkers for early detection of high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), the most common type of ovarian cancer, with particular attention to its origins in fallopian tube epithelium. Traditional serum biomarkers CA125 and HE4 show differential performance depending on menopausal status, with HE4 demonstrating superior specificity in premenopausal patients while CA125 performs better postmenopausally; however, both markers are limited by elevation in benign conditions including endometriosis. Novel biomarkers across multiple categories—including proteins (Annexin A2, IL-6), epigenetic markers (DNA methylation patterns in PCDHB12, C17orf64, IRX2, and TUBB6), genetic mutations (TP53, BRCA1/2, PALB2), and metabolites (ceramide)—show promise for improving detection accuracy, with some multi-marker panels achieving near-perfect discrimination. The review identifies epigenetic biomarkers and multi-protein combinations as particularly valuable approaches, with combination strategies consistently outperforming single biomarkers in achieving sensitivity and specificity thresholds necessary for clinical implementation. Emerging detection methods including uterine lavage for high-risk populations and circulating tumor DNA analysis represent novel strategies for early HGSC identification before symptomatic presentation.

title: Low-dose aspirin use and risk of ovarian cancer: a combined analysis from two nationwide studies in Denmark and Sweden
summary: This combined Danish-Swedish study examined the relationship between low-dose aspirin use and ovarian cancer risk in a large population-based cohort. Overall, low-dose aspirin use showed no strong association with ovarian cancer risk, but important subgroup differences emerged: nulliparous women demonstrated a protective effect (OR = 0.80), while parous women showed no significant risk reduction. The protective association in nulliparous women suggests that aspirin's anti-inflammatory properties may be particularly relevant in this reproductive phenotype. Notably, the relationship between aspirin use and ovarian cancer risk was further modified by cardiovascular disease history, with women without prior CVD showing reduced risk while those with CVD history showed increased risk. These findings indicate that the chemopreventive potential of low-dose aspirin for ovarian cancer is not uniform across populations and appears contingent on reproductive and cardiovascular factors, highlighting the importance of personalized risk stratification in cancer prevention strategies.

title: Molecular and pathologic data to guide selection of patients with endometrioid endometrial cancer for ovarian preservation
journal: International journal of gynecological cancer : official journal of the International Gynecological Cancer Society
summary: This study establishes molecular and pathologic criteria to identify endometrioid endometrial cancer patients who can safely preserve their ovaries during surgery. Molecular subtyping reveals dramatically different risks of concurrent ovarian malignancy, with microsatellite instability-high/MMRd and copy number-high/TP53 abnormal tumors carrying approximately 50% concurrent ovarian tumor rates, while POLE-mutant and copy number-low/NSMP tumors show only 0-4% rates. Clinical-pathologic features including higher tumor grade, lymphovascular space invasion, deep myometrial invasion, positive peritoneal cytology, and large tumor size are all independently associated with increased concurrent ovarian disease risk. The integration of molecular profiling (via MMR and p53 immunohistochemistry) with histopathologic assessment enables improved risk stratification compared to pathology alone, allowing selection of low-risk patients for ovarian preservation while avoiding the long-term morbidities of surgical menopause including heart disease and osteoporosis. Careful intraoperative examination can identify approximately 50% of concurrent ovarian tumors and should complement preoperative molecular and pathologic evaluation in clinical decision-making.

title: Diagnosing and staging epithelial ovarian cancer by serum glycoproteomic profiling
summary: This study demonstrates that blood glycoproteomic profiling enables effective diagnosis and staging of epithelial ovarian cancer through analysis of characteristic glycopeptide biomarkers. A glycoprotein biomarker panel achieved 83.5% sensitivity and specificity in distinguishing epithelial ovarian cancer from benign pelvic lesions, with particular utility in stage stratification. Fucosylated multi-antennary glycopeptide markers were significantly elevated in late-stage compared to early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer, providing a quantitative staging signature. Blood glycopeptide biomarker profiles reflect glycosylation patterns in circulating and tumor tissue proteins, suggesting a mechanistic link between tumor-associated glycosylation changes and detectable serum signatures. These findings support the clinical application of glycoproteomics as a minimally invasive blood-based tool for both detecting epithelial ovarian cancer and assessing disease stage, addressing a critical need for improved diagnostic discrimination in patients with pelvic masses.

title: Variables affecting CA15.3 tumor antigen expression and antibodies against it in female National Health and Nutritional Survey (NHANES) participants
journal: Cancer epidemiology, biomarkers & prevention : a publication of the American Association for Cancer Research, cosponsored by the American Society of Preventive Oncology
summary:
# Summary

MUC1 (CA15.3) is a glycoprotein normally expressed at low levels on glandular epithelium but becomes severely overexpressed across the entire surface of cancer cells, where it promotes tumor growth and spread while suppressing immune responses through interactions with signaling proteins like EGFR, Src, and beta-catenin. Elevated CA15.3 antigen levels associate with poor cancer prognosis, but importantly, naturally occurring anti-MUC1 antibodies associate with improved outcomes and reduced ovarian cancer risk, particularly for serous subtypes. In this NHANES population study, endometriosis emerged as a significant predictor of elevated CA15.3 levels and reduced anti-MUC1 antibodies, reinforcing the established link between endometriosis and increased ovarian cancer risk. Reproductive factors demonstrate complex associations: increased ovulatory cycles paradoxically reduce anti-MUC1 antibodies while increasing cancer risk across breast, endometrial, and ovarian tissues, whereas parity and prolonged oral contraceptive use lower CA15.3 levels. Importantly, African-American women showed significantly higher CA15.3 expression with lower antibody responses, potentially contributing to documented disparities in MUC1-associated cancer prognosis, while obesity and smoking further suppressed protective antibody responses through elevated myeloid-derived suppressor cells.

title: Increased TP53 somatic evolution in peritoneal washes of individuals with BRCA1 germline mutations
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that BRCA1 germline carriers exhibit accelerated TP53 somatic evolution in peritoneal washes, a finding with implications for understanding high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) risk. Using ultra-deep duplex sequencing, the researchers detected significantly elevated pathogenic TP53 mutations in peritoneal fluid from BRCA1 carriers compared to BRCA2 carriers and controls, with burden increasing with age, prior chemotherapy, and higher BMI. TP53 mutations in peritoneal washes and cervical cytology resembled cancer mutations in their distribution patterns, clustering at hotspot codons in the DNA binding domain and showing predominantly C>T transitions characteristic of age-related mutations. While identical pathogenic TP53 mutations were identified in both peritoneal and cervical samples from five individuals, these mutations were largely absent from blood, suggesting local somatic evolution in gynecological tissues rather than systemic clonal expansion. The excess of pathogenic TP53 mutations in BRCA1 carriers may provide a mechanistic link to their elevated HGSC risk, as these mutations likely originate from precursor lesions in the fallopian tube that can disseminate to the peritoneal cavity and potentially progress to serous tubal intraepithelial carcinomas.

title: Predictive Role of HE4 in Diagnosis of Ovarian Tumors
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

The study evaluates serum biomarkers CA-125 and HE4 for distinguishing benign from malignant ovarian tumors, a clinically important challenge given that approximately 20% of women develop ovarian masses. While CA-125 demonstrates high sensitivity (95%) and negative predictive value (72%) for detecting malignancy, it suffers from high false positivity in benign conditions, leading to unnecessary interventions. HE4 shows superior specificity (93%) and positive predictive value (98%) when combined with CA-125, enabling better discrimination of pelvic mass pathology preoperatively. The combination of both markers significantly outperforms either marker alone, with improved diagnostic accuracy in differentiating benign, borderline, and malignant tumors. These findings suggest that dual biomarker assessment using CA-125 and HE4 provides enhanced preoperative discrimination of ovarian pathology and may help reduce unnecessary surgical interventions in women with benign disease.

title: Associations between common contraceptive use and circulating inflammatory biomarkers
summary: This epidemiological study examined associations between contraceptive methods and circulating inflammatory biomarkers in the context of ovarian cancer risk. Oral contraceptive use was associated with declining ovarian cancer incidence, yet paradoxically increased C-reactive protein levels by approximately 9.9% per five years of use, suggesting a pro-inflammatory effect that may occur through mechanisms distinct from cancer prevention. In contrast, intrauterine device use was associated with reduced CRP levels over time and did not elevate long-term circulating inflammatory markers, despite its lack of direct ovarian cancer risk reduction. Tubal ligation similarly showed no association with elevated inflammatory markers long-term, while maintaining its protective effect against ovarian cancer incidence. These findings indicate that the ovarian cancer preventive benefits of oral contraceptives, tubal ligation, and IUD use operate through diverse biological mechanisms independent of systemic inflammation. The differential inflammatory profiles among contraceptive methods suggest that long-term OC use may represent a distinct inflammatory phenotype that warrants consideration when evaluating contraceptive risks and benefits in endometriosis-related populations.

title: Predicting Survival Outcomes for Patients with Ovarian Cancer Using National Cancer Registry Data from Taiwan: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: This retrospective cohort study using Taiwanese National Cancer Registry data developed predictive models for ovarian cancer survival outcomes by identifying key clinical and pathological prognostic factors. Advanced tumor stage (TNM staging), high tumor grade, clear cell and mucinous histologies, distant metastasis, elevated lymph node ratio, elevated CA125 levels, and residual tumor after surgery were all independently associated with significantly increased mortality risk. Notably, chemotherapy demonstrated protective effects in patients with poorly differentiated tumors, advanced disease with lymph node involvement, and high-grade serous cancers, offsetting its apparent association with higher mortality in the overall cohort. The developed prediction models (M1 and M2) achieved excellent discrimination ability (c-index 0.8–0.83) for survival prediction and were validated across different racial and ethnic populations. Serous subtype tumors showed particularly high heterogeneity in prognostic factors, with undifferentiated serous histology, elevated CA125 >100 µg/mL, and residual disease conferring 27-fold, 14-fold, and 83-fold increased mortality risks, respectively. These findings underscore the importance of complete surgical cytoreduction and histology-specific treatment strategies in optimizing ovarian cancer survival outcomes.

title: Extracellular urinary microRNAs as non-invasive biomarkers of endometrial and ovarian cancer
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This review examines urinary extracellular microRNAs (miRNAs) as non-invasive biomarkers for detecting ovarian and endometrial cancers. Small non-coding miRNAs show promise as liquid biopsy tools because urine provides an accessible and feasible source for cancer biomarker detection. Specific miRNA expression signatures have been identified that are distinctly associated with both ovarian and endometrial cancer, enabling their potential use for diagnostic purposes. The study highlights that while endometrial cancer is the most prevalent gynecological malignancy, ovarian cancer carries the highest mortality rate, making non-invasive biomarker discovery particularly important for early detection. By leveraging the growing field of liquid biopsy, urinary miRNA analysis offers a practical alternative to traditional invasive diagnostic methods for identifying these gynecological cancers. This approach represents a significant advance in developing clinically applicable screening and diagnostic tools for at-risk populations.

title: The emerging roles and potential applications of circular RNAs in ovarian cancer: a comprehensive review
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Circular RNAs (circRNAs) emerge as critical regulators in ovarian cancer through their multifaceted involvement in fundamental cellular processes including gene expression, cell growth and death, and signal transduction. In ovarian cancer specifically, circRNAs orchestrate several hallmark malignant features by controlling cell growth, invasiveness, metastasis, angiogenesis, and chemoresistance, thereby contributing directly to cancer development and progression. Beyond their mechanistic roles, circRNAs hold significant clinical promise as biomarkers, with evidence supporting their utility in diagnosis, prognosis prediction, and identification of therapeutic targets in ovarian cancer patients. The comprehensive regulatory functions of circRNAs across these diverse pathways suggest they represent key nodes in ovarian cancer biology that warrant further investigation as potential intervention points. These findings position circRNAs as valuable candidates for developing improved diagnostic and prognostic tools in ovarian cancer management, potentially advancing personalized treatment strategies.

title: Pseudo-Meigs’ syndrome in mucinous ovarian carcinoma: A case report
summary: This case report describes pseudo-Meigs' syndrome presenting with mucinous ovarian carcinoma, a novel clinical association not previously documented in the literature. Pseudo-Meigs' syndrome manifests as concurrent pleural effusion and ascites, conditions that can result from pleural invasion, transdiaphragmatic migration of malignant cells, or impaired pleural fluid resorption in the setting of ovarian cancer. The patient presented with elevated tumor markers (CA-125, CEA, and CA 19-9), and pleural fluid analysis revealed inflammatory cells including histiocytes and eosinophils. Surgical resection of the primary mucinous ovarian tumor, which originated from the right ovary and was associated with a borderline mucinous tumor, resulted in complete resolution of both pleural effusion and ascites within weeks. This case emphasizes the critical importance of obtaining tissue diagnosis and fluid cytology for proper staging of ovarian cancers and guiding treatment decisions, particularly when patients present with advanced radiological findings suggesting metastatic disease.

title: Peritoneal Tuberculosis and the Associated Diagnostic Challenges in Gynecology: A Case Report
summary: Peritoneal tuberculosis, caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, presents with a constellation of clinical features including ascites (73% of cases), abdominal pain, weight loss, fever, and abdominal tenderness, along with less frequent signs such as constipation, diarrhea, hepatomegaly, and splenomegaly. The disease is characterized histologically by caseating granulomas and macroscopically by white-yellowish nodules and peritoneal adhesions, with imaging studies showing uniform peritoneal thickening and lymph node calcifications on CT scan. Diagnostic challenges arise because CA125 levels are elevated not only in peritoneal tuberculosis but also in endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and fibroids, making this biomarker non-specific for differentiating tuberculosis from gynecological pathology. Adenosine deaminase in ascitic fluid provides superior diagnostic accuracy, with sensitivity and specificity as high as 100% and 97%, respectively, making it valuable for distinguishing peritoneal tuberculosis from other conditions. Laboratory findings are non-specific but often include elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, lymphocytosis, and mild anemia, while imaging features must be distinguished from peritoneal carcinomatosis, which presents with nodular implants and irregular thickening. Standard first-line antituberculous therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide is effective for treatment once the diagnosis is established through peritoneal biopsy demonstrating granulomatous peritonitis.

title: The independent and joint associations of hysterectomy and uterine fibroids or endometriosis with ovarian cancer incidence: results from a US-based cohort
summary: This US-based cohort study investigated whether uterine conditions—fibroids, endometriosis, and hysterectomy—are independently associated with ovarian cancer risk. Uterine fibroids showed the strongest association with ovarian cancer incidence (hazard ratio 1.65), while endometriosis displayed a weaker, non-significant association (hazard ratio 1.16). Hysterectomy itself was modestly associated with increased ovarian cancer risk, though there was suggestive evidence that hysterectomy may attenuate the elevated risk among women with fibroids. These findings suggest that uterine fibroids may serve as a more significant biomarker or risk indicator for ovarian cancer than endometriosis in this population. The results support further investigation into the biological mechanisms linking benign uterine conditions to ovarian cancer development.

title: p53-autoantibody may be more sensitive than CA-125 in monitoring microscopic and macroscopic residual disease after primary therapy for epithelial ovarian cancer
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated p53-autoantibodies (p53-aab) as a biomarker for detecting residual epithelial ovarian cancer after primary surgery and chemotherapy. Unlike CA-125, which frequently becomes undetectable after treatment even in patients with early relapse, p53-autoantibodies remained elevated in patients who subsequently experienced early progression, suggesting superior sensitivity for minimal residual disease. Importantly, patients whose p53-aab levels achieved seroconversion to negativity during follow-up demonstrated significantly prolonged progression-free and long-term survival, indicating that p53-aab dynamics may predict therapeutic response. The immune system's amplification of p53-aab signals enables detection of even small tumor cell populations that might escape conventional CA-125 monitoring. While p53-aab positivity was not associated with platinum sensitivity or surgical resection status, the marker shows promise as a more reliable tool than CA-125 for monitoring microscopic and macroscopic residual disease in ovarian cancer patients following standard therapy.

title: Potential Efficacy of Inflammatory Response Markers for the Detection of Ovarian Cancer in Patients with Endometrioma
summary: This study evaluates inflammatory response markers as biomarkers for distinguishing ovarian cancer from benign endometrioma in patients at risk. Multiple inflammatory markers—including C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, D-dimer, neutrophil/lymphocyte ratio (NLR), systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), white blood cell count, fibrinogen, and platelet/lymphocyte ratio—were significantly elevated in clear cell and endometrioid carcinoma cases compared to endometrioma cases (P < 0.01). Among these inflammatory markers, NLR and SII derived from complete blood counts showed particular diagnostic efficiency for detecting stage I ovarian cancer. While conventional tumor markers CA125 and CA19-9 demonstrated higher specificity, combining these tumor markers with inflammatory response markers enhanced diagnostic accuracy in differentiating ovarian cancer from endometrioma. These findings suggest that readily available inflammatory response markers could serve as useful adjunctive tools for ovarian cancer detection in endometriosis patients.

title: Reproductive factors and risk of epithelial ovarian cancer: results from the Asia Cohort Consortium
summary: This Asia Cohort Consortium study examined reproductive and lifestyle factors associated with epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) risk, identifying parity as a key protective factor with a dose-response relationship—women with five or more children showed substantially lower risk (HR=0.44) compared to nulliparous women. Later age at menopause emerged as a consistent risk factor across EOC subtypes, with women reaching menopause at 55+ years demonstrating significantly elevated risk (HR=1.77 overall, and markedly higher for non-serous subtypes at HR=4.65). The study revealed important subtype-specific associations, particularly for non-serous tumors, where later menarche was protective while later menopause was strongly associated with increased risk. Notably, several factors commonly investigated—including oral contraceptive use, breastfeeding, body mass index, smoking, and age at first delivery—showed no significant associations with EOC risk in this large Asian population. The findings underscore parity and menopausal timing as critical reproductive factors influencing ovarian cancer risk, with implications for understanding the disease's heterogeneous subtypes including serous, endometrioid, clear cell, and mucinous carcinomas.

title: A new risk algorithm combining D-dimer and HE4 differentiates borderline tumor from patients with ovarian tumor
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a novel risk algorithm combining D-dimer (DD) and human epididymis protein 4 (HE4) to differentiate borderline ovarian tumors (BOTs) from benign ovarian lesions and other ovarian pathologies. Both DD and HE4 were significantly elevated in BOT patients compared to controls, with DD showing an individual diagnostic area under the curve (AUC) of 0.818; the combined DD-HE4 model achieved superior performance with an AUC of 0.852, and demonstrated exceptional utility for serous BOTs with an AUC of 0.941. The mechanistic basis involves tumor cell activation of the clotting-fibrinolytic system, which promotes neoangiogenesis through vascular endothelial cell proliferation, thereby facilitating tumor growth and progression. D-dimer, a fibrinogen breakdown product reflecting abnormal coagulation, showed direct positive correlation with CA125 and HE4 levels, highlighting the interconnection between hemostatic markers and traditional tumor biomarkers. Notably, the DD-HE4 algorithm remained effective for detecting small-diameter BOTs (AUC 0.772), addressing a significant diagnostic challenge in this population of reproductive-age women at risk for malignant transformation and fertility compromise. This biomarker combination offers improved clinical discrimination for BOT diagnosis and may help guide treatment decisions in this intermediate-risk tumor category.

title: Unlocking the future: mitochondrial genes and neural networks in predicting ovarian cancer prognosis and immunotherapy response
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that mitochondrial genes represent powerful biomarkers for predicting ovarian cancer prognosis and immunotherapy response when integrated with neural network algorithms. Mitochondrial genes regulate tumor metabolism, immune cell infiltration, and treatment outcomes, with the MYC pathway—elevated in ovarian cancer and particularly in clear cell carcinomas—playing a central role in promoting mitochondrial oxidative consumption and platinum resistance. Multiple complementary mechanisms beyond mitochondrial genes, including ferroptosis-related pathways, autophagy-related genes, and tumor mutational burden, also associate with patient survival and immunotherapy efficacy. The study reveals that inhibiting mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, nucleotide metabolism, and the tricarboxylic acid cycle represents a promising therapeutic strategy. Risk factors for ovarian cancer include endometriosis, BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, and other genetic factors, establishing a connection between endometriosis and cancer development. By combining mitochondrial gene signatures with neural network prediction models, clinicians can better anticipate which patients will respond to emerging therapies including immune checkpoint inhibitors and poly-ADP-ribose polymerase inhibitors.

title: Assessment of tissue factor pathway inhibitor 2 (TFPI2) as a novel serum marker for malignant tumors of the ovary before and after treatment: A case‐control study
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study establishes tissue factor pathway inhibitor 2 (TFPI2) as a novel serum biomarker for discriminating epithelial ovarian cancer, particularly clear cell carcinoma (CCC), from benign ovarian lesions and borderline tumors. TFPI2, a serine protease inhibitor involved in blood coagulation, is markedly elevated in CCC (>270 pg/mL cutoff) with a preoperative positivity rate of 72.2%, whereas it is substantially lower in non-clear cell epithelial ovarian cancers and unaffected by menstrual cycles or benometriotic cysts—limitations that compromise the clinical utility of the traditional marker CA125. Notably, CCC exhibits strong association with endometriosis and demonstrates chemoresistance to standard paclitaxel, carboplatin, and poly(ADP)-ribose polymerase inhibitor regimens, while also showing low CA125 levels and reduced BRCA mutation prevalence. After primary debulking surgery, TFPI2 levels declined to mean values of 150.2 pg/mL in patients without recurrence but surged to 492.6 pg/mL in those with recurrent disease, indicating its utility for monitoring therapeutic response and predicting recurrence. These findings position TFPI2 as a superior, endometriosis-associated CCC-specific biomarker with potential prognostic significance in ovarian cancer management.

title: Artificial intelligence algorithm for preoperative prediction of FIGO stage in ovarian cancer based on clinical features integrated 18F-FDG PET/CT metabolic and radiomics features
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Artificial Intelligence for Preoperative FIGO Staging in Ovarian Cancer

This study developed machine learning algorithms integrating clinical features with 18F-FDG PET/CT metabolic and radiomics features to predict FIGO stage in ovarian cancer patients. The researchers identified significant biomarker differences between early and advanced disease, including elevated CA125, HE4, systemic immune-inflammation index, and platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio in advanced stages, alongside reduced albumin and other nutritional markers. An adaptive ensemble model achieved the best predictive performance with an AUC of 0.819, demonstrating that combined metabolic, radiomics, and clinical data substantially improves staging accuracy compared to single-modality approaches. The study noted important limitations, as certain histological subtypes including clear cell and mucinous carcinomas exhibit lower FDG uptake and may be underestimated by PET/CT imaging. These findings establish a comprehensive AI-driven framework for preoperative stage prediction that could guide treatment planning and prognostication in ovarian cancer patients, though histological subtype-specific considerations warrant further investigation.

title: Polybasic nanogels for intracellular co-delivery of paclitaxel and carboplatin: a novel approach to ovarian cancer therapy†
summary: This study presents a novel polybasic nanogel delivery system designed to overcome limitations of traditional ovarian cancer chemotherapy. The nanogel, composed of poly(diethylaminoethyl methacrylate-co-cyclohexyl methacrylate) grafted with polyethylene glycol, simultaneously delivers paclitaxel and carboplatin—two chemotherapeutic agents with a strong clinical history in ovarian cancer treatment—while reducing associated toxicities. The system employs environmentally responsive crosslinkers (disulfide bonds degradable by intracellular glutathione and carboxylesterase-sensitive linkages) that enable controlled drug release specifically within cancer cells, triggering apoptosis more effectively than free drugs in OVCAR-3 ovarian cancer cell models. By concentrating both chemotherapeutic agents in a single particle internalized via endocytosis, this approach addresses treatment resistance that emerges during extended intervals between conventional chemotherapy cycles. The nanogel formulation significantly reduces vehicle toxicities associated with paclitaxel's poor water solubility, which currently requires toxic Cremophor EL in FDA-approved formulations. Overall, this dual-agent nanoparticle strategy represents a promising advancement toward more effective and less toxic ovarian cancer therapy.

title: Decision tree model for predicting ovarian tumor malignancy based on clinical markers and preoperative circulating blood cells
journal: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
summary: This study developed a machine learning decision tree model to improve discrimination between benign and malignant ovarian tumors by integrating serum biomarkers, imaging findings, and preoperative blood cell parameters. The model identified postoperative ROMA index (combining CA125 and HE4) as the most critical root node predictor, with tumor mass size and HE4 serving as secondary classifiers. Malignant tumors demonstrated characteristic hematologic alterations including elevated white blood cells, platelets, neutrophils, and monocytes, alongside reduced red blood cells, hemoglobin, lymphocytes, and related indices compared to benign lesions. Patient demographics were also significant, with malignant cases associated with older age, post-menopausal status, and ascites development. The decision tree model achieved an AUC of 0.86, combining traditional imaging markers with readily available blood routine indicators and tumor markers to enhance diagnostic accuracy in clinical settings. This approach offers a practical, economical tool for improving early detection of ovarian cancer, particularly in primary care hospitals where advanced diagnostic resources may be limited.

title: Infertility and risk of ovarian cancer: A systematic review and meta analysis
journal: Journal of Family Medicine and Primary Care
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the relationship between infertility and ovarian cancer risk, identifying infertility itself as associated with a modest increased ovarian cancer risk (OR: 1.35). Notably, endometriosis emerged as a critical risk factor, conferring a three- to eight-fold increased risk of ovarian tumors in affected women, particularly those also experiencing infertility. While individual infertility drugs such as clomiphene citrate showed minimal cancer risk, assisted reproductive technology (ART) treatment and ovarian stimulation for in vitro fertilization demonstrated significant associations with ovarian cancer, with increased treatment cycles (RR: 2.63) and multiple ART cycles substantially elevating risk. Additional risk factors included nulligravid or nulliparous status, advanced maternal age, family history of ovarian cancer, and BRCA mutations, whereas oral contraceptive use provided protective effects with a 0.66-fold risk reduction. The findings suggest that infertility-related cancer risk stems primarily from underlying reproductive conditions and ovarian stimulation protocols rather than specific medication exposures, with endometriosis being a particularly important driver of ovarian malignancy in infertile populations.

title: A Case of Atypical Meig’s Syndrome Presenting With Pleural and Pericardial Effusion
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an atypical presentation of Meigs syndrome, a rare condition classically characterized by the triad of benign ovarian tumors, ascites, and pleural effusion, in which a 75-year-old patient presented with concurrent pleural and pericardial effusions—an exceptionally uncommon manifestation. The underlying mechanism involves vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) secretion by the ovarian fibroma, which promotes angiogenesis, increases vascular permeability, and drives peritoneal and pleural fluid accumulation, while inflammatory mediators and lymphatic obstruction further contribute to effusion formation. Surgical resection of the ovarian fibroma resulted in complete resolution of both effusions and clinical symptoms including shortness of breath and abdominal fullness, confirming the benign and self-limiting nature of the condition. The case highlights the diagnostic challenge posed by elevated CA-125 levels, which can occur in Meigs syndrome alongside ovarian cancer and endometriosis, necessitating comprehensive clinical assessment beyond biomarker evaluation. This atypical variant lacking the typical ascites component underscores the importance of recognizing varied presentations of Meigs syndrome to enable timely surgical intervention and favorable prognosis.

title: Integrative Analysis of Gene Expression and Promoter Methylation to Differentiate High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer from Benign Tumors
summary: This integrative study identifies molecular biomarkers to distinguish high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) from benign ovarian tumors through combined analysis of gene expression and promoter methylation patterns. The research focuses on dysregulation of the Notch and Wnt signaling pathways, which normally regulate stemness, differentiation, and proliferation in ovarian tissue. Key findings include that CTNNB1 (a Wnt pathway component) serves as the best single biomarker for HGSOC detection with an AUC of 0.97, while a seven-gene Notch pathway signature (NOTCH1-4, DLL1, JAG2, HES1) effectively stratifies HGSOC from benign cases. Additionally, HOX-related genes show elevated promoter methylation in HGSOC samples, particularly HOPX, with approximately 30-49% methylation frequencies across ALX4, CDX2, and ARID1A. The study links ARID1A loss and HOX gene hypermethylation to chemotherapy resistance, suggesting that DNMT inhibition may resensitize HOX-deficient tumors to carboplatin therapy. These integrated molecular signatures offer potential clinical applications for improved HGSOC diagnosis and therapeutic targeting in patients with endometriosis-associated cancers.

title: A narrative review of epigenetic marker in H3K27ac and its emerging potential as a therapeutic target in cancer
summary: H3K27ac, a critical histone acetylation mark, functions as a major regulator of chromatin structure and gene expression with significant implications for cancer development. This epigenetic modification plays a prominent role in tumorigenesis and carcinogenesis across multiple cancer types, including breast, colon, lung, hepatocellular, and prostate cancers. H3K27ac mediates gene transcription through coordinated action with pioneer factors and enhancer-promoter loops, mechanisms that become dysregulated during malignant transformation. The review identifies therapeutic opportunities targeting H3K27ac through inhibition of histone acetyltransferases (HAT inhibitors) and histone deacetylases (HDAC inhibitors) as potential cancer treatment strategies. While H3K27ac serves as a promising biomarker for cancer identification and prognosis, the comprehensive epigenetic mechanisms underlying its role in tumorigenesis remain incompletely understood and warrant further investigation.

title: Early Diagnosis of Ovarian Cancer: A Comprehensive Review of the Advances, Challenges, and Future Directions
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines the molecular and clinical landscape of ovarian cancer early diagnosis, with particular emphasis on endometriosis-associated malignancies. Endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas, which arise frequently from endometriosis, harbor distinct mutational profiles including KRAS, PIK3CA, PTEN, ARID1A, and CTNNB1 alterations, distinguishing them from high-grade serous carcinomas driven by BRCA1/BRCA2 and TP53 mutations. Imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound, MRI, and FDG-PET/CT combined with serum biomarkers such as CA-125 and HE4 form the diagnostic foundation, with emerging biomarkers including circulating tumor DNA, microRNAs, and DNA methylation showing promise for enhanced detection. Treatment approaches are mutation-specific: BRCA-mutant tumors respond to PARP inhibitors, low-grade serous carcinomas benefit from MEK/BRAF inhibitors alongside platinum-based chemotherapy, and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors target clear cell carcinomas. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy remains the most effective prevention strategy for BRCA carriers, while oral contraceptive use reduces ovarian cancer risk in the general population.

title: Giant Ovarian Tumors in Young Women: Diagnostic and Treatment Challenges—A Report of Two Cases and Narrative Review of the Recent Literature
summary: Giant ovarian tumors in young women present significant diagnostic and clinical challenges, manifesting through nonspecific symptoms including progressive abdominal enlargement, distension, pain, constipation, early satiety, and gastrointestinal distress. The article presents two cases—a 17-year-old with ovarian cystadenofibroma and a 25-year-old with intestinal-endocervical mucinous borderline tumor—both successfully managed through laparotomy and complete tumor resection. Serum biomarkers including CA-125, CA 19.9, HE4, and lactate dehydrogenase were elevated in affected patients, with CA-125 and the Risk of Malignancy Index serving as important diagnostic tools alongside imaging modalities such as ultrasound and CT scanning. The most common histopathological findings in giant ovarian tumors are mature cystic teratomas, followed by mucinous and serous cystadenomas, with rare cases of borderline tumors and granulosa cell tumors also documented. Early detection through regular gynecological screening and ultrasonography has substantially reduced the incidence of large ovarian cysts, while genetic predisposition (BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations) and modifiable risk factors including nulliparity, hormone replacement therapy, smoking, and obesity contribute to ovarian cancer development. Surgical intervention remains the gold standard treatment, achieving favorable postoperative outcomes when complete excision is accomplished in young women presenting with these large pelvic masses.

title: Effects of Clinical Covariates on Serum miRNA Expression among Women without Ovarian Cancer
summary: Serum microRNAs (miRNAs) represent promising biomarkers for ovarian cancer detection, functioning through posttranscriptional regulation of gene expression and showing associations with cancer diagnosis, prognosis, and progression. Multiple clinical covariates significantly influence circulating miRNA levels, including advancing age, menopausal status, obesity, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and bilateral oophorectomy, with endometriosis showing an association of moderate strength. Specific miRNA signatures vary by clinical phenotype: age and postmenopause correlate with reduced miR-205-5p and miR-574-3p levels, while BRCA1/2 mutations are associated with lower miR-1237-3p, and ovarian status influences a five-miRNA panel including miR-17-5p, miR-18b-5p, and miR-93-5p. These estrogen-responsive miRNAs are predicted to target estrogen receptors ER1 and ER2, which influence ovarian carcinogenesis through opposite effects on cancer cell proliferation and apoptosis. The ER1/ER2 ratio correlates with ovarian malignant progression, suggesting a mechanistic link between miRNA-mediated regulation of estrogen signaling and ovarian cancer development. Clinical covariates must be considered when interpreting miRNA-based biomarkers for ovarian cancer risk assessment.

title: Exploring the characteristics of immortalized human ovarian surface epithelial cell lines
summary:
# Summary

This study characterized immortalized human ovarian surface epithelial (IHOSE) cell lines as models for understanding ovarian cancer biology and biomarker identification. The IHOSE cell lines were generated by infecting primary ovarian surface epithelial cells with either SV40T or HPV E6/E7 oncogenes, which inactivate p53 and RB to enable immortalization, with SV40T demonstrating more efficient growth inhibition and higher proliferation rates. Through immunological profiling, the study established a distinct biomarker signature distinguishing IHOSE cells from malignant ovarian cancer lines: IHOSE cells expressed high cytokeratin 7 and cytokeratin 18 but lacked E-cadherin, EpCAM, and CA125, while ovarian cancer cell lines showed elevated PAX8 and CA125 expression. Notably, two specific IHOSE variants (IHOSE4138_SV40T and IHOSE0160_SV40T) exhibited intermediate characteristics, with IHOSE4138_SV40T showing elevated PAX8 and calretinin expression and closer clustering to malignant cell lines, suggesting potential for studying early malignant transformation. These cell lines represent valuable tools for investigating ovarian cancer development, particularly given that epithelial ovarian cancers may originate from either ovarian surface epithelium or fallopian tube epithelium and are influenced by reproductive factors including repeated ovulation and menstrual history.

title: Diagnostic value of magnetic resonance imaging for malignant ovarian tumors mis-subclassified by the ultrasound-based ADNEX model
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer remains the fifth leading cause of cancer-related deaths in women, with dramatically different prognoses depending on stage: stage I tumors have >90% five-year survival rates, while advanced-stage disease drops below 30%. The ultrasound-based ADNEX model is the primary tool for distinguishing benign from malignant ovarian masses and differentiating primary from metastatic tumors, but this study reveals it has significant limitations in subtyping certain malignancies, particularly clear cell carcinomas (accuracy 0.17) compared to high-grade serous carcinomas (accuracy 0.94). Contrast-enhanced MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging provides complementary diagnostic value through specific radiological signatures: malignant tumors display hyperintense DWI signal intensity and low apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) values, while clear cell carcinomas show characteristically higher ADC values and hemorrhage signals. MRI achieved 90.2% overall diagnostic accuracy and successfully reclassified 83.33% of masses misclassified by ultrasound as benign or borderline, particularly by detecting solid components and evaluating morphological features like nodule height-to-width ratios. The study demonstrates that MRI is an essential second-line imaging modality when ADNEX model assessment is indeterminate, especially for identifying type I epithelial ovarian cancers (including clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas) that are strongly associated with endometriosis and commonly present with solid-cystic morphology.

title: The Role of CA‐125 in the Management of Ovarian Cancer: A Systematic Review
summary: CA-125 (carbohydrate antigen 125) serves as a multifunctional biomarker throughout the management pathway of ovarian cancer, functioning in screening, diagnosis, treatment monitoring, and prognostication. The marker demonstrates particular utility in detecting epithelial ovarian cancers, which comprise approximately 90% of all ovarian cancer cases, and shows strongest elevation in serous histology and advanced-stage disease (III-IV), with further elevation correlating with higher tumor grade and ascites presence. While CA-125 alone detects over half of early-stage cases, its diagnostic accuracy substantially improves when combined with other markers such as HE4 (human epididymis protein 4) and ROMA scoring, and with symptom indices achieving 89.3% detection rates. Post-treatment CA-125 kinetics powerfully predict clinical outcomes: a post-surgical reduction below 97.6% after the fourth chemotherapy cycle predicts relapse within 12 months, while normal pre-treatment levels and negative markers after the third cycle associate with improved overall and progression-free survival. Preoperative CA-125 thresholds also predict surgical feasibility, with levels below 500 U/mL showing 82% positive predictive value for optimal cytoreduction, and levels exceeding 535 indicating lymph node metastasis. Integration of CA-125 with additional tumor markers and tissue kallikreins is recommended to enhance prognostic accuracy in epithelial ovarian cancer management.

title: Ovarian Cancer in Women with Intellectual Disability: Current Data
summary: Women with intellectual disability experience ovarian cancer at significantly earlier ages (55 versus 63 years) and with distinct epidemiological patterns compared to the general population. The article identifies both genetic risk factors—including BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations and Lynch syndrome, which account for over 20% of ovarian cancers—and modifiable lifestyle factors such as obesity, overweight status, and endometriosis that increase cancer risk. Importantly, women with intellectual disability have higher prevalence of obesity and overweight, compounding their cancer risk, while also being less likely to benefit from protective factors like multiparity and contraceptive pill use. A critical distinction emerges in tumor histology: germ cell tumors comprise 77% of ovarian cancers in individuals with intellectual disability compared to only 0.5–5% in the general population, with dysgerminoma particularly associated with Down syndrome and ataxia telangiectasia. Diagnostic delays are common in this population due to intellectual impairment and communication barriers, resulting in late-stage detection that limits curative treatment options. Enhanced clinical surveillance and adapted screening strategies are essential to improve outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: Few-Layer Graphene-Based Optical Nanobiosensors for the Early-Stage Detection of Ovarian Cancer Using Liquid Biopsy and an Active Learning Strategy
summary:
# Summary

This study develops and validates graphene-based optical nanobiosensors (G-NBSs) integrated with active learning algorithms for early-stage ovarian cancer detection using liquid biopsy analysis of serum protease signatures. The research focuses on quantifying activities of multiple proteases—including matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), cathepsins, and ADAM proteins—which are dysregulated in cancer and promote tumor progression, invasion, and metastasis through extracellular matrix degradation and proteolytic network activation. A distinctive protease panel revealed that ADAM15 activity is elevated in metastatic ovarian cancer compared to localized disease, while MMP3, MMP24, MMP28, CTSK, and ADAM10/12 show higher activity in healthy controls, enabling differentiation between disease states. The graphene-based nanobiosensor platform exploits fluorescence quenching properties of few-layer graphene to detect and quantify these cancer-associated protease biomarkers in serum, where proteolytic activity is highest compared to plasma or whole blood. By combining this biosensing technology with an active learning prediction framework, the study demonstrates statistically significant differentiation of early-stage from advanced ovarian cancer, offering a promising non-invasive liquid biopsy approach that could improve the poor prognosis associated with late-stage diagnosis.

title: The Diagnostic Role of the Platelet-to-Lymphocyte Ratio in Ovarian Cancer: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis of 22 studies demonstrates that the platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), a systemic inflammation marker, is significantly elevated in ovarian cancer patients and serves as a useful diagnostic biomarker for distinguishing benign from malignant ovarian lesions and early-stage from advanced disease. The elevated PLR in ovarian cancer arises from tumor-induced thrombocytosis and lymphopenia, with platelet-lymphocyte interactions facilitating neutrophil extracellular trap formation and metastatic progression through immune evasion mechanisms. The article emphasizes that endometriosis, particularly ovarian endometriomas and deep infiltrating endometriosis, markedly increases ovarian cancer risk (9.7-fold) through chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and activation of the PI3K/AKT pathway, with PIK3CA mutations frequently found in endometriosis-associated clear-cell and endometrioid carcinomas. Multiple genetic mutations including BRCA1/BRCA2, TP53, KRAS, and ARID1A are linked to distinct ovarian cancer subtypes, with BRCA mutations prevalent in recurrent high-grade serous carcinoma and enabling PARP inhibitor treatment. Beyond PLR, emerging biomarkers including CA125, HE4, and circulating tumor DNA offer complementary diagnostic potential for improving ovarian cancer detection and stratification.

title: Serine/threonine kinase 11 (STK11) associated adnexal tumors: from biology to therapeutic impact
summary:
# Summary

STK11 is a tumor suppressor gene located on chromosome 19p13.3 that regulates cellular energy metabolism, polarity, and growth through phosphorylation of multiple proteins and modulation of the AMPK-mTOR and Wnt-C-Myc pathways. Mutations or inactivation of STK11 cause Peutz-Jeghers syndrome and predispose patients to multiple malignancies, particularly adnexal tumors including sex cord tumors with annular tubules, minimal deviation adenocarcinoma, and granulosa cell tumors. STK11 loss is associated with epithelial-mesenchymal transformation, G2/M cell cycle arrest, and increased metastatic potential; concurrent deletion of STK11 and PTEN results in high-grade papillary serous carcinoma with elevated mTOR signaling. Clinical management of STK11-associated adnexal tumors incorporates fertility-sparing surgery for young women, chemotherapy with platinum and paclitaxel, immunotherapy, and prophylactic adnexectomy, complemented by enhanced screening protocols using ultrasonography, CA-125, HE4, and ROMA algorithm. Genetic testing serves as a preventive measure for high-risk populations with STK11 mutations, identifying affected individuals before tumor development.

title: Evaluation of a novel ensemble model for preoperative ovarian cancer diagnosis: Clinical factors, O-RADS, and deep learning radiomics
summary: This study developed and validated a novel ensemble machine learning model that integrates clinical variables, O-RADS ultrasound scoring, and deep learning radiomics features to improve preoperative ovarian cancer diagnosis. The ensemble model achieved exceptional diagnostic performance with an AUC of 0.97, sensitivity of 0.96, and specificity of 0.91 across internal and external validation sets, substantially outperforming standalone clinical (lower AUC) and O-RADS models. Deep learning radiomics predictions were the most influential component, followed by O-RADS scores and CA-125 levels, with radiomics features extracted via ResNet50 and LASSO regression showing particular diagnostic value. The model significantly enhanced individual sonographers' diagnostic capabilities and inter-sonographer consistency (kappa values increased from 0.65-0.68 to 0.76+), with marked improvements in AUC for all three sonographers tested. The ensemble approach demonstrated robust performance even in challenging clinical scenarios, achieving an AUC of 0.97 in tumors <10 cm and maintaining accuracy in cases with normal CA-125 levels. These findings suggest that integrating artificial intelligence-derived radiomics with traditional clinical assessment provides a clinically practical tool for improving ovarian cancer detection accuracy and standardizing diagnostic precision across practitioners.

title: Exosomes in Ovarian Cancer: Towards Precision Oncology
summary: Exosomes derived from ovarian cancer cells function as critical mediators of tumor progression through multiple mechanisms, including regulation of angiogenesis, cell proliferation, and migration. These extracellular vesicles promote metastasis, drug resistance, and peritoneal dissemination by transferring bioactive molecules that facilitate intercellular communication within the tumor microenvironment and establish pre-metastatic niches. Ovarian cancer-derived exosomes carry distinctive protein and lipid cargo, including tumor-associated antigens (Mart1, HER2/neu), membrane proteins (CD24, EpCAM, HSP70), and carcinogenic proteins (ATF2, MTA1, ROCK1/2, CD147, EPHA2) that actively promote migration and angiogenesis. Additionally, exosomes regulate immune evasion by modulating NF-κB and STAT3 signaling pathways while suppressing natural killer cell activity, and elevated levels of LRP1 in patient exosomes control MMP2 and MMP9 expression to enhance cell migration. Current FDA-approved serum biomarkers CA125 and HE4 have limitations due to elevation in benign conditions like endometriosis and menstruation, suggesting that exosome profiling and their protein cargo represent promising precision oncology approaches for improved ovarian cancer detection and stratification.

title: Evaluating CA-125 and PET/CT for cancer detection in idiopathic inflammatory myopathies
summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of CA-125 and PET/CT imaging for cancer detection in patients with idiopathic inflammatory myopathies. CA-125, a commonly used serum biomarker, showed significant limitations due to high false-positive rates, particularly in patients with endometriosis and uterine fibroids, conditions that elevate CA-125 levels independent of malignancy. Similarly, PET/CT imaging demonstrated reduced specificity, with lymphadenopathy and lung nodules being the predominant sources of false-positive findings. These results highlight that neither CA-125 nor PET/CT alone provides sufficient diagnostic accuracy for reliable cancer detection in this patient population, and clinicians must interpret both biomarkers and imaging results cautiously when benign gynecological conditions like endometriosis are present. The study underscores the need for additional diagnostic strategies and integrated clinical assessment to improve cancer detection specificity in patients with inflammatory myopathies who may present with elevated CA-125 levels from non-malignant causes.

title: Diagnostic performances of the Ovarian Adnexal Reporting and Data System, the Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm, and the Copenhagen Index in the preoperative prediction of ovarian cancer: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study evaluated three diagnostic algorithms for preoperative ovarian cancer prediction: the Ovarian Adnexal Reporting and Data System (O-RADS), the Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm (ROMA), and the Copenhagen Index (CPH-I). O-RADS demonstrated superior diagnostic performance with an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.949 and 88.52% sensitivity/88.98% specificity at optimal cutoff, outperforming both ROMA (AUC 0.880) and CPH-I. The combination of O-RADS with the serum biomarker CA125 achieved the highest predictive accuracy (AUC 0.969), suggesting that integrating imaging with biomarker data enhances cancer detection. Serum levels of CA125 and HE4 were significantly elevated in ovarian cancer cases compared to benign tumors, and progressive O-RADS classifications (3, 4, and 5) showed increasing malignancy rates of 7.7%, 39.7%, and 96.4% respectively. These findings support O-RADS combined with CA125 as an effective preoperative screening strategy for distinguishing ovarian cancer from benign adnexal pathology.

title: The predictive prognostic value of serum CEA, CA153, HE4 combined with a variety of immune inflammatory indicators in breast cancer
summary: This study evaluated the combined predictive value of serum tumor markers CEA, CA153, and HE4 alongside immune-inflammatory indicators (neutrophil count, platelet count, and systemic immune-inflammation index) for breast cancer prognosis. All three tumor markers and inflammatory parameters were significantly elevated in patients with poor prognosis compared to those with good prognosis, establishing them as effective predictors of adverse outcomes. CEA and CA153 are specifically associated with systemic metastatic disease and postoperative recurrence, while HE4 demonstrates particular utility in predicting progression-free survival and increased abnormal nuclear divisions in cancer cells. Neutrophils and platelets contribute to poor prognosis through distinct mechanisms: neutrophils promote tumor proliferation and metastasis via release of inflammatory mediators including neutrophil elastase and interleukin-8, whereas platelets facilitate tumor angiogenesis and metastasis while shielding tumor cells from immune surveillance. The integrated assessment of these serum markers combined with immune-inflammatory indicators provides robust prognostic stratification for identifying breast cancer patients at high risk of recurrence and metastasis, which represent major contributors to patient mortality.

title: Role of HE4 in evaluation of adnexal masses and its comparison with CA125, ROMA and RMI in premenopausal women
summary:
# HE4 as a Biomarker for Ovarian Cancer in Premenopausal Women

This study evaluated Human Epididymis Protein 4 (HE4) as a biomarker for ovarian cancer diagnosis in premenopausal women, comparing its diagnostic performance with CA125, the ROMA (Risk of Malignancy Algorithm) score, and the RMI index. While CA125 demonstrated higher overall sensitivity (86.6%), HE4 proved more specific for epithelial ovarian cancer and notably absent in benign conditions including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and dermoid cysts—conditions where CA125 is frequently falsely elevated. The combined use of HE4 and CA125 achieved superior sensitivity (73%) compared to either marker alone, and ROMA emerged as the most reliable diagnostic algorithm for identifying ovarian malignancy in this population. Critically, HE4 successfully identified malignancies missed by CA125 in 56.6% of cases, including certain epithelial subtypes and borderline tumors, highlighting its complementary diagnostic value. These findings support incorporating HE4 into a diagnostic algorithm alongside CA125 to improve both sensitivity and specificity in distinguishing malignant adnexal masses from benign conditions in premenopausal women.

title: Development and validation of a prognostic nomogram for ovarian clear cell carcinoma: a study based on the SEER database and a Chinese cohort
summary: Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) is a highly malignant tumor significantly associated with ovarian endometriosis that presents at a younger age and earlier stage than other epithelial ovarian carcinomas, yet exhibits poor prognosis due to high chemotherapy resistance and recurrence rates once advanced. Multiple independent prognostic factors for overall and cancer-specific survival were identified, including age, marital status, FIGO stage, disease site, tumor grade, CA125 elevation, and lymph node metastasis status. Surgical resection combined with chemotherapy represents the primary treatment approach, with radiotherapy showing potential additional benefit for improving survival outcomes in select cases. A prognostic nomogram was developed and validated using both SEER database and Chinese cohort data to quantify individual risk and predict one-, three-, and five-year survival probabilities, providing clinicians with a visual prognostic tool for improved patient stratification. Notably, unmarried women demonstrated higher ovarian cancer risk compared to married women, suggesting sociodemographic factors may influence disease outcomes beyond traditional clinicopathological variables.

title: Explainable AI-based feature importance analysis for ovarian cancer classification with ensemble methods
summary:
# Summary

This study presents an explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) approach using ensemble machine learning methods and interpretability tools like LIME and SHAP Kernels for improved ovarian cancer detection, diagnosis, and prognosis. A three-stage ensemble classifier achieved 98.66% accuracy and 0.96 AUC-ROC for ovarian cancer prediction by analyzing complex patterns within multi-modal clinical datasets, addressing the challenges of delayed detection and high recurrence rates in the disease. The research demonstrates that ensemble methods—particularly Random Forest, XGBoost, and SVM—consistently outperform traditional approaches, with Random Forest reaching 98.13% accuracy in classification. Deep learning models, including fuzzy CNN-based classifiers and U-Net architectures, further enhanced diagnostic accuracy, with some achieving near-perfect performance (98-100%) for specific tasks such as bevacizumab efficacy prediction and cancer segmentation. Game-theoretic frameworks using SHAP values provided critical insights into the decision-making processes of these models, enabling clinicians to understand which biomarkers—such as CA125, HE4, and ROMA—most strongly influence predictions. Overall, this integrated AI-based methodology combines computational sophistication with clinical interpretability, positioning explainable ensemble models as powerful tools for improving ovarian cancer management across multiple clinical applications.

title: The association of rs25487 of the XRCC1 gene and rs13181 of the ERCC2 gene polymorphisms with the ovarian cancer risk
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how genetic polymorphisms in DNA repair genes influence ovarian cancer risk. The rs25487 polymorphism in XRCC1 and the rs13181 polymorphism in ERCC2—both critical components of the DNA repair system—were found to be significantly associated with ovarian cancer development. Specifically, carriers of the T allele at rs25487 XRCC1 showed a 1.45-fold increased risk, while the T/T genotype further elevated risk; conversely, the T/G heterozygous genotype at rs13181 ERCC2 was protective. The mechanistic basis involves impaired DNA repair capacity, as the T allele correlates with increased spontaneous and induced chromatid breaks, while the G allele at rs13181 associates with reduced ERCC2 expression and diminished nucleotide excision repair activity. Higher XRCC1 expression itself correlates with advanced cancer stage, platinum resistance, and poor prognosis, suggesting that defective DNA repair contributes to both cancer initiation and treatment resistance. These genetic biomarkers may help stratify ovarian cancer risk and inform treatment decisions, particularly regarding PARP and topoisomerase inhibitor therapies that target DNA repair pathways.

title: MicroRNA profiling reveals potential biomarkers for the early transformation of endometriosis towards endometriosis-correlated ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNA profiling has been identified as a promising approach for early detection of malignant transformation in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (ECOC). A panel of four microRNAs—hsa-miR-10a-5p, hsa-miR-141–3p, hsa-miR-183–5p, and hsa-miR-200a-3p—were consistently upregulated in both benign ovarian endometriosis and ECOC tissues, with hsa-miR-183–5p achieving perfect discrimination (accuracy of 1.0) between benign and malignant lesions. Nine additional miRNAs, including members of the miR-200 family and the miR-183/182/96 cluster, demonstrated excellent diagnostic capability in distinguishing malignant from benign endometriotic tissues. These upregulated microRNAs regulate critical cancer-promoting mechanisms including epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, tumor growth, invasion, and therapy resistance, positioning them as biomarkers for identifying endometriosis cases at highest risk of progression to ovarian cancer.

title: Feasibility of assessing symptoms associated with ovarian cancer using an electronic medical intake questionnaire
summary: This study evaluated the feasibility of using an electronic symptom inventory questionnaire to assess ovarian cancer risk in clinical practice. The most common symptoms associated with ovarian cancer were abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, and urinary complaints, with urinary urgency or frequency being the most frequently reported symptom. Women reporting any ovarian cancer symptoms were significantly more likely to have medical comorbidities including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, depression, irritable bowel syndrome, GERD, and chronic pelvic pain, which can complicate clinical assessment. Among symptomatic patients, 28% underwent imaging studies and only a small proportion received biomarker testing (CA125 or HE4), with all measured CA125 levels remaining within normal limits. The study identified adenomyosis, ovarian cysts, and uterine fibroids as the most common gynecologic abnormalities detected in patients with positive symptom screening. These findings highlight the challenge of distinguishing ovarian cancer symptoms from benign gynecologic and gastrointestinal conditions, suggesting that structured symptom assessment may help stratify patients but must be combined with appropriate biomarker and imaging evaluation for effective cancer detection.

title: Modern Emerging Biosensing Methodologies for the Early Diagnosis and Screening of Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This review synthesizes emerging biosensing methodologies for ovarian cancer detection, emphasizing multiple biomarkers with distinct advantages over traditional approaches. Classical markers like CA-125 are widely elevated in advanced-stage epithelial ovarian cancer but suffer from limited specificity, as they are also elevated in endometriosis and other benign conditions. HE4 emerges as a superior discriminator, being significantly elevated in serous and endometrioid epithelial ovarian cancers while notably absent in endometriosis, where it promotes tumor progression through the EGFR-MAPK pathway and affects cancer cell adhesion and proliferation. Complementary biomarkers including prostasin, mesothelin, osteopontin, and kallikreins demonstrate elevated expression in ovarian cancer cells and ascitic fluid, with kallikrein family members (KLK5, KLK6, KLK10) and HSP family proteins (HSP27, HSP70, HSP90) associated with poor prognosis and peritoneal metastasis. Emerging molecular biomarkers, including microRNAs (miR-199b-5p, miR-27b-5p, miR-665, miR-205) and exosomal proteins (annexin A3, CRABP2), regulate cancer cell behavior through pathways including JAG1-Notch1 signaling and CXCL1-mediated growth, while lipid signaling molecules like lysophosphatidic acid provide additional diagnostic targets. Collectively, these biomarkers offer improved early detection and differentiation of ovarian cancer from benign gynecological conditions through integrated biosensing platforms.

title: The Clinical Relevance of Distinguishing Between Simple and Complex Adnexal Cystic Structures by Ultrasound in Peri- and Postmenopause
summary:
# Summary

This study establishes ultrasound morphology as a critical diagnostic tool for distinguishing benign from malignant adnexal masses in peri- and postmenopausal women. Complex cyst morphology—characterized by papillary projections, thick irregular walls, septations, and solid components—was strongly associated with malignancy, particularly in postmenopausal patients and when cysts exceeded 5 cm in diameter. In contrast, simple unilocular cysts demonstrated low malignancy risk with spontaneous resolution, especially in postmenopausal women where malignancy occurred in only 0.26% of cases. Elevated CA-125 levels enhanced malignancy detection when combined with transvaginal ultrasonography, though CA-125 elevation also occurs in benign conditions including endometriosis, suggesting the importance of integrated diagnostic approaches. The study emphasizes that cyst morphology and growth behavior are stronger predictors of malignancy than size alone, supporting the clinical utility of standardized risk stratification systems such as the O-RADS US risk score, ADNEX model, and IOTA simple rules to guide management decisions and reduce unnecessary surgical interventions.

title: The Optimal Age for Oophorectomy in Women with Benign Conditions: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the optimal timing of oophorectomy in women with benign conditions, emphasizing the critical balance between cancer prevention and long-term health outcomes. Ovarian hormones play essential roles in maintaining cardiovascular health, metabolic function, and cognitive integrity, making ovarian conservation strongly recommended for average-risk women to reduce the incidence of cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, and cognitive decline. However, prophylactic oophorectomy is advised for high-risk populations, particularly BRCA1/2 and Lynch syndrome carriers, where the procedure significantly reduces ovarian, breast, and fallopian tube cancer risks. Premenopausal oophorectomy, especially before age 50 without hormone replacement therapy, substantially increases mortality risk, with women under 45 experiencing 31% higher mortality by age 80, though estrogen therapy substantially mitigates these adverse effects. For benign conditions such as endometriosis, severe adnexal torsion, and tubo-ovarian abscess, salpingo-oophorectomy provides effective treatment; however, a staged approach with delayed oophorectomy improves quality of life and sexual health outcomes. The evidence underscores that individualized decision-making based on genetic risk, age, and careful consideration of hormone replacement therapy is essential to optimize both cancer prevention and overall survival in women undergoing ovarian surgery.

title: Improved breast cancer diagnosis using a CA15-3 capture antibody-lectin sandwich assay
summary:
# Summary

Oncogenic transformation triggers metabolic reprogramming in cells, which subsequently facilitates multiple hallmarks of cancer progression including immune evasion, apoptosis resistance, hyperproliferation, angiogenesis, invasion, and metastasis. Cell surface glycoconjugates, particularly those undergoing aberrant sialylation, play critical roles in these processes by modulating cell signaling, immune recognition, cell-matrix interactions, and invasive capacity. Many cancers, including breast and ovarian cancer, preferentially accumulate N-glycolylneuraminic acid (Neu5Gc) on their glycoproteins, leading to elevated serum Neu5Gc levels that serve as a biomarker for cancer onset and progression. The researchers developed an improved neuCA15-3 sandwich assay utilizing a lectin (SubB2M) that specifically binds Neu5Gc in combination with CA15-3 capture antibodies, achieving 81% classification accuracy and an AUC of 0.886 for breast cancer detection. This dual-epitope approach—targeting both the CA15-3 protein epitope and the aberrant Neu5Gc glycan epitope—substantially enhances assay specificity and sensitivity compared to conventional CA15-3 measurements alone, offering a more reliable diagnostic tool for breast cancer that is independent of receptor subtype.

title: MiR-575 in Exosomes of Vaginal Discharge Is Downregulated in Ovarian Cancer Patients
summary: Exosomal miR-575 in vaginal discharge is significantly downregulated in ovarian cancer patients compared to those with benign gynecological diseases, making it a potential diagnostic biomarker for this malignancy. MiRNAs, including miR-575, are small non-coding RNAs transported via exosomes that function as intercellular chemical messengers and regulate gene expression by binding to target sequences. The study identified miR-575 as part of a broader class of exosomal biomarkers that can facilitate early cancer detection and prognosis prediction, offering advantages over conventional tumor markers like CA125 that lack ovarian cancer specificity due to elevation in benign conditions such as endometriosis. Mechanistically, miR-575 interacts with multiple downstream targets across various cancer types (including PTEN, CDKN1B, BRCA1, and RIPK4) and participates in pathways controlling cell proliferation, apoptosis, and metastasis. This study represents the first documentation of exosomal biomarkers in vaginal discharge specifically for ovarian cancer, expanding the potential for minimally invasive, body-fluid-based diagnostics in gynecological malignancies. The findings suggest that vaginal discharge sampling combined with exosomal miR-575 quantification could complement or improve upon existing serological markers for improved ovarian cancer screening and monitoring.

title: Malignant ascites in ovarian cancer is compatible with long-term (10 year) survival with associations to clinicopathological features
summary:
# Summary

Despite representing advanced disease, malignant ascites in ovarian cancer is compatible with long-term survival in a subset of patients, with favorable outcomes most strongly associated with younger age, low-grade serous and endometrioid histology, and favorable hematologic/biochemical parameters. Key independent predictors of overall survival identified through multivariable analysis include activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), ECOG performance status, total serum protein, and prior chemotherapy history. Patients receiving chemotherapy after ascites onset experienced significantly improved outcomes compared to those not treated, while elevated platelet counts—possibly driven by pro-inflammatory cytokine stimulation—correlated with poor survival. Clinical and laboratory variables such as hemoglobin levels greater than 10 g/dL, serum albumin above 40 g/dL, total protein exceeding 70 g/L, and age 50 years or younger defined cutoff values associated with substantially better survival outcomes, underscoring the prognostic importance of both patient characteristics and hematologic markers in this population.

title: Ultrasensitive Impedimetric Biosensor for Ovarian Cancer Screening
summary:
# Summary

This study presents an ultrasensitive impedimetric biosensor utilizing iron oxide-selenium (Fe₃O₄-Se) nanocomposites for detecting miRNA-21, a key biomarker elevated in ovarian cancer. miRNA-21 is significantly overexpressed in ovarian cancer tissues and circulating in blood, urine, and saliva, where it drives tumor progression, metastasis, and chemoresistance to cisplatin, making it a valuable diagnostic and prognostic target. The engineered Fe₃O₄-Se nanocomposite (11.87 nm crystallite size, 20 nm average diameter) exhibits superior magnetic properties and biosensing capabilities compared to conventional gold nanoparticles, achieving an exceptionally low detection limit of 75 zeptomolar with high selectivity against other miRNAs and biomolecules. Using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, the biosensor demonstrated a wide linear detection range from 75 zeptomolar to 0.751 picomolar, substantially outperforming existing serological ovarian cancer markers such as cancer antigen-125 and HE4. This nanocomposite-based approach offers a non-invasive, highly sensitive platform for consumer electronics-based ovarian cancer screening through readily accessible biosamples.

title: Carbon Nanotubes in the Diagnosis and Treatment of Ovarian Cancer
summary: Ovarian cancer suffers from late detection and high recurrence rates, complications worsened by conventional chemotherapy's limited bioavailability and toxicity to normal cells. Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, graphene derivatives, fullerenes, and carbon quantum dots, offer promising solutions for both diagnosis and treatment by enhancing drug delivery, reducing systemic toxicity, and enabling multi-modal therapeutic approaches. These nanocarriers facilitate targeted delivery of hydrophobic anticancer drugs to tumor microenvironments while minimizing immunogenicity when functionalized appropriately. For diagnostic applications, multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified with electrodes can detect ovarian cancer biomarkers including CA125, squamous cell carcinoma antigen, and carcinoembryonic antigen with enhanced electrical conductivity and sensitivity. Therapeutically, graphene nanoribbons decorated with sonosensitizers inhibit tumor spheroid growth, while graphdiyne and fullerene derivatives enable photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, and immune system stimulation alongside conventional chemotherapy. These carbon nanomaterial platforms represent a paradigm shift in ovarian cancer management by addressing both the detection challenge and the therapeutic limitations of current approaches.

title: FLOT1 Is a Novel Serum Biomarker of Ovarian Cancer Targeted by N6‐methyladenosine Modification Inhibition
summary: This study identifies FLOT1 as a novel serum biomarker for ovarian cancer with diagnostic and therapeutic potential. Serum FLOT1 levels are significantly elevated in ovarian cancer patients compared to healthy controls, with a detection rate of 77.78%, comparable to the standard marker CA125.
