FLOT1 is an integral membrane protein involved in membrane trafficking and signal transduction that promotes metastasis in ovarian cancer cells. The elevated FLOT1 expression in cancer cells is regulated by N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modification, which enhances mRNA stability; inhibiting m6A modification with 3-deazaadenosine reduces FLOT1 mRNA expression and suppresses tumor formation in xenograft models. The correlation between serum FLOT1 levels and FLOT1 overexpression in tumor tissue supports its clinical utility as a biomarker. These findings suggest that targeting m6A-mediated FLOT1 regulation represents a promising therapeutic strategy for ovarian cancer, while also highlighting that CA125 elevation in endometriosis may complicate diagnostic specificity.

title: The Role of CA125 and HE4 in Uterine Sarcomas: Beyond Diagnosis and Prognosis—A Systematic Review and Case Series from a Single Institution
summary: This systematic review examines serum biomarkers CA125 and HE4, traditionally used for ovarian and endometrial cancer detection, as potential diagnostic and prognostic tools for uterine sarcomas, particularly uterine leiomyosarcoma. CA125 levels were significantly elevated in patients with advanced leiomyosarcoma and associated with overall survival and disease progression, though notably absent from uterine sarcoma tissue itself, suggesting elevation derives from mesothelial irritation rather than direct tumor production. Elevated CA125 correlated with poor prognosis and was elevated in 75% of stage I cases with extrauterine spread, while HE4 showed elevation in 43% of patients, particularly among premenopausal women. Beyond CA125 and HE4, tumor size, lymph node positivity, high mitotic activity, vascular invasion, BMI, and histological subtype emerged as independent prognostic factors, with spindle cell leiomyosarcoma associated with longer overall survival compared to conventional subtypes. The ROMA score, designed for ovarian cancer risk assessment, was also evaluated for uterine leiomyosarcoma, showing elevation in 83% of premenopausal patients. These findings suggest CA125 may serve as a useful adjunctive marker for prognosis and treatment monitoring in uterine sarcomas, though its low diagnostic specificity limits standalone clinical application.

title: On the Quest for Biomarkers: A Comprehensive Analysis of Modified Nucleosides in Ovarian Cancer Cell Lines
summary: Ovarian cancer represents a significant clinical challenge due to poor long-term survival rates and non-specific early symptoms, with current biomarkers like CA-125 having limited diagnostic value. This study identifies modified nucleosides as promising biomarkers that accumulate in blood and urine from ovarian cancer cell lines, reflecting the altered DNA and RNA metabolism characteristic of malignant cells. Seven distinct ovarian cancer cell lines, including SKOV3, COV362, and A2780, demonstrate elevated levels of modified nucleosides compared to control cells, with nucleoside modifications supporting both tumor growth and progression through mechanisms including RNA stabilization and altered translation efficiency. These findings align with evidence from other cancer types such as breast, pancreatic, and kidney cancers, where modified nucleosides have already demonstrated utility as diagnostic biomarkers. The role of specific modifications like N6-methyladenosine in activating purine receptors and altering intracellular metabolic processes suggests that modified nucleosides serve as functional drivers of cancer phenotypes. These results support further clinical validation of modified nucleosides as potential non-invasive biomarkers for early detection and monitoring of ovarian cancer.

title: Characterization of small extracellular vesicles from ovarian cancer patients and pre-diagnostic patient samples: Evidence from the Danish blood donor study
summary:
# Summary

This study characterized small extracellular vesicles (EVs) from blood as potential ovarian cancer biomarkers, comparing samples from ovarian cancer patients, benign tumor patients, pre-diagnostic samples, and healthy controls. Extracellular vesicles circulate in blood and mediate cell-to-cell communication by transferring cargo including microRNAs between organs and tissues, with cancer cells producing EVs that differ significantly in composition from those of normal cells. The researchers identified several promising EV surface protein markers, including elevated CD63 and CD151 in ovarian cancer EVs, along with characteristic size heterogeneity distinguishing cancer patient samples from controls, though notably CA125 appeared only in two control samples and no pre-diagnostic samples. MicroRNA analysis revealed significantly reduced levels of hsa-miR-140-5p and miR-140-3p in ovarian cancer EVs compared to controls, suggesting potential diagnostic value. While the study found that many traditional ovarian cancer markers were not detectable on EVs, the combination of EV protein signatures and miRNA profiles shows promise for improving upon current biomarkers like CA125, which lack sufficient sensitivity and specificity for early-stage disease detection.

title: Utility of CA-125 for diagnosis and prognosis of breast cancer: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

CA-125, a mucin-derived serum biomarker produced by coelomic epithelium, demonstrates significant utility in breast cancer diagnosis, prognosis, and monitoring of disease progression. Elevated CA-125 levels are particularly associated with advanced disease stages, occurring in over 90% of stage IV breast cancer cases and correlating strongly with tumor progression, larger tumor size, lymph node involvement, and distant metastases including bone and peritoneal spread. The protein is overexpressed when cells undergo malignant transformation via upregulation of its parent molecule MUC16, and functions to promote cancer cell growth while suppressing antitumor immunity. Preoperative CA-125 testing predicts prognosis effectively, with levels below 35 U/mL indicating favorable outcomes, and is particularly useful in triple-negative breast cancer where CA-125 elevation is most common; additionally, CA-125 can be detected in multiple body fluids including serum, nipple discharge, and milk. The biomarker's clinical value is enhanced when combined with other markers such as AFP, CEA, and CA15-3, though notably it shows no correlation with HER2 status and can be elevated in non-malignant conditions including menstruation, pregnancy, and liver disease, necessitating careful clinical interpretation.

title: Infection characteristics and risk factors in ovarian cancer patients with concomitant vaginitis
summary: This study identified a significant association between vaginal infections and ovarian cancer, with vulvovaginal candidiasis and bacterial vaginosis each present in approximately one-third of ovarian cancer patients experiencing concomitant vaginitis. Patients with vaginal infections demonstrated substantially elevated levels of the pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines IL-4, IL-6, and IL-10 compared to non-infected controls, suggesting that local immune dysregulation accompanies these infections. Multiple modifiable risk factors were identified through multivariate analysis, including sexual frequency, self-vaginal douching practices, educational level, and a history of prior vaginitis, indicating that behavioral and demographic factors contribute to infection susceptibility in this population. The cancer biomarker HE4 showed no significant difference between infected and non-infected groups, suggesting it is not directly associated with concurrent vaginitis. These findings suggest that vaginal infections and their associated inflammatory responses may represent important clinical considerations in ovarian cancer patients, potentially serving as indicators of altered genital tract immunity that warrant further investigation for biomarker development.

title: Engrailed-2 (EN2) protein in cervical mucus: a novel biomarker for endometrial carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies Engrailed-2 (EN2) protein in cervical mucus as a novel non-invasive biomarker for detecting endometrial cancer and its precancerous lesions. EN2 protein is significantly elevated in endometrial cancer tissues and endometrial cancer cell lines (HEC1B and KLE), with elevated levels in cervical mucus that correlate with malignant pathology. Using a diagnostic threshold of 321.1 ng/mL determined by receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis, EN2 protein concentration effectively distinguishes endometrial cancer and endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia grade III from benign controls. Notably, EN2 demonstrates superior sensitivity compared to the established tumor marker CA125 across all cancer stages. EN2 expression is unassociated with pathological type, differentiation grade, or menopausal status, suggesting it functions as a consistent diagnostic indicator independent of these clinical variables. These findings support EN2 protein as a promising cervical mucus-based biomarker for endometrial cancer screening, consistent with EN2's established dysregulation in multiple cancer types including ovarian and bladder cancer.

title: Comparative Meta-Analysis of Carbohydrate Antigen 125 (CA125), Human Epididymis Protein 4 (HE4), and Diagnostic Indices (Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) and Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm (ROMA)) for Pre-operative Detection of Ovarian Carcinoma
summary: This meta-analysis compared four diagnostic approaches for preoperative detection of ovarian cancer, evaluating CA125, HE4, and the combined algorithms ROMA and RMI. CA125, an antigenic tumor marker expressed by ovarian epithelial cells and the endometrium, is elevated in approximately 80% of epithelial ovarian cancer cases but suffers from high false-positive rates due to elevation during menstruation and in pelvic endometriosis, resulting in high sensitivity (0.82) but compromised specificity. HE4, encoded by the WFDC2 gene and overexpressed in ovarian cancer, demonstrated superior specificity and diagnostic performance compared to CA125 alone, with a mean diagnostic odds ratio of 17.0. The combined diagnostic indices ROMA and RMI, which integrate CA125 and HE4 levels with menopausal status or ultrasound findings, showed comparable and superior performance to individual markers, with ROMA achieving the highest area under the curve (0.8619). These findings suggest that combined biomarker algorithms offer improved diagnostic reliability for ovarian cancer detection while reducing the false-positive burden associated with CA125 alone, particularly important in distinguishing benign endometriosis-related elevations from malignant pathology.

title: Retroperitoneal growth of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma: A case report
summary: High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC) originates from serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma in the fallopian tube epithelium and subsequently metastasizes to distant sites, including the retroperitoneum, through peritoneal extension without anatomical barriers restricting tumor cell dissemination. This case report documents a patient with HGSOC originating from the left fallopian tube that disseminated to form a retroperitoneal mass causing ureteral infiltration, with immunohistochemical analysis confirming the metastatic lesion through positive expression of CA125, PAX-8, and WT1 markers. CA125 serves as a valuable biomarker for HGSOC diagnosis and monitoring treatment response, though it lacks specificity for early detection given its elevation in endometriosis and other conditions; elevated serum CA125 levels are observed in approximately 90% of advanced HGSOC cases. Current therapeutic approaches for HGSOC include cytoreductive surgery combined with platinum-based chemotherapy (paclitaxel and carboplatin), with complete tumor resection associated with improved long-term outcomes, and emerging treatments such as bevacizumab maintenance therapy and PARP inhibitors for BRCA-positive tumors. Genetic mutations in tumor suppressors including WT1, P16, and BRCA genes contribute to HGSOC pathogenesis, and WT1-targeted vaccines represent a promising immunotherapeutic strategy under clinical investigation for ovarian cancer treatment.

title: Comprehensive overview of antibody drug-related clinical studies in gynecology: insights from ClinicalTrials.gov
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive analysis of antibody drug-related clinical trials in gynecology reveals that monoclonal antibodies and engineered proteins have become central to treating gynecological malignancies and related conditions. Immune checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1/PD-L1 and CTLA-4 pathways represent the most extensively studied class, with pembrolizumab and dostarlimab approved for treating cervical and endometrial cancers, while cadonilimab demonstrates bispecific targeting of both pathways. Bevacizumab, which inhibits VEGF-mediated neovascularization, has established efficacy as first-line therapy for epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, and primary peritoneal cancers when combined with chemotherapy, and demonstrates sustained benefit in cervical cancer maintenance therapy. Emerging antibody therapeutics include HER2-targeting agents like trastuzumab-based combinations for endometrial serous carcinoma, folate receptor-alpha directed therapy, and novel bispecific agents such as navicixizumab targeting both DLL4 and VEGF for ovarian cancer. Notably, despite endometriosis being a prevalent gynecological condition associated with ovarian cancer development, only a limited number of clinical trials have evaluated antibody-based treatments specifically for endometriosis-related pain, representing a significant gap in current therapeutic development.

title: Role of CDH1 gene variants and E-cadherin localization in gastric mucosal cancerization
summary:
# Summary

The CDH1 gene encodes E-cadherin, a calcium-dependent adhesion molecule essential for maintaining cell-cell contacts, cytoskeleton stabilization, and normal tissue morphology. Aberrant E-cadherin expression—characterized by decreased membrane localization and pathological cytoplasmic redistribution—significantly increases the risk of gastric mucosal cancerization, with cytoplasmic ectopic expression elevating severe dysplasia and gastric cancer risk by up to 20-fold in epithelial regions. Long-term Helicobacter pylori infection initiates a cascade of gastric mucosal damage including atrophy and intestinal metaplasia, conditions associated with altered CDH1 mRNA levels and aberrant E-cadherin localization that promote malignant transformation. The CDH1-160 C>A promoter polymorphism influences gene expression and cancer susceptibility across multiple cancer types including ovarian, breast, and gastric cancers, with the A allele potentially conferring protective effects. These findings establish E-cadherin localization patterns and CDH1 genetic variants as important biomarkers for identifying individuals at elevated risk of gastric cancer development and progression.

title: Assessment of knowledge and awareness about ovarian cancer and its risk factors among women in Jazan, Saudi Arabia: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study assessed knowledge and awareness of ovarian cancer and its risk factors among women in Jazan, Saudi Arabia, revealing significant gaps in understanding despite the disease's serious clinical implications. Ovarian cancer's insidious presentation, characterized by peritoneal dissemination and limited early symptoms, underscores the critical importance of early detection, which substantially improves long-term survival rates and quality of life. The study identified established risk factors including age over 50, family history, nulliparity, delayed childbirth, reduced breastfeeding duration, and hormone replacement therapy use, while also demonstrating that phytoestrogen-rich diets (soy, whole grains, legumes) are associated with lower incidence. Knowledge and awareness levels were significantly correlated and associated with education level, age group, nationality, and occupation, with higher education showing good to moderate knowledge. The findings underscore that increased awareness of ovarian cancer symptoms and risk factors—particularly BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations warranting genetic testing and prophylactic interventions—can encourage timely medical assistance and screening participation, while biomarkers like CA125 may support screening despite limitations in specificity, as elevated levels also occur in benign conditions such as endometriosis.

title: Ovarian Cancer—Epidemiology, Classification, Pathogenesis, Treatment, and Estrogen Receptors’ Molecular Backgrounds
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer is a hormone-dependent malignancy strongly associated with estrogen signaling, particularly through elevated ERα receptor expression encoded by the ESR1 gene, which serves as a molecular biomarker in 60% of cases. Inherited mutations in BRCA1, BRCA2, and other DNA repair genes (RAD51C, RAD51D, BRIP1) significantly increase ovarian cancer risk, while endometriosis substantially elevates the risk of specific histological subtypes, particularly clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas. Type I ovarian cancers characteristically harbor BRAF and KRAS mutations, whereas Type II tumors are defined by p53 mutations in approximately 90% of cases. Preventive strategies include oral contraceptive use (reducing risk by up to 60%), multiple pregnancies, and prophylactic ovariectomy in high-risk BRCA1/2 mutation carriers. Current treatment approaches combine surgery with chemotherapy using paclitaxel and platinum derivatives, complemented by hormonal agents and PARP inhibitors for maintenance therapy, with tumor markers CA125 and HE4 used for diagnostic assessment.

title: Knockdown TNF family prognosis index crucial gene PDE4B promoted PANoptosis of ovarian carcinoma cell:Based in vitro and in vivo experiments
summary: This study identified a 14-gene prognostic signature for ovarian cancer, with TNF family genes playing a central role in cancer pathogenesis. PDE4B emerged as a crucial prognostic biomarker whose knockdown significantly promoted PANoptosis—a form of cell death encompassing apoptosis, pyroptosis, and necroptosis—in ovarian cancer cells. The researchers demonstrated that PDE4B suppression reduced cell proliferation and activated multiple death pathways involving caspase-1, caspase-3, GSDMD, GSDME, and MLKL proteins in both in vitro cell models and in vivo xenograft experiments. The prognostic model also incorporated other key genes including GFPT2, PODNL1, TGFBI, and CSF1R, validated across multiple datasets. These findings suggest that PDE4B represents a promising therapeutic target for ovarian cancer, with potential clinical relevance for patients stratified by this prognostic signature.

title: Diagnostic Value of PET/CT for Ovarian Cancer Recurrence or Metastasis in Postoperative Patients With Elevated Serum CA125 Levels: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that 18F-FDG PET/CT is highly effective for detecting ovarian cancer recurrence and metastasis in postoperative patients, achieving a pooled sensitivity of 0.94 and specificity of 0.83, with an exceptional diagnostic odds ratio of 64.22. Elevated serum CA125 levels after surgery strongly associate with ovarian cancer relapse and metastasis, though CA125 alone cannot localize lesions anatomically and may increase in benign conditions such as endometriosis and peritoneal inflammation. The combined evaluation of CA125 and PET/CT imaging represents an improved diagnostic approach, as PET/CT identifies metabolic changes at the molecular level and reveals radiopharmaceutical uptake distribution in tumor tissues, complementing CA125's sensitivity with anatomical localization. Most malignant ovarian tumors exhibit high FDG uptake, making this imaging modality particularly valuable for early lesion detection; however, PET/CT has a limitation in detecting microlesions smaller than 0.5 centimeters. Further enhancement of diagnostic accuracy is achieved when PET/CT is combined with both CA125 and HE4 biomarker levels, providing clinicians with reliable information for treatment decisions and monitoring disease progression in ovarian cancer survivors.

title: Scope and limitations of intraoperative cytological methods of diagnosis of ovarian tumors
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer risk is associated with multiple factors including aging, BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, obesity, and hormone replacement therapy. The serum biomarker CA-125 lacks diagnostic specificity, as it is elevated not only in early ovarian cancer but also in benign conditions such as endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease, limiting its utility for cancer detection. Intraoperative cytological methods, particularly frozen sections and imprint cytology, are essential for rapid pathological assessment during surgery, with frozen sections remaining the gold standard despite being susceptible to freezing artifacts that compromise tissue structure. Imprint cytology offers superior cytomorphological preservation and similar diagnostic accuracy to frozen sections, while scrape cytology provides better cellular yield, though both methods struggle with borderline tumors due to tumor heterogeneity. Certain tumor types, including mucinous tumors and clear cell carcinomas, present diagnostic challenges; clear cell carcinoma is particularly notable as it requires accurate intraoperative diagnosis to guide aggressive surgical debulking, which directly improves patient survival. Overall, the choice of intraoperative diagnostic technique must account for tumor type-specific characteristics and the critical need for rapid, accurate diagnosis to optimize surgical intervention.

title: Diagnostic accuracy of frozen section in ovarian masses: A single centre study
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of frozen section analysis in classifying ovarian masses as benign, borderline, or malignant at a single center. Frozen section achieved an overall accuracy of 90%, demonstrating high sensitivity (96.2%) and specificity (86.9%) for benign tumors and excellent sensitivity (89.4%) with perfect specificity (100%) for malignant tumors, though borderline tumors proved diagnostically challenging with only 50% sensitivity. The technique significantly aids intraoperative decision-making by enabling surgeons to tailor surgical approach appropriately—preserving fertility through conservative surgery in benign cases while ensuring complete staging in malignancies. However, frozen section has notable limitations, particularly with large borderline and mucinous tumors that exhibit tumor heterogeneity, as well as susceptibility to both overdiagnosis and underdiagnosis, emphasizing the importance of continued efforts to improve diagnostic accuracy in borderline lesions. These findings underscore frozen section's critical clinical utility as a rapid histological diagnostic tool that helps prevent unnecessary extensive surgeries while avoiding suboptimal surgical management.

title: Diagnostic value of the Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm (ROMA) index in the detection of ovarian cancer in postmenopausal women: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: The ROMA index, a composite biomarker combining CA125 and HE4 levels with menopausal status, demonstrates significant diagnostic value for differentiating ovarian cancer from benign ovarian tumors in postmenopausal women. This meta-analysis found that the ROMA index achieved 90% sensitivity and specificity in cross-sectional studies, with particularly strong performance in postmenopausal Asian women (88% sensitivity, 93% specificity). While CA125 alone is commonly used for ovarian cancer detection, it suffers from limited specificity due to elevation in benign conditions including endometriosis, myomas, ovarian cysts, and pelvic inflammatory disease, making the dual-marker ROMA approach clinically advantageous. The composite index shows superior discrimination compared to individual biomarkers, especially in postmenopausal populations where CA125 levels naturally increase with age. Early detection using the ROMA index is critical given that ovarian cancer presents with non-specific symptoms and early diagnosis substantially improves patient survival rates. These findings support incorporating the ROMA index into diagnostic algorithms for postmenopausal women with suspected ovarian pathology.

title: Risk-reducing salpingectomy: considerations from an OBGYN perspective
summary: Recent evidence demonstrates that high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), a major type of ovarian cancer, frequently originates in the fallopian tube rather than the ovary itself, fundamentally changing ovarian cancer prevention strategies. Risk-reducing salpingectomy (RRS)—surgical removal of the fallopian tubes—has emerged as an effective primary prevention intervention that reduces ovarian cancer risk, particularly in women with family histories of ovarian cancer or early-onset breast cancer. Unlike complete ovarian removal, which prevents HGSC but causes substantial morbidity through increased all-cause mortality, coronary heart disease, and osteoporosis, salpingectomy preserves ovarian function and its protective health benefits. Risk stratification tools such as CanRisk can estimate lifetime ovarian cancer risk to identify candidates for RRS, while established protective factors like oral contraceptive use also reduce ovarian cancer risk. However, clinicians must weigh the benefits of RRS against potential surgical complications, which remain a significant consideration. This approach represents a paradigm shift toward targeted tube removal in high-risk populations while maintaining ovarian hormone production and avoiding the systemic consequences of oophorectomy.

title: Accurate prediction of benign and malignant adnexal tumors in surgical resection and conservative treatment: construction and external validation of a diagnostic model based on CEUS, HE4, and O-RADS US v2022 evaluation
summary: This study validates a multimodal diagnostic model combining O-RADS US v2022, contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS), and the serum biomarker HE4 for distinguishing benign from malignant adnexal tumors. CEUS imaging parameters including enhancement time, enhancement intensity, and dynamic changes all independently predict malignancy, while HE4 levels are significantly elevated in ovarian cancer compared to benign conditions and provide superior specificity to CA125, which is nonspecifically elevated in endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease. The integrated three-modality model achieved an area under the curve of 0.980, substantially outperforming O-RADS US v2022 alone or combined with CEUS, thereby markedly improving diagnostic specificity and reducing unnecessary overtreatment of benign lesions. HE4 additionally enables early cancer detection and aids in post-treatment surveillance, addressing limitations of previous O-RADS US v2020 protocols. This combined approach provides both enhanced diagnostic accuracy and clinically meaningful improvements in the management of women with adnexal masses.

title: Non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis in ovarian cancer: A systematic review
summary: Non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE) represents a serious paraneoplastic complication predominantly associated with advanced ovarian cancer, particularly adenocarcinomas, occurring in 67% of cases at stage III or IV disease. The pathophysiology involves macrophages and malignant cells releasing cytokines such as tumor necrosis factor and interleukins, which damage valvular endothelial cells and trigger platelet aggregation and thrombus formation on the mitral valve (52.4% of cases) and aortic valve (33.3%), with these sterile vegetations prone to systemic embolization. NBTE manifests clinically with cerebrovascular accidents as the most common presentation (53% of cases), organ infarcts affecting the brain and spleen (27% each), and elevated biomarkers including CA-125, D-dimer, and thrombocytopenia. Management involves long-term anticoagulation with heparin and chemotherapy targeting the underlying ovarian cancer, with chemotherapy demonstrating dramatic efficacy in reducing mortality to 40% compared to 100% mortality in untreated patients. The association between NBTE and ovarian cancer, combined with negative blood cultures and documented valvular vegetations on echocardiography, distinguishes this complication from infectious endocarditis and warrants early recognition given its high morbidity and mortality in this patient population.

title: Multi-analyte approach combining cfDNA sequencing and protein testing for early ovarian cancer detection
summary: This study presents a multi-analyte approach combining circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) sequencing with protein biomarkers for improved early ovarian cancer detection, particularly relevant to distinguishing ovarian cancer from benign conditions like endometriosis. The EarlySEEK model integrates CA125, HE4, ctDNA detection, and additional proteins (prolactin, interleukin-6, and cancer antigen 19-9) to achieve superior diagnostic accuracy, with an AUC of 0.97 and 93% sensitivity in validation cohorts. Key findings demonstrate that ctDNA detection rates increase with disease stage (36.8% in stage I to 68.5% in stage III), while TP53 is the most frequently mutated gene in epithelial ovarian cancer (42.0%), followed by PIK3CA and APC. The combination of CA125 and ctDNA significantly outperforms CA125 alone, particularly for early-stage disease detection, with an AUC of 0.96 compared to individual biomarkers. Notably, the EarlySEEK model's performance is unaffected by age or menopausal status, and it demonstrates higher sensitivity compared to the traditional ROMA (Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm) index. This multi-analyte approach addresses the clinical challenge that CA125 alone is undetectable in up to 50% of stage I ovarian cancer patients and is elevated in benign conditions, thereby enabling earlier detection when ovarian cancer is more curable.

title: Mechanistic Insights into Tumorigenesis from Serum Proteins
summary:
# Summary

This proteomic study identified serum protein biomarkers associated with multiple cancer types and their underlying biological mechanisms. Established cancer biomarkers such as BRCA1/BRCA2 for breast and ovarian cancer, PSA/KLK3 for prostate cancer, and CA-125/MUC16 for ovarian cancer were confirmed, alongside novel associations including SIGLEC6 with bladder and pancreatic cancer, and HAVCR1 with kidney and lung cancer. Eight proteins (MPP2, TRAT1, TMEM106A, UBE2E3, TREM2, CREBBP, ITIH1, and RAB22A) demonstrated associations with multiple cancer types, with distinct mechanistic roles including immune modulation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, tumor microenvironment remodeling, and cellular senescence. Additional findings highlighted tissue-specific biomarkers such as trefoil factors TFF2 and TFF3 (linked to gastric and breast cancers through epithelial integrity mechanisms) and WNT10B in breast cancer through Wnt/β-catenin pathway activation. These results provide comprehensive insights into circulating protein signatures that may serve as predictive tools for cancer development while elucidating the molecular pathways driving tumorigenesis.

title: IGF-II promoter methylation and ovarian cancer prognosis
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study demonstrates that DNA methylation patterns at different promoters of the insulin-like growth factor-II (IGF-II) gene serve as important biomarkers for ovarian cancer prognosis. Since the IGF-II gene contains multiple promoters that are regulated by DNA methylation, the authors examined how methylation variations at three IGF-II promoters correlate with clinical outcomes in ovarian cancer patients. Methylation at the P2 promoter was significantly associated with adverse prognostic features, including high tumor grade, suboptimal surgical debulking, and increased disease progression (hazard ratio 1.73). Conversely, P3 promoter methylation without concurrent P2 methylation was associated with reduced disease progression risk (hazard ratio 0.39), indicating a protective effect. These contrasting methylation patterns reveal that IGF-II promoter-specific methylation variation can stratify ovarian cancer patients into distinct prognostic groups, with P2 methylation status emerging as a potential molecular marker for identifying patients at higher risk of aggressive disease and poor outcomes.

title: Increased activity of lysosomal enzymes in the peritoneal fluid of patients with gynecologic cancers and pelvic inflammatory disease
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Lysosomal enzymes in peritoneal fluid represent a novel biomarker for gynecologic cancers and pelvic inflammatory disease, with three key enzymes—β-glucuronidase, β-galactosidase, and α-mannosidase—significantly elevated in both conditions compared to healthy controls. In gynecologic cancer patients, β-glucuronidase activity was approximately 5-fold higher (120 nmol) than controls, while β-galactosidase and α-mannosidase showed 4-5 fold increases. Pelvic inflammatory disease also demonstrated substantially elevated enzyme activity, with β-galactosidase and α-mannosidase levels comparable to or exceeding those in cancer patients. Notably, β-glucuronidase activity correlated positively with cancer stage, suggesting these enzymes may serve as staging biomarkers. The elevation of lysosomal enzyme activity occurred even in cancer cases without malignant cells detected in peritoneal fluid, indicating these enzymes reflect pelvic pathology independently of direct cellular involvement. These findings establish lysosomal enzyme activity as a promising diagnostic and prognostic indicator for distinguishing gynecologic malignancies and inflammatory conditions from normal pelvic physiology.

title: Deep plasma proteomics identifies and validates an eight-protein biomarker panel that separate benign from malignant tumors in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study used deep plasma proteomics to develop and validate an eight-protein biomarker signature for distinguishing benign from malignant ovarian tumors, addressing limitations of the conventional CA-125 marker. CA-125 (MUC16 protein) exhibits poor sensitivity for early-stage ovarian cancer and is frequently elevated in benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, acute pancreatitis, and heart failure, reducing its clinical utility. The researchers identified 191 unique proteins associated with ovarian cancer from 327 biomarker associations and developed an optimized eight-protein panel that achieved 97% sensitivity and 68% specificity for benign versus malignant separation overall, with 91% sensitivity for early-stage tumors compared to CA-125's 85% sensitivity. While only 11% of the 191 identified proteins showed significant direct correlation with tumor gene expression, circulating biomarkers likely originate from both tumor cells and surrounding tissue, with 62 proteins showing co-expression patterns suggesting coordinated biological networks. The eight-protein biomarker model substantially outperforms existing multi-protein assays such as ROMA Score, OVA1, and OVERA, offering improved diagnostic accuracy for ovarian cancer detection regardless of disease stage.

title: Impacts of BRCA mutations and clinical factors on niraparib efficacy in patients with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study identified multiple biomarkers that predict niraparib efficacy in patients with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer. Multivariate analysis confirmed that BRCA mutations, lower CA125 levels (≤500 U/mL), higher BMI (≥24 kg/m²), platinum sensitivity, and FIGO staging are independent predictors of progression-free survival outcomes following niraparib treatment. Platinum sensitivity and FIGO staging emerged as the most influential factors for both 1-year and 3-year PFS prediction through time-dependent ROC analysis. While niraparib demonstrated clinical benefit in this cohort, adverse events occurred in 62.84% of patients, primarily manifesting as hematological and gastrointestinal toxicities. These findings suggest that personalized risk stratification based on BRCA status, tumor biomarkers, and clinical parameters can optimize treatment selection and prognostication for niraparib therapy in recurrent ovarian cancer management.

title: Ovarian cancer staging and follow-up: updated guidelines from the European Society of Urogenital Radiology female pelvic imaging working group
summary:
# Summary

High-grade serous carcinoma represents the most common histologic subtype of ovarian cancer, typically presenting at advanced stages with extensive peritoneal disease and potentially distant metastases. Current European guidelines emphasize comprehensive multi-modality imaging for staging and follow-up, with CT serving as the primary imaging modality of choice due to its widespread availability, cost-effectiveness, and rapid acquisition time, particularly using multislice scanners with multiplanar reconstructions and portal venous phase contrast enhancement. Complete staging now includes chest CT to detect FIGO stage IVB findings such as pleural metastases, pleural effusion, thoracic lymphadenopathy, and pulmonary nodules, while abdominal and pelvic imaging assesses resectability factors including peritoneal carcinomatosis, bowel invasion, mesenteric involvement, and diaphragmatic disease. MRI and [18F]FDG PET-CT serve as complementary modalities, with whole-body MRI superior for detecting challenging-to-resect sites such as serosal and mesenteric involvement, though PET-CT may yield false positives with endometriosis and fails to detect mucinous and clear cell subtypes due to low FDG uptake. Pre-operative imaging is critical for multidisciplinary treatment planning to achieve optimal cytoreduction combined with platinum-based chemotherapy, while CT remains the standard modality for post-treatment follow-up and recurrence detection, supplemented by [18F]FDG PET-CT when elevated CA-125 occurs with negative CT findings.

title: Treatment of ovarian cancer: From the past to the new era (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer presents with non-specific symptoms including abdominal bloating, pain, and urinary changes, making early detection challenging. Key risk factors include endometriosis, BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, and Lynch syndrome, while protective factors such as multiparity, contraceptive use, and lactation reduce cancer risk through mechanisms counteracting incessant ovulation and chronic pelvic inflammation. Biomarkers including CA-125, HE4, circulating tumor DNA, microRNAs, and MUC16 enhance diagnostic accuracy, particularly for advanced-stage disease. Treatment approaches combine cytoreductive surgery with platinum-based chemotherapy and taxanes as first-line therapy, while PARP inhibitors provide targeted benefits for BRCA-mutated cases, reducing recurrence risk by up to 70%. Bevacizumab addition improves progression-free survival, though chemotherapy-associated toxicities including nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, and fatigue remain clinical concerns manageable through carboplatin and alternative agents for platinum-resistant disease.

title: Emerging trends of the tumor microenvironment in peritoneal malignancies (2010-2024): a visualization analysis
summary: This bibliometric analysis examines trends in tumor microenvironment (TME) research in peritoneal malignancies from 2010-2024, revealing that the TME plays a central role in peritoneal cancer progression through multiple cellular and molecular mechanisms. Key immune and stromal components—including tumor-associated macrophages, cancer-associated fibroblasts, adipocytes, mesothelial cells, and regulatory T cells—actively promote peritoneal metastasis and angiogenesis, while peritoneal macrophages regulate CD8+ T cell adaptive immunity. The research identifies cytoreductive surgery (CRS) combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy as the primary treatment approach, with complete CRS significantly improving progression-free survival, distant metastasis-free survival, and overall survival in ovarian and gastric cancers. Emerging therapeutic strategies increasingly target the TME through immunotherapy and angiogenesis inhibition, with single-cell RNA sequencing identified as a crucial tool for developing personalized treatments. The analysis also highlights endometriosis, hypoxia, inflammation, and immunosuppression as critical TME-associated factors, alongside extracellular matrix components, exosomes, and extracellular vesicles in mediating tumor biology. These findings underscore that comprehensive understanding of TME composition and dynamics is essential for developing next-generation therapeutic interventions in peritoneal malignancies.

title: HE-4 in the Diagnosis of Carcinoma of Ovary Presented as Unknown primary Carcinoma: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates the superior clinical utility of HE4 (human epididymis protein 4) as a biomarker for ovarian cancer diagnosis and monitoring, particularly in cases where CA-125 levels remain normal. The patient presented with advanced ovarian cancer detected as an unknown primary carcinoma, exhibiting markedly elevated HE4 (462 pmol/L) while maintaining normal CA-125 levels, highlighting HE4's advantage in early detection and stage I disease identification. Unlike CA-125, which is elevated in only 50% of early-stage cases, HE4 maintains high specificity for ovarian malignancy and does not elevate in benign conditions like endometriosis, making it valuable for differential diagnosis. The combined use of HE4 and CA-125 in the ROMA algorithm achieved 94% sensitivity and 85% detection in early-stage disease, substantially improving diagnostic accuracy. The patient underwent standard chemotherapy with bevacizumab, paclitaxel, and carboplatin, with HE4 levels serving as a superior marker for monitoring treatment response and detecting recurrence earlier than CA-125. Elevated persistent HE4 levels are independently associated with poor prognosis, disease progression, and reduced recurrence-free and overall survival, establishing HE4 as a critical prognostic indicator in ovarian cancer management.

title: Diagnostic value of serum pepsinogen, gastrin, and carbohydrate antigens in gastric ulcer and gastric cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated serum biomarkers for distinguishing gastric ulcer from gastric cancer, finding that pepsinogen (PG) and gastrin-17 (G17) levels are elevated in both conditions but with distinct patterns. While both gastric ulcer and gastric cancer show elevated PGII and G17, the PGI/PGII ratio is markedly reduced in gastric cancer but only moderately reduced in ulcer, making this ratio a key discriminatory marker. Carbohydrate antigen biomarkers—CEA, CA19-9, and CA72-4—are significantly elevated exclusively in gastric cancer patients and associate with disease progression. Combined detection of PGII, the PGI/PGII ratio, G17, and tumor markers substantially improves diagnostic accuracy for gastric cancer, with CA72-4 achieving the highest individual diagnostic performance (AUC 0.778). These findings demonstrate that serological biomarker profiling effectively differentiates gastric pathologies and optimizes early detection of gastric malignancies.

title: Assessment of the prognostic value of CA125 for miscarriage risk in patients with threatened abortion: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: CA125, a glycoprotein produced by decidual cells and expressed on coelomic epithelium-derived tissues including the endometrium, peritoneum, and fallopian tubes, serves as a prognostic biomarker for miscarriage risk in threatened abortion. During normal pregnancy, CA125 remains localized in the amniotic cavity; however, when placental barrier integrity is compromised in threatened abortion, the protein enters maternal circulation due to decidual cell damage and trophoblast injury. Elevated serum CA125 levels correlate with the extent of trophoblast destruction, placental inflammation, endometrial inflammatory state, and subchorionic hemorrhage, all of which are associated with increased miscarriage risk. Meta-analysis findings demonstrate that CA125 exhibits 91% specificity for predicting miscarriage risk in threatened abortion patients, making it a clinically useful biomarker for stratifying pregnancy loss risk. Beyond its obstetric applications, CA125 is an established diagnostic and monitoring marker for gynecological malignancies including ovarian carcinoma, endometrial carcinoma, and endometriosis, reflecting its broad utility across reproductive medicine. These findings support CA125 as a valuable prognostic tool for identifying high-risk pregnancies that may progress to miscarriage or other adverse perinatal outcomes such as fetal growth restriction and placenta previa.

title: Genome-Wide cfDNA Methylation Profiling Reveals Robust Hypermethylation Signatures in Ovarian Cancer
summary: This genome-wide study identifies robust DNA methylation signatures in circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) as potential biomarkers for ovarian cancer detection. Using cfMeDIP-seq, researchers discovered 536 differentially methylated regions (DMRs) between ovarian cancer patients and controls, with fifteen genes showing consistent hypermethylation patterns. These hypermethylated genes are predominantly involved in gland development, embryonic morphogenesis, and endocrine regulation, suggesting that aberrant methylation of developmentally important genes contributes to tumorigenesis. The DMRs were enriched in CpG islands and gene bodies, consistent with the characteristic cancer methylation pattern of global hypomethylation in repetitive regions alongside localized hypermethylation in promoter regions that silences tumor suppressor genes. These cfDNA hypermethylation patterns outperformed traditional markers like CA125 and the Risk of Malignancy Index, demonstrating the clinical potential of cfMeDIP-seq as a non-invasive approach for detecting tumor-specific epigenetic alterations in plasma. The findings underscore cell-free DNA methylation profiling as a promising liquid biopsy strategy for early ovarian cancer detection and monitoring.

title: Sequential Plasma Metabolome and Proteome Analyses to Develop a Novel Monitoring Strategy for Patients with Epithelial Ovarian Cancer: A Pilot Study
summary: This pilot study employed sequential plasma metabolomic and proteomic analyses to identify novel biomarkers for monitoring epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) patients, particularly those with poor chemotherapy response. The researchers identified ten metabolic indicators and multiple proteins associated with chemotherapy resistance, including elevated polyamine synthesis, serotonin, phospholipase A2 activity, and glycolytic enzymes, alongside reduced levels of protective metabolites like 2-methylbutyrylglycinuria and certain protective proteins. Key mechanistic findings revealed that serotonin promotes ovarian cancer growth while simultaneously suppressing tumor-associated CD8+ T cells and upregulating PD-L1, and that dysbiosis-associated alterations in bile acid enterohepatic circulation contribute to chemotherapy resistance. The study demonstrated that integrin signaling proteins (integrin beta-3, vinculin, and Talin-1) were elevated in poor-response patients, while cathepsin D and aminopeptidase N were reduced. These findings suggest that multiomics profiling could enable personalized monitoring strategies for EOC patients and identify potential targets for overcoming chemotherapy resistance, complementing current treatment approaches with bevacizumab and PARP inhibitors.

title: CA125 as a Potential Biomarker in Non-Malignant Serous Effusions: Diagnostic and Prognostic Considerations
summary: CA125, the extracellular fragment of MUC16 produced by coelomic and mesothelial cells lining serous cavities, serves as a clinically important biomarker with applications extending far beyond ovarian cancer. While CA125 is predominantly elevated in epithelial ovarian cancer—present in up to 50% of early-stage cases and 85% of advanced cases—the marker is nonspecifically elevated in numerous benign and malignant conditions, including endometriosis, adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, heart failure, cirrhosis, autoimmune diseases, and multiple cancer types. In endometriosis specifically, CA125 shows marked elevation and correlates with disease severity, though with variable sensitivity and specificity for diagnostic purposes. The underlying mechanisms of CA125 elevation involve mechanical stretching of mesothelial cells by fluid accumulation, upregulation by proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-10), and in some conditions like heart failure, bacterial translocation from intestinal congestion. CA125 demonstrates prognostic value in heart failure, where elevated levels predict poor ultrafiltration outcomes and increased risk of hospitalization and 1-year mortality, correlating specifically with systemic venous congestion and right-sided heart dysfunction rather than left ventricular dysfunction. These findings underscore that while CA125 remains valuable for monitoring ovarian cancer treatment response, its clinical interpretation in other conditions requires integration with imaging, clinical presentation, and additional biomarkers to distinguish malignant from benign etiologies.

title: The Effect of MicroRNA 21 and MicroRNA 200b Expression on Carcinogenesis in Endometriosis-Associated Ovarian Cancers and Relationship with Clinicopathological Parameters
summary:
# Summary

This study examines the role of microRNA-21 and microRNA-200b as potential biomarkers for endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC), demonstrating that both microRNAs are significantly elevated in the eutopic endometrium of patients with EAOC compared to control groups. MicroRNA-21 exhibits oncogenic properties and its upregulation in ovarian cancers correlates with advanced disease stage, higher tumor grade, and shorter overall survival, while also promoting cisplatin resistance through PTEN suppression. Conversely, the microRNA-200 family, particularly microRNA-200b, generally functions as a tumor suppressor through regulation of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, stem cell phenotype, and cell proliferation via targeting of transcription factors ZEB1, ZEB2, and KLF4. The study found significantly higher expression levels of both microRNAs in endometriotic foci and cancer tissues compared to normal eutopic endometrium, suggesting these microRNAs may serve as molecular markers for disease progression from endometriosis to malignant transformation. These findings support the emerging understanding that microRNA dysregulation plays a critical role in the carcinogenesis of endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers and could inform future therapeutic strategies targeting these pathways.

title: Case Report: Serous borderline ovarian tumor and extensive abdominopelvic endometriosis mimicking advanced epithelial ovarian cancer in a postmenopausal patient
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a postmenopausal patient with a 20-year history of endometriosis who presented with a serous borderline ovarian tumor (BOT) arising within an extensive endometriotic cyst, initially mimicking advanced epithelial ovarian cancer. Endometriosis confers significant malignant potential through multiple mechanisms, including repetitive inflammatory cycles, mutations in key genes such as ARID1A, K-RAS, B-RAF, and PTEN, elevated estrogen levels, and progression through atypical endometriosis to borderline and invasive tumors. The patient's risk factors—long-standing endometriosis, large endometriomas (>9 cm), infertility, and low parity—are established contributors to tumor development, with BRAF or KRAS mutations found in 88% of BOTs representing early oncogenic events. Despite initial concern for advanced malignancy based on elevated CA125 levels, final pathology confirmed a stage IC3 serous BOT with low malignant potential, treated definitively with cytoreductive surgery including total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. This case illustrates the diagnostic challenge of distinguishing endometriosis-associated BOTs from advanced epithelial ovarian cancer and underscores the importance of recognizing that endometriosis-associated cancers typically occur in younger patients with better prognosis than sporadic ovarian cancers, despite their occurrence in a postmenopausal individual.

title: Methylated ARHGAP40 DNA as a potential biomarker for early diagnosis in high-grade ovarian serous cancer
summary:
# Summary

High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) of the ovary is characterized by epigenetic silencing of ARHGAP40, a negative regulator of Rho GTPase signaling that promotes cell morphology control. ARHGAP40 protein is robustly expressed in normal fallopian tube epithelium, benign ovarian tumors, and borderline tumors, but is absent or weakly expressed in 93.8% of HGSC cases due to CpG island hypermethylation in the ARHGAP40 promoter region, which directly suppresses gene transcription. This methylation-mediated silencing appears to be a hallmark of malignant transformation in high-grade disease, as methylated ARHGAP40 circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) was detected in 80% of HGSC patient plasma but was absent in healthy controls and benign tumor samples. Circulating methylated ARHGAP40 ctDNA thus emerges as a potential biomarker for early detection and differentiation of HGSC from benign or low-grade lesions, potentially complementing existing markers like CA125 and HE4 that show limited efficacy in early-stage disease. This liquid biopsy approach addresses a critical clinical need, as the majority of ovarian cancers are diagnosed at advanced stages when five-year survival is only 20-41%, compared to over 90% survival when detected early.

title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals the immunotherapeutic significance of tumor cells with high FN1 expression in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This integrated multi-omics study identifies fibronectin-1 (FN1) expression on tumor cells as a critical determinant of ovarian cancer prognosis and immunotherapy resistance. High FN1-expressing tumor cells are associated with poor survival outcomes and contribute to an immunosuppressive microenvironment through FN1-CD44 mediated interactions with immune cells and stromal components. Functionally, FN1 knockdown significantly inhibits tumor growth by reducing cell aggregation, invasion, and migration while diminishing overall tumor cell invasiveness and activity. The researchers employed single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and CellChat analysis to reveal that FN1+ tumor cells facilitate immune evasion and promote microenvironments favorable to proliferation, making FN1 a potential therapeutic target and biomarker for predicting immunotherapy response in epithelial ovarian cancer. These findings suggest that FN1 expression profiling could enable more precise immunotherapy strategies and improved clinical staging of ovarian cancer patients.

title: Ovarian Cancer and the Risk of Cataract Episodes: A Nationwide Cohort Study
summary: This nationwide cohort study reveals that ovarian cancer patients experience a marginally elevated incidence of cataracts and cataract surgery, with oxidative stress emerging as a unifying pathophysiological mechanism linking both conditions. Multiple metabolic and hormonal factors contribute to ovarian cancer risk, including endometriosis, delayed childbearing, oral contraceptive use, hormone replacement therapy, obesity, and genetic mutations in BRCA1/BRCA2 genes, alongside elevated steroid hormones (estrogen and progesterone) and oxidative stress markers such as inducible nitric oxide synthase and superoxide dismutase. Cataract development similarly involves oxidative damage characterized by increased reactive oxygen species and lipid peroxidation with reduced antioxidant capacity, processes also implicated in ovarian cancer pathogenesis. Metabolic comorbidities including type-2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and ischemic heart disease are significantly more prevalent in the ovarian cancer group and independently promote cataract formation through hyperglycemic and oxidative mechanisms. The antioxidant gene NRF2 shows promise as a prognostic marker for ovarian cancer outcomes, while current therapeutic approaches include surgical resection, conventional chemotherapy, and newer agents like bevacizumab. These findings suggest that shared oxidative stress pathways underlie the association between ovarian cancer and cataracts, highlighting potential opportunities for antioxidant-based preventive or adjunctive therapeutic strategies.

title: Endometrioid adenofibroma of ovary – a literature review
journal: Romanian Journal of Morphology and Embryology
summary:
# Summary

Endometrioid adenofibroma represents a rare benign ovarian tumor accounting for approximately 1% of epithelial ovarian neoplasms, characterized histologically by glandular patterns and a substantial fibrous stromal component. While ovarian neoplasms generally carry poor prognosis due to asymptomatic progression and late-stage diagnosis, endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers present a distinct clinical picture, occurring at younger ages and earlier stages, with endometriosis increasing ovarian cancer risk threefold. The pathogenesis of ovarian tumors involves multiple mechanisms including continuous ovulatory cycles causing epithelial damage, genetic mutations (BRCA1/BRCA2, ARID1A, PIK3CA, KRAS, TP53), and pelvic inflammation, while protective factors include ovulation suppression via oral contraceptives and lifestyle modifications such as vegetable consumption. For early-stage tumors including adenofibromas, laparoscopic surgical approaches are preferred to preserve fertility in younger patients, with immunohistochemistry providing definitive diagnostic differentiation between benign adenofibromas and borderline endometrioid tumors. Age significantly influences ovarian cancer presentation and prognosis, with older patients experiencing reduced survival rates and higher incidence of aggressive high-grade serous carcinomas, whereas clear cell and endometrioid subtypes predominate in younger populations.

title: Trends in the Incidence of Ovarian Cancer Among Premenopausal and Postmenopausal Women in the United States, 2001 to 2021
summary:
# Summary

This epidemiological analysis reveals divergent trends in ovarian cancer incidence between premenopausal and postmenopausal women in the United States from 2001 to 2021, driven by distinct metabolic and hormonal risk factors. Among premenopausal women, rising rates of obesity, diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and nulliparity—alongside decreased oral contraceptive use—have contributed to increased early-onset ovarian cancer incidence. Conversely, overall ovarian cancer incidence has declined, largely attributable to reduced postmenopausal hormone replacement therapy use following the Women's Health Initiative trial, which demonstrated associations between HRT and cardiovascular disease, dementia, and breast cancer. Endometriosis emerges as a notable risk factor specifically for clear cell carcinoma of the ovary, while emerging biomarkers such as cell-free DNA methylation analysis and cancer antigen-125 show promise for improving early detection. The persistently high mortality rate remains linked to the asymptomatic growth pattern of ovarian tumors and delayed symptom onset, underscoring the critical need for improved screening strategies and biomarker development.

title: Ovarian Cancer: Multi-Omics Data Integration
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics integration study identifies critical biomarkers and molecular subtypes for ovarian cancer, with particular relevance to endometriosis-associated cases. BRCA1/2 and TP53 mutations emerge as major risk factors, while high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is characterized by significant genomic instability and dysregulated immune pathways. Multi-omics approaches integrating DNA methylation, gene expression, copy number variation, and RNA-Seq data successfully identify novel cancer subtypes, including a chemoresistant subgroup associated with BRCA2 methylation and homologous recombination deficiency, and a stromal-rich subtype linked to extracellular matrix remodeling. For clinical applications, serum biomarker panels such as ROMA (HE4/CA125) and OVA1 (B2M, APOA1, TTR, TF) effectively assess malignancy risk in adnexal masses. Emerging diagnostic modalities including liquid biopsy, microRNA profiling, and epigenetic markers show promise for early detection, while next-generation sequencing techniques (RNA-Seq, ATAC-Seq, ChIP-Seq) coupled with bioinformatics tools represent the primary discovery platforms for identifying novel biomarkers in ovarian cancer research.

title: Clinical utility of various liquid biopsy samples for the early detection of ovarian cancer: a comprehensive review
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines liquid biopsy as a non-invasive approach for early ovarian cancer detection, demonstrating superior clinical utility compared to invasive tissue biopsies, particularly given that early-stage disease carries a 92% five-year survival rate compared to only 29% for late-stage disease. Multiple biomarker categories show promise, including circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) carrying TP53 mutations at high detection rates (75-100%) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer, cell-free DNA elevation, and DNA methylation patterns in genes such as RASSF1A, BRCA1, and OPCML. MicroRNA profiling from extracellular vesicles—particularly the miR-200 family, miR-21, miR-100, and others—demonstrates strong diagnostic potential, with combined signatures like sEVmiR-EOC and OCEM achieving area under curve values above 0.95. Advanced technologies including next-generation sequencing, digital PCR, and machine learning algorithms such as MethylBERT and neural network models enable integration of multiple genomic features (copy number variations, methylation patterns, and miRNA expression) to achieve sensitivity and specificity rates exceeding 90%. Circulating tumor cells detected through optimized multi-marker approaches (EpCAM, MUC1, WT1) and cell-free DNA methylation platforms (OvaPrint™, DrCf10CNV) provide additional complementary tools for cancer stratification and early detection.

title: Enhancing ovarian cancer prognosis with an artificial intelligence-derived model: Multi-omics integration and therapeutic implications
summary: This study describes development of the AIDPI artificial intelligence-derived prognostic model for ovarian cancer that integrates multi-omics data to predict patient outcomes and guide treatment decisions. The AIDPI model demonstrates superior accuracy in prognostication compared to existing approaches and correlates strongly with clinical treatment outcomes, including chemotherapy responsiveness, which was subsequently incorporated into a predictive nomogram. Functional analysis revealed that AIDPI genes regulate critical oncogenic processes including tumor immune infiltration and cell cycle control. The study identified MFAP4 as a key therapeutic target, as this gene is highly expressed in metastatic lesions and associated with poor prognosis; experimental knockdown of MFAP4 substantially reduces ovarian cancer cell metastasis in both in vitro and in vivo models. The findings suggest that AI-integrated multi-omics approaches can improve ovarian cancer prognostication while identifying actionable molecular targets like MFAP4 for precision therapeutic intervention, with potential applications in both early detection and treatment stratification.

title: Primary Malignant Mesothelioma of the Peritoneum Mistaken for Peritoneal Tuberculosis due to Elevated Cancer Antigen 125
summary: This case report illustrates the diagnostic challenge of distinguishing peritoneal malignant mesothelioma from peritoneal tuberculosis, both of which present with elevated serum CA 125 levels—a tumor marker traditionally associated with gynecologic malignancies and endometriosis. CA 125 is non-specific and can be substantially elevated in peritoneal tuberculosis (10- to 20-fold higher than baseline), peritoneal carcinomatosis, and non-malignant conditions including peritonitis, pelvic inflammatory disease, autoimmune diseases, and pancreatitis, complicating differential diagnosis. Mesothelin emerged as a more specific biomarker for peritoneal mesothelioma, with high-affinity binding to CA 125 in mesothelioma tissues. Immunohistochemical analysis proved essential for diagnosis, with mesothelioma specimens expressing cytokeratin, CK 7, and calretinin while remaining negative for CK 20 and CEA. The case emphasizes that ascitic fluid analysis and interferon-gamma release assays are crucial adjuncts to serum biomarkers for reliably differentiating peritoneal tuberculosis from malignant mesothelioma, particularly when asbestos exposure history is available.

title: A stage IV high-grade serous ovarian cancer patient carrying ERBB2 amplification and ERBB2 overexpression benefits from late-line pyrotinib treatment: A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates that ERBB2 amplification and overexpression occur in a significant proportion of high-grade serous ovarian cancers and drive chemotherapy resistance through activation of the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathways. A stage IV metastatic high-grade serous ovarian cancer patient who progressed through platinum-based chemotherapy and antiangiogenic therapies achieved clinical benefit from the irreversible pan-ErbB receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor pyrotinib, reporting reduced pain and improved quality of life. The patient's metastatic peritoneal lesions demonstrated ERBB2 overexpression and high PD-L1 expression, supporting pyrotinib as a viable late-line treatment option for ERBB2-positive metastatic high-grade serous ovarian cancer. This case provides preliminary clinical evidence that direct ERBB2-targeted therapy with pyrotinib may offer an alternative strategy for patients who have exhausted conventional chemotherapy and antivascular approaches, particularly when ERBB2 amplification is confirmed through molecular profiling.

title: Plasma WFDC2 (HE4) as a Predictive Biomarker for Clinical Outcomes in Cancer Patients Receiving Anti-PD-1 Therapy: A Pilot Study
summary: This pilot study evaluated plasma WFDC2 (human epididymis protein 4, HE4) as a predictive biomarker for clinical outcomes in cancer patients receiving anti-PD-1 immunotherapy. Among 21 patients with non-small cell lung cancer, gastric cancer, or bladder cancer treated with nivolumab or pembrolizumab, greater increases in circulating WFDC2 levels during treatment were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression-free survival, and correlated strongly with tumor progression. WFDC2 demonstrated superior predictive performance compared to soluble PD-L1 and PD-1, with the combination of WFDC2 and sPD-L1 yielding the highest diagnostic accuracy. The findings suggest that WFDC2 upregulation during immunotherapy may reflect activation of resistance pathways independent of PD-1/PD-L1 blockade, potentially through mechanisms involving extracellular matrix remodeling, immune suppression, and immune escape. These results support WFDC2 as a dynamic biomarker for monitoring treatment response and predicting immunotherapy resistance in cancer patients.

title: The Future of Tumor Markers: Advancing Early Malignancy Detection Through Omics Technologies, Continuous Monitoring, and Personalized Reference Intervals
summary:
# Summary

This review examines tumor markers as critical tools for early malignancy detection across multiple cancer types, emphasizing the importance of multimarker panels over individual biomarkers. While traditional markers such as AFP for hepatocellular carcinoma, CA 125 for ovarian cancer, and PSA for prostate cancer remain clinically important, combining multiple markers—such as AFP with AFP-L3 and DCP for liver cancer, or CA 125 with HE4 for ovarian cancer—significantly enhances diagnostic sensitivity and specificity. The article highlights emerging technologies, including circulating tumor cells (CTCs) and omics-based approaches, as promising strategies for improving early detection and reducing false positives that currently plague single-marker screening. Importantly, the authors stress that personalized approaches incorporating genetic analysis and continuous monitoring are essential for advancing cancer diagnostics, as most conventional markers lack sufficient specificity and can be elevated in benign conditions. Overall, the integration of novel tumor-specific biomarkers with advanced algorithms and omics technologies represents the future direction for developing more accurate, non-invasive early detection strategies across diverse malignancies.

title: Therapeutic Potential of Glucose Oxidase-Loaded Biogenic Mesoporous Silica Nanoparticles in Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates glucose oxidase (GOx) immobilized on biogenic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) as a therapeutic strategy for ovarian cancer, which shows epidemiological association with endometriosis. The GOx-loaded nanoformulation operates through a starvation therapy mechanism, wherein the enzyme catalyzes glucose oxidation to generate hydrogen peroxide, which subsequently increases reactive oxygen species and induces tumor cell death. The nanoformulation significantly downregulates CTNNB1, an oncogene overexpressed in ovarian cancer, and achieves 18.06% cell viability in SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells at 200 µg/mL, demonstrating superior cytotoxic potency compared to free GOx. Although enzyme immobilization on MSNs reduced GOx enzymatic activity by approximately 98% due to substrate diffusion limitations, the nanoparticle delivery system enhanced overall therapeutic efficacy, maintained high biocompatibility with normal cells, and improved drug stability while reducing systemic toxicity. These findings support the therapeutic potential of GOx-loaded MSNs as a targeted treatment for ovarian cancer, particularly when combined with other therapeutic agents like curcumin to synergistically promote apoptosis.

title: Unveiling the Diagnostic Significance of Calprotectin in Serous Ovarian Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates calprotectin as a diagnostic biomarker for serous ovarian cancer, the most common epithelial ovarian cancer subtype, examining its utility alongside the established marker CA-125. Calprotectin, a protein released from activated neutrophils, is significantly elevated in ovarian cancer patients, particularly those with advanced disease, and showed higher concentrations compared to healthy controls. The inflammatory basis of ovarian cancer development involves chronic inflammatory processes triggered by ovulation, which activates proinflammatory cascades that promote oxidative stress, DNA damage, and upregulation of chemical mediators driving tumor initiation and progression. Calprotectin functions through multiple mechanisms including activation of NADPH oxidase, apoptotic pathway induction, and inhibition of metalloproteinases that facilitate angiogenesis and metastatic growth. When combined with CA-125, calprotectin enhanced the detection range for ovarian cancer, with both biomarkers showing strong statistical correlations and associations with tumor burden and disease stage. These findings suggest calprotectin serves as a complementary inflammatory marker that reflects the tumor-associated inflammatory microenvironment, potentially improving diagnostic accuracy and risk stratification in ovarian cancer patients.

title: Unilateral hyperreactio luteinalis of the ovary during pregnancy mimics ovarian malignancy: A case report
summary: Hyperreactio luteinalis is a rare benign ovarian condition characterized by bilateral ovarian enlargement and multiple thin-walled luteal cysts with a characteristic "spoke wheel" sign on ultrasound, commonly associated with multiple gestations and assisted reproductive technology involving gonadotropin stimulation. The condition results from dual stimulation by progesterone and human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), which promote proliferation and cystic dilation of luteal tissue. A significant clinical challenge is that hyperreactio luteinalis can mimic ovarian malignancy, presenting with elevated tumor markers including CA125, large unilateral masses, and ascites, leading to potential misdiagnosis. CA125 elevation is not specific to malignancy, as it can be elevated during normal pregnancy, menstrual cycles, endometriosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease, further complicating diagnostic accuracy. The condition typically resolves spontaneously in later stages of pregnancy, making conservative management the primary treatment approach, though surgery may be necessary for complications such as torsion, rupture, or persistent suspicion of malignancy. Ultrasonography and magnetic resonance imaging are essential imaging modalities for distinguishing this benign entity from true ovarian cancer in pregnant patients.

title: Knowledge and application of sonographic scoring models for ovarian cancer management among gynecologists in Saudi Arabia: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This study assessed knowledge and application of sonographic scoring models for ovarian cancer among Saudi Arabian gynecologists, revealing a significant gap between awareness and clinical implementation. While CA125 is strongly associated with ovarian cancer diagnosis, its elevation also occurs in benign conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease, limiting its diagnostic specificity. The Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) and the ADNEX model—both incorporating ultrasound findings, CA125 levels, and patient demographics—represent evidence-based frameworks that improve preoperative diagnostic accuracy and reduce unnecessary surgeries. However, only 46% of gynecologists regularly used the RMI and 32% used the ADNEX model despite 72% familiarity with RMI and 58% awareness of ADNEX, with barriers including insufficient training and limited access to diagnostic tools. Gynecologists with over 10 years of experience and those practicing in specialized or teaching hospitals demonstrated significantly higher usage rates and knowledge levels. The ADNEX model showed superior diagnostic accuracy (especially when combined with CA125) compared to the RMI, suggesting that targeted training and institutional support could enhance application of these validated scoring systems for improved ovarian cancer management.

title: Association between ERCC2 Lys751Gln, Asp312Asn, and Arg156Arg polymorphisms and gynecological cancer susceptibility: a meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that ERCC2 gene polymorphisms serve as important genetic markers for gynecological cancer susceptibility, with the Lys751Gln variant showing the strongest association with increased risk of gynecological neoplasms, particularly ovarian cancer (OR 1.33, 95% CI 1.06-1.60). ERCC2 is a critical component of the nucleotide excision repair pathway, which removes DNA damage induced by estrogen and other carcinogenic agents; impairment of this repair system through genetic variation can lead to genomic instability and tumorigenesis. In contrast, the Asp312Asn polymorphism showed no increased risk for gynecological tumors and even demonstrated protective effects against cervical cancer in Asian populations, while the Arg156Arg silent mutation exhibited no association with gynecological tumor susceptibility. Beyond cancer predisposition, ERCC2 polymorphisms also influence chemotherapy outcomes, as the Lys751Gln variant was associated with reduced mortality in ovarian cancer patients receiving platinum-based chemotherapy, likely because this polymorphism affects DNA repair capacity and responsiveness to cisplatin-induced damage. The Lys751Gln polymorphism shows elevated prevalence and increased risk across both Caucasian and African populations, suggesting consistent biological effects across diverse genetic backgrounds. These findings establish ERCC2 polymorphisms as potential biomarkers for identifying women at heightened gynecological cancer risk and predicting treatment response.

title: Integrative single-cell and exosomal multi-omics uncovers SCNN1A and EFNA1 as non-invasive biomarkers and drivers of ovarian cancer metastasis
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study integrating single-cell and exosomal transcriptomics identifies SCNN1A and EFNA1 as critical drivers and non-invasive biomarkers of ovarian cancer metastasis. Both genes are significantly upregulated in primary tumor tissues, metastatic foci, and circulating plasma exosomes, and their high expression correlates with stem-like differentiation states, immune evasion, and enhanced cell adhesion properties that facilitate metastatic dissemination. Machine learning analysis of exosomal transcriptomics, particularly an Adaboost model, achieved exceptional diagnostic performance (AUC 0.955) for ovarian cancer detection, substantially outperforming the conventional CA125 marker which shows poor sensitivity in early-stage disease and lacks specificity due to elevation in benign conditions like endometriosis. Single-cell transcriptomics revealed extensive intercellular communication between tumor cells and the microenvironment, with metastatic tumor cells showing distinct signatures in brain metastases and pleural effusions that involve fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and immune populations. These findings establish SCNN1A and EFNA1 as promising candidates for non-invasive biomarker-driven detection and prognostication of ovarian cancer metastasis, while highlighting exosomes as critical mediators of intercellular communication in the tumor microenvironment.

title: Development and Validation of Predictive Risk Scores for Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma: A Penalized Regression Model
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) represents a distinct ovarian cancer subtype characterized by platinum chemotherapy resistance, frequent ARID1A mutations (unlike TP53-mutated high-grade serous ovarian cancer), and poor outcomes particularly in advanced stages, with documented occult metastasis risk even in early-stage disease. This study developed and validated three machine-learning-derived risk scores for OCCC recurrence prediction, incorporating preoperative blood parameters (cholesterol, BUN, WBC, platelets, neutrophils, CA125, liver enzymes) and postoperative surgical factors (completeness of cytoreduction, ascites characteristics, disease stage).
