Risk Score 3, which incorporated surgical completeness and ascites cytology alongside blood markers, demonstrated superior external validation performance for identifying high-risk recurrence patients compared to clinician-selected variables. The penalized regression approach and machine-learning-based variable selection outperformed traditional clinicopathological prognostication methods, offering improved stratification for OCCC patients who typically present at younger ages and exhibit prognostic heterogeneity within stage I disease.

title: Adenomyosis as a prognostic factor in ovarian cancer: a retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study reveals a paradoxical relationship between adenomyosis and ovarian cancer prognosis, wherein adenomyosis coexistence is associated with more favorable tumor characteristics and improved survival outcomes. Patients with adenomyosis demonstrated earlier-stage disease, smaller tumors, and lower histological grades compared to those without adenomyosis, with a strong association to the endometrioid subtype (OR = 2.89). Three-year disease-free survival was significantly better in the adenomyosis group (79.2% versus 73.9%), and notably, high-grade tumors with adenomyosis showed improved overall survival (71% versus 57%). The underlying mechanism likely involves shared molecular pathways including estrogen-mediated signaling, ARID1A mutations, and PI3K/AKT pathway activation, with adenomyosis potentially modulating the tumor microenvironment through inflammatory molecules, sex steroid hormone receptors, and growth factors. Independent predictors of improved survival included negative lymphovascular space invasion, absence of para-aortic lymph node metastasis, and absence of distant metastasis, suggesting that adenomyosis may exert a protective effect particularly in high-grade ovarian malignancies.

title: Insights into the role of MSLN-positive circulating tumor cell as an auxiliary diagnostic biomarker in epithelial ovarian cancer
summary: Mesothelin (MSLN) is highly overexpressed in epithelial ovarian cancer compared to normal ovarian tissues and is associated with aggressive tumor behaviors including enhanced cell adhesion, invasion, drug resistance, and peritoneal metastasis. MSLN-positive circulating tumor cells (MSLN(+)CTCs) demonstrate diagnostic utility as an auxiliary biomarker, effectively differentiating malignant ovarian lesions from benign ones with 66.67% sensitivity and high specificity. When MSLN(+)CTCs are combined with the established marker CA125, diagnostic performance substantially improves to 92.86% sensitivity and 94.74% specificity, addressing a critical clinical need since CA125 and HE4 alone suffer from false positives in benign gynecological conditions such as endometriosis and adenomyosis. The detection of MSLN(+)CTCs can be achieved using EpCAM-targeting magnetic nanoparticles (Pep@MNPs), providing a practical platform for CTC capture and analysis. These findings establish MSLN-positive circulating tumor cells as a promising supplementary diagnostic tool that could improve the accuracy of ovarian cancer detection and help distinguish it from benign conditions that commonly confound existing serum biomarkers.

title: A Novel Serum-Based Bioassay for Quantification of Cancer-Associated Transformation Activity: A Case–Control and Animal Study
summary:
# Summary

This study presents a novel serum-based bioassay using TY cells—an immortalized fallopian tube epithelial cell line expressing HPV16 E6/E7 oncoproteins—to quantify cancer-associated transformation activity through anchorage-independent growth (AIG). The assay exploits the ability of cancer serum to activate HGF/c-MET and IGF/IGF-1R signaling pathways, which drive AIG colony formation in TY cells at optimal passage numbers (91–115). In both human case-control studies and an ID8 mouse ovarian cancer model, the TY-AIG assay achieved 75% sensitivity and 81% specificity for cancer detection, significantly outperforming conventional biomarkers like CA125 and HE4, with TY-AIG colony counts showing remarkable correlation with tumor burden (r = 0.95). This functional bioassay directly measures malignant potential rather than static protein levels, offering improved specificity and sensitivity for ovarian cancer detection and potentially broader application across cancer types, as demonstrated by comparable responsiveness to breast cancer sera.

title: Unlocking the Potential of Liquid Biopsy: A Paradigm Shift in Endometrial Cancer Care
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial cancer shows rising incidence and remains a significant clinical challenge, with certain patients experiencing recurrence and distant metastasis despite early-stage diagnosis and treatment. Liquid biopsy has emerged as a non-invasive, safe, and effective paradigm for cancer diagnosis, treatment guidance, and real-time monitoring, offering advantages over traditional tissue biopsies through its repeatability and ability to capture tumor molecular heterogeneity. The field employs diverse biomarkers including circulating tumor cells (identified via EpCAM and cytokeratin expression), circulating tumor DNA (detected through quantitative PCR, droplet digital PCR, and next-generation sequencing), and circulating tumor RNA (including microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs), with emerging focus on epigenetic modifications like ctDNA methylation patterns. Extracellular vesicles and exosomes play critical roles throughout cancer progression by facilitating intercellular communication and promoting tumor transformation, angiogenesis, invasion, metastasis, and drug resistance through epithelial-mesenchymal transition mechanisms. Advanced technologies including mass spectrometry, metabolomics, and flow cytometry enable comprehensive biomarker detection and immunophenotyping, while molecular profiling has become increasingly valuable for guiding treatment decisions and precision medicine approaches. Collectively, liquid biopsy represents a paradigm shift enabling early detection, prognosis assessment, and personalized management of endometrial cancer across all clinical stages.

title: Anatomic subtype-specific causal effects of endometriosis on ovarian cancer: a two-sample Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

Using Mendelian randomization analysis, this study demonstrates that endometriosis has subtype-specific causal effects on ovarian cancer risk, with clear cell carcinoma showing the strongest association (1.87-fold increased risk), followed by endometrioid (1.48-fold) and high-grade serous (1.12-fold) subtypes. Different anatomical locations of endometriosis confer distinct malignant potentials: ovarian endometriosis particularly elevates clear cell and endometrioid risks, pelvic peritoneal lesions show highest risk for clear cell carcinoma, and deep infiltrating endometriosis increases high-grade serous and endometrioid risks. The molecular basis involves multiple genetic alterations in progressive endometriotic lesions, including ARID1A loss, PIK3CA mutations, TP53 modifications, and COX-2/PTEN dysregulation, alongside oxidative stress, inflammation, and immune deregulation in the tumor microenvironment. Notably, endometriosis shows no causal relationship with low-grade serous or mucinous ovarian cancers, suggesting that malignant transformation follows specific pathways dependent on endometriotic subtype and anatomical location.

title: Emerging biomarkers for early cancer detection and diagnosis: challenges, innovations, and clinical perspectives
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews emerging biomarkers for early cancer detection, emphasizing that early diagnosis significantly improves patient outcomes and survival rates, with median overall survival increasing from 14 months to 38 months when cancer is detected at early stages. Cancer arises through accumulation of molecular alterations in the genome caused by genetic mutations in proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes, as well as epigenetic modifications and environmental exposures, with certain viruses such as human papillomavirus and hepatitis C virus associated with increased cancer risk. The article identifies multiple cancer-specific biomarkers detectable in blood, saliva, urine, and tissue—including CA 125 and HCG-β for ovarian cancer, PSA for prostate cancer, EGFR for lung cancer, and estrogen/progesterone receptors and HER2 for breast cancer classification—that enable diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment monitoring. Elevated biomarker levels correlate with treatment failure and disease recurrence, making them critical tools for stratifying patients into molecular subtypes and guiding therapeutic decisions. Screening regimens using these biomarkers have successfully reduced advanced-stage cancer diagnoses, demonstrating their clinical value in improving detection and management across multiple cancer types.

title: Future theranostic strategies: emerging ovarian cancer biomarkers to bridge the gap between diagnosis and treatment
summary: Epithelial ovarian cancer shows strong epidemiological and molecular associations with endometriosis, particularly in Type I cancers including endometrioid and clear cell subtypes. Current diagnostic approaches rely on biomarkers such as MUC16 and HE4, which achieve improved sensitivity when combined in multiplex assays with proteins like APOA1, TTR, and B2M, though MUC16's elevation in benign conditions including endometriosis limits its specificity. Emerging biomarkers including FOLR1, MSLN, nectin-2, and CD24 show promise for early detection and patient stratification, with FOLR1 already enabling targeted therapy through the companion diagnostic for mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx. Angiogenic pathways, particularly VEGF signaling and CHI3L1-mediated extracellular matrix degradation, represent critical mechanisms in ovarian cancer progression, with CHI3L1 inhibition reducing angiogenesis, tumor development, and metastasis in preclinical models. Additional candidate biomarkers including PSN, nectin-4, SLC34A2, and PAX8 provide complementary information on tumor biology, cell adhesion, metabolic adaptation, and transcriptional regulation. These integrated biomarker panels and mechanistic insights are advancing theranostic strategies to bridge diagnosis and personalized treatment selection in ovarian cancer.

title: Circulating proteins and metabolites panel for noninvasive preoperative diagnosis of epithelial ovarian cancer
summary: Epithelial ovarian cancer exhibits characteristic dysregulation of metabolic pathways, particularly abnormalities in sphingolipid metabolism, steroid hormone biosynthesis, and tryptophan metabolism coupled with alterations in PI3K-Akt signaling. The study identified a noninvasive diagnostic panel comprising four proteins (LRG1, ITIH3, PDIA4, and PON1) and three metabolites (kynurenine, indole, and 3-hydroxybutyrate) that effectively distinguish ovarian cancer from benign conditions, addressing limitations of conventional markers like CA-125, which shows poor early-stage detection and generates false positives in endometriosis. Mechanistically, tryptophan 2,3-dioxygenase (TDO2) is upregulated in cancer-associated fibroblasts, driving elevated kynurenine levels that serve as a diagnostic indicator. Complementary transcriptomic validation using TCGA and single-cell RNA sequencing confirmed TDO2 enrichment in ovarian cancer tissues and stromal fibroblasts. Additional proteomic studies have identified biomarker panels for high-grade serous tumors (EEF1G, MSLN, BCAM, TAGLN2) and early-stage detection (TTR, hemoglobin, ApoAI, transferrin combined with CA-125). This integrated proteomic-metabolomic approach offers improved sensitivity and specificity for preoperative diagnosis while illuminating the metabolic reprogramming underlying ovarian cancer pathogenesis.

title: Pelvic Malignant Tumor with Liver Metastasis is Actually Conventional Leiomyoma with Abdominal Metastasis
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with a pelvic mass and elevated liver imaging initially suspected to be metastatic ovarian cancer, but ultimately diagnosed as parasitic leiomyoma—a benign metastatic fibroid. Uterine leiomyomas typically present with menorrhagia, pelvic pressure, and chronic pain, with growth driven by estrogen and progesterone signaling and associated with MED12 mutations. Ectopic leiomyomas can arise through iatrogenic dissemination and intravascular spread following surgical procedures such as myomectomy or laparoscopic surgery, leading to parasitic implants that are frequently misdiagnosed as malignant tumors with metastasis. The case emphasizes that elevated CA125 and imaging findings commonly used to screen for ovarian cancer lack specificity and can occur with benign leiomyoma metastasis, necessitating intraoperative frozen section examination to confirm diagnosis and prevent unnecessary aggressive treatment. This diagnostic challenge highlights the importance of considering benign tumor implantation in the differential diagnosis of pelvic tumors presenting with abdominal findings.

title: TCR and BCR repertoire analysis reveals distinct signatures between benign and malignant ovarian tumors
summary: Malignant ovarian tumors exhibit a distinctive immunological signature characterized by reduced T cell receptor (TCR) and B cell receptor (BCR) diversity, richness, and clonotype numbers compared to benign tumors, despite paradoxically displaying greater immune repertoire heterogeneity. This immunological imbalance, marked by clonal expansion of dominant T and B cell populations, correlates with reduced immune protection and poorer clinical outcomes, including higher mortality risk and diminished survival rates. The study developed a machine learning-based early screening model utilizing 16 TCR V-J genes and 11 BCR V-J genes to distinguish benign from malignant ovarian tumors, with TCR demonstrating superior spatial distribution patterns and stronger association with malignant transformation. Higher TCR diversity in peripheral blood is associated with improved survival and effective anti-tumor responses, whereas reduced TCR diversity predicts poorer prognosis in ovarian cancer patients. These findings underscore the critical role of adaptive immune repertoire analysis as a biomarker for identifying malignancy and could enable earlier detection of ovarian cancer, improving outcomes for a disease that currently carries only a 47% five-year survival rate when diagnosed at advanced stages.

title: A Comparison of the Flow Cytometric Analysis Results of Benign and Malignant Serous Tumors of the Ovary
summary:
# Summary

This study used flow cytometric analysis to distinguish benign serous cystadenomas from malignant serous cystadenocarcinomas of the ovary by comparing cell cycle characteristics and apoptotic markers. Malignant tumors displayed significantly elevated S phase cell ratios, proliferative indices, aneuploidy cell ratios, and annexin V apoptotic indices compared to benign tumors, with an annexin V apoptotic index cutoff of 27.65% achieving 90% sensitivity for cancer detection. These proliferative and chromosomal abnormality markers correlated strongly with each other, indicating coordinated dysregulation during malignant transformation. The findings support a model where serous tumors progress from benign cystadenomas through borderline lesions to high-grade carcinomas, with endometriosis identified as a potential risk factor for ovarian cancer development. Flow cytometric detection of annexin V expression offers a rapid, cost-effective diagnostic approach for identifying malignant serous tumors, though surgery and chemotherapy remain the primary treatments with modest 5-year survival rates for advanced disease.

title: Decoding the Link Between Talcum Powder and Ovarian Cancer: A Comprehensive Review
summary:
# Summary

Epidemiological evidence demonstrates a 30-32% increased risk of ovarian cancer associated with genital talc powder use, with talc particles capable of migrating from the genital tract to pelvic tissues including lymph nodes, cervix, fallopian tubes, and uterine corpus. The pathogenic mechanism involves talc-induced chronic inflammation of the reproductive tract, which subsequently triggers oxidative stress, DNA damage, and aberrant cell proliferation that collectively facilitate carcinogenesis. While both endometrioid and clear cell ovarian cancer subtypes are notably associated with endometriosis, talc exposure appears particularly linked to high-grade serous ovarian cancer development, with disparate risk elevation observed across racial groups—36% in White women and 41% in African American women. Additional risk factors including nulliparity, BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, hormone replacement therapy, and certain dietary patterns contribute to ovarian cancer susceptibility independent of talc exposure. This comprehensive analysis establishes the biological plausibility for talc-mediated ovarian carcinogenesis while highlighting the multifactorial nature of ovarian cancer development and the need for continued surveillance in populations with elevated feminine hygiene product usage.

title: Redefining Risk,
summary:
# Summary

This article examines hereditary ovarian cancer risk through the lens of germline mutations in DNA repair genes, particularly BRCA1 and BRCA2, which confer 35–70% and 10–30% lifetime ovarian cancer risks respectively. BRCA1 and BRCA2 encode tumor suppressors essential for homologous recombination-mediated DNA repair; their dysfunction promotes genomic instability and greatly elevates cancer susceptibility. Beyond BRCA1/2, additional high-risk mutations including ATM, BRIP1, RAD51C, RAD51D, and PALB2 contribute to hereditary ovarian cancer development, with RAD51C and PALB2 mutations specifically associated with high-grade serous carcinoma. The therapeutic landscape has been revolutionized by PARP inhibitors (olaparib, niraparib, rucaparib), which exploit synthetic lethality by selectively targeting homologous recombination-deficient tumors and significantly improving progression-free survival in BRCA mutation carriers. Beyond genetic interventions, modifiable risk factors including nulliparity, early menarche, and late menopause increase ovarian cancer susceptibility by extending ovulatory cycles, while protective factors such as pregnancy, breastfeeding, long-term oral contraceptive use (≥5 years), and risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy reduce incidence by 30–50% or more.

title: Carbohydrate antigen 125 and clinical outcomes in heart failure: systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), a glycoprotein produced by serous epithelium, serves as a significant prognostic biomarker in heart failure patients across both acute and chronic presentations. Elevated CA125 levels are strongly associated with volume overload, congestion parameters, and invasively measured filling pressures, correlating closely with established heart failure biomarkers such as NT-proBNP. The marker independently predicts adverse outcomes including mortality, hospitalization, and cardiovascular death, with particularly strong associations in chronic heart failure (hazard ratio 2.52) and acute heart failure (hazard ratio 1.88), and shows greater predictive value when combined with NT-proBNP. Clinical utility extends to disease severity assessment, monitoring treatment response, and risk stratification, with CA125 levels rising in patients with pleural or pericardial effusion, higher NYHA functional classes, and advanced structural changes such as right ventricular dilatation. Serial CA125 measurements from admission to discharge provide prognostic information, with declining levels associated with better outcomes, enabling personalized monitoring strategies and potentially reducing intensive care requirements for patients with low admission values.

title: CT and MRI in Advanced Ovarian Cancer: Advances in Imaging Techniques
summary:
# Summary

Advanced ovarian cancer presents a complex diagnostic and management challenge, with peritoneal metastases being a hallmark feature present in most patients at diagnosis. While contrast-enhanced CT remains the gold standard for staging and assessing tumor extent, it significantly underestimates small-volume peritoneal disease, particularly in gastrointestinal and mesenteric regions. Multiparametric MRI, enhanced by diffusion-weighted imaging and whole-body protocols, demonstrates superior sensitivity for detecting small peritoneal metastases and predicting suboptimal cytoreduction with 94% sensitivity, outperforming CT in categorizing tumor volume (91% vs. 50%). FDG-PET-CT further complements these modalities by outperforming CT in identifying malignant lymph nodes and detecting ovarian cancer recurrence, which represents a critical prognostic concern as lymph node involvement correlates with advanced disease and poorer outcomes. Complete macroscopic tumor clearance through cytoreductive surgery combined with platinum-based chemotherapy remains the most crucial prognostic factor for survival, making accurate preoperative imaging essential for surgical planning. Emerging technologies including CT-based radiomics models, machine-learning algorithms, and standardized reporting lexicons show promise in predicting resectability, metastatic risk, and survival outcomes, potentially enabling more personalized treatment approaches.

title: A Wearable In‐Pad Diagnostic for the Detection of Disease Biomarkers in Menstruation Blood
summary: This study describes MenstruAI, a wearable in-pad diagnostic device designed to detect disease biomarkers from menstrual blood, offering a non-invasive alternative to venous blood sampling. The device performs semi-quantitative detection of three key biomarkers: cancer antigen 125 (CA-125), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), and C-reactive protein (CRP), which are associated with endometriosis, cancer, and inflammation respectively. Menstrual blood is particularly valuable for biomarker detection because it exhibits strong correlation with venous blood biomarker levels while containing 385 unique proteins and lower concentrations of interfering coagulation factors, hemoglobin, and hematocrit. The technical design incorporates a blood separation membrane to filter red blood cells and cellular debris, capillary pressure-driven microfluidics for fluid movement, and antibody-functionalized gold nanoparticles that interact with target proteins for detection. Notably, CA-125 levels are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients (>100 U mL−1) and in 82% of ovarian cancer patients, making it a clinically relevant biomarker for distinguishing between benign endometriosis and malignant gynecological cancers. This wearable platform enables convenient, accessible monitoring of women's health status through a previously underutilized biofluid source.

title: Utilizing Serum-Derived Lipidomics with Protein Biomarkers and Machine Learning for Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer in the Symptomatic Population
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that a multiomics approach combining serum lipidomic profiling with protein biomarkers substantially improves early detection of ovarian cancer in symptomatic populations. The researchers identified significant alterations in multiple lipid classes in ovarian cancer patients, including elevated gangliosides (GD2, GD3), phospholipids, sphingomyelins, and ceramides, alongside reduced triacylglycerols and phosphatidylethanolamines. Traditional protein biomarkers such as CA125, HE4, FOLR1, and MUC1 were also elevated in ovarian cancer, though CA125 elevation in benign conditions like endometriosis creates diagnostic challenges. Machine learning integration of lipid and protein data achieved 92% diagnostic accuracy, significantly outperforming lipid-only or protein-only models, enabling detection of early-stage ovarian cancer in complex clinical controls including patients with benign adnexal masses and gastrointestinal disorders. These findings highlight the importance of early diagnosis, as patients diagnosed at early stages achieve over 90% survival rates compared to less than 30% for late-stage presentation, underscoring the clinical potential of this multiomic biomarker panel for reducing diagnostic delay.

title: Cancer of the ovary, fallopian tube, and peritoneum: 2025 update
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: This 2025 update on ovarian, fallopian tube, and peritoneal cancers elucidates distinct molecular signatures stratifying these tumors by histologic subtype and genetic predisposition. High-grade serous carcinomas, predominantly arising from TP53-mutated precursors in the fallopian tube fimbria, represent the most common malignancy and carry the poorest prognosis. Endometrioid ovarian cancers, which frequently develop within endometriotic lesions, display a contrasting mutational profile including PTEN loss, PI3K pathway activation, and CTNNB1/ARID1A/PIK3CA mutations, with TP53 involvement indicating aggressive behavior. Low-grade serous carcinomas harbor MAP kinase pathway alterations including BRAF and KRAS mutations. Hereditary ovarian cancer risk is substantially elevated by germline mutations in BRCA1/BRCA2 and homologous recombination repair genes (CHEK2, ATM, BRIP1, BARD1, PALB2, RAD51C/D, MSH6), with Lynch syndrome predisposing to early-stage endometrioid and clear cell histologies. Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy effectively reduces ovarian cancer incidence in BRCA mutation carriers, though peritoneal cancer remains a residual risk, particularly when serous tubal intraepithelial carcinomas are present.

title: Dynamics of Conventional Metabolic Indices in Relation to Endometriosis Severity: A Retrospective Analysis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder presenting with pelvic pain and infertility while substantially elevating risks for multiple pregnancy complications including pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, and placental abnormalities, with potential for malignant transformation to ovarian cancer. This retrospective analysis identified several conventional metabolic indices as independent biomarkers of endometriosis severity: fasting blood glucose and total protein were significantly elevated in severe disease, while alanine transaminase, total bilirubin, and direct bilirubin were paradoxically reduced, suggesting dysregulated hepatic glucose metabolism and impaired antioxidant protection. Serum carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA-125) and human epididymal protein 4 (HE4) emerged as significant covariates, with CA-125 particularly correlating with severe and deep infiltrating endometriosis. The inverse relationship between bilirubin levels and disease severity suggests that reduced bilirubin—an endogenous antioxidant—may indicate progression toward more aggressive phenotypes, while elevated glucose reflects altered metabolic homeostasis supporting ectopic endometrial implant survival. These findings establish conventional metabolic markers as potential diagnostic and severity-stratification tools, warranting validation as non-invasive biomarkers to supplement current imaging and serological approaches in endometriosis assessment.

title: A Review on Biomarker‐Enhanced Machine Learning for Early Diagnosis and Outcome Prediction in Ovarian Cancer Management
summary:
# Summary

This review examines biomarker-enhanced machine learning approaches for improving ovarian cancer diagnosis and prognosis. Traditional serum biomarkers including CA-125 and HE4 form the foundation of ovarian cancer detection, with combined risk assessment models like ROMA and RMI substantially improving diagnostic accuracy over single markers. Emerging biomarkers such as TTR, ApoA-I, OPN, PSN, and KLK6/KLK7 show superior sensitivity for early-stage disease detection compared to CA-125 alone, while mechanistic markers reveal critical pathways—including VEGF-mediated immune evasion via MDSCs, and the uPA/uPAR axis driving invasion and metastasis. Advanced machine learning algorithms, particularly ensemble methods like Random Forest and XGBoost alongside deep learning approaches, substantially outperform traditional statistical methods, achieving AUC values exceeding 0.90 for classification and survival prediction. Integration of multi-omics data combining genomics, proteomics, and radiomics with biomarker panels represents the frontier for personalized ovarian cancer management, enabling earlier detection and improved patient stratification for treatment outcomes.

title: Serum Levels of CA125 and HE4 as a Tool for Predicting Regional Lymph Node Metastatic Involvement in Endometrial Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study evaluated serum biomarkers CA125 and HE4 as predictive tools for lymph node metastasis (LNM) in endometrial carcinoma patients. Both biomarkers were significantly elevated in patients with LNM compared to those without, with HE4 demonstrating greater biological plausibility through its promotion of invasion, proliferation, and metastasis in aggressive endometrial tumors. Using optimized cutoff values (CA125 ≥35 IU/mL and HE4 ≥103 pmol/L), the biomarkers achieved 70% sensitivity and 92% specificity for CA125 alone, while HE4 achieved 78% sensitivity and 80% specificity. A combined prediction model incorporating both biomarkers with ultrasound-detected myometrial invasion depth provided enhanced stratification of patients into low- and high-risk LNM groups, offering a non-invasive alternative to imaging and surgical staging methods. This approach addresses the clinical need for improved preoperative lymph node assessment while reducing perioperative complications associated with comprehensive lymph node dissection.

title: Clinical Evaluation of a Multi-Omic Diagnostic Model for Early-Stage Ovarian Cancer Detection
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer diagnosis is critically hindered by late-stage detection, with the standard CA125 biomarker achieving only 67.5% sensitivity for early-stage cases and showing elevated levels in benign conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and adenomyosis. A multi-omic diagnostic model integrating over 100 gangliosides, 480 lipids and fatty acids, and multiple proteins substantially outperforms single biomarkers, achieving 98.7% sensitivity and 94% AUC for early-stage ovarian cancer detection while maintaining 94.4% sensitivity when distinguishing early-stage tumors from benign adnexal masses. The model demonstrates robust performance across ovarian cancer subtypes and stages, with lipids emerging as particularly important drivers for early-stage discrimination. Additionally, the multi-omic platform reveals distinctive molecular signatures associated with chemotherapeutic treatment, including coordinated lipid profile shifts and protein abundance changes, suggesting potential applications beyond diagnostic detection to monitoring therapeutic response and treatment efficacy.

title: Meigs syndrome was misdiagnosed as a malignant ovarian tumor: a case report
summary: Meigs syndrome, a benign condition characterized by the triad of a benign ovarian tumor (typically a fibroma or thecoma), ascites, and pleural effusion, is frequently misdiagnosed as ovarian cancer due to overlapping clinical presentations and elevated biomarkers. The case presented involved a large left ovarian thecoma that triggered preoperative suspicion of malignancy through imaging findings and markedly elevated CA125 levels (335.1 U/ml), which can be produced by mesothelial cells in response to peritoneal and pleural irritation rather than indicating malignancy. Surgical resection via left adnexectomy successfully resolved both the ascites and pleural effusion within one week, with CA125 levels decreasing to 164.6 U/ml, confirming the benign nature of the condition. Pathological examination and immunohistochemical analysis showing expression of CD10, CEA, EGFR, PR, vimentin, and inhibin-a along with absence of CD117, ER, and P53 definitively identified the mass as a benign thecoma. This case underscores the critical importance of intraoperative frozen section examination and comprehensive pathological evaluation in distinguishing benign ovarian tumors associated with Meigs syndrome from malignant ovarian neoplasms, as imaging and CA125 elevation alone are insufficient diagnostic markers.

title: Machine-Learning-Assisted
summary: This machine learning study identified key biomarkers and clinical features for distinguishing ovarian cancer from benign ovarian pathology in the context of endometriosis. HE4 and CA125 emerged as the most discriminatory biomarkers across both premenopausal and postmenopausal populations, consistently elevated in cancerous compared to benign tumors. Menopausal status and age substantially contributed to cancer classification, with menopausal transition and associated hormonal shifts linked to ovarian cancer pathogenesis. Secondary biomarkers including albumin, red blood cell distribution width, lymphocyte ratio, and mean corpuscular hemoglobin also demonstrated significant discriminatory power. Multiple supervised machine learning algorithms were evaluated, with logistic regression and random forest algorithms achieving 90.3% accuracy, while gradient boosting showed superior performance in stratified analyses, correctly classifying all cancerous tumors in postmenopausal patients. These findings suggest that integrated machine learning approaches combining classical tumor biomarkers with readily available clinical and hematological parameters can substantially improve ovarian cancer detection in women with endometriosis.

title: Bartholin's gland cyst with markedly elevated CA19-9 level: A case report
summary:
# Summary

CA19-9 is a carbohydrate antigen synthesized by epithelial cells throughout the biliary tract, pancreatic ducts, endometrium, and fallopian tubes through glycosyltransferase-catalyzed processes involving enzymes such as β3GALT5 and FUT3. While markedly elevated CA19-9 levels (>1,000 U/ml) are classically associated with pancreatic, liver, gastric, and colorectal malignancies where increased glycosyltransferase activity drives overproduction, this case report documents the first instance of a benign Bartholin's gland cyst producing substantially elevated CA19-9. The elevation occurs through mucinous epithelial oversecretion, inflammation-induced epithelial hyperplasia, and antigen leakage from barrier disruption, mechanisms shared with other benign conditions including endometriosis, ovarian endometriomas, and cystic lesions. Clinically, rapid CA19-9 normalization following marsupialization (83% reduction within 24 hours, complete normalization by day 59) confirmed the benign nature and local cystic origin, demonstrating that serial biomarker monitoring combined with imaging can effectively differentiate benign from malignant etiologies. This case emphasizes that CA19-9 elevation alone is insufficient for cancer diagnosis and highlights the diagnostic importance of temporal biomarker trends rather than absolute values.

title: Diagnostic Use of Selected Metalloproteinases in Endometrioid Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Endometrioid ovarian cancer (EnOC), a distinct subtype linked to endometriosis progression, shows elevated plasma levels of specific matrix metalloproteinases that can serve as diagnostic biomarkers. Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs)—proteolytic enzymes that degrade extracellular matrix components—play multifaceted roles in cancer development by promoting genomic instability, tumor cell proliferation, invasion, migration, angiogenesis, and metastasis. Among multiple MMP isoforms examined, MMP-7 emerged as the most promising diagnostic marker, achieving the highest sensitivity (98.55%), negative predictive value (95.41%), and diagnostic accuracy with an AUC of 0.9658, with patients with EnOC showing significantly elevated plasma MMP-7 levels (5.64 ng/mL) compared to healthy controls (0.95 ng/mL) and benign lesions. MMP-11 and MMP-26 also demonstrated elevated concentrations in EnOC patients, though MMP-10 and MMP-26 paradoxically showed higher levels in benign lesions, suggesting differential diagnostic utility. MMP-7 may facilitate tumor progression through extracellular matrix degradation and direct interaction with β-catenin, a regulator of the Wnt signaling pathway controlling cell cycle and adhesion. These findings support the clinical utility of MMP-7, MMP-11, and MMP-26 as novel biomarkers for distinguishing endometrioid ovarian cancer from benign ovarian pathology.

title: Protein and Peptide in Cancer Research: From Biomarker to Biotherapeutics
summary:
# Summary

Proteins and peptides play pivotal roles in cancer pathogenesis by driving tumor development, growth, immune evasion, angiogenesis, and metastasis through dysregulated expression, mutations in oncogenes like EGFR and HER2, and loss of tumor suppressors including p53 and PTEN. Cancer arises from a complex interplay of genetic mutations, epigenetic dysregulation, and altered cellular signaling pathways that collectively produce hallmark features such as uncontrolled proliferation, apoptosis resistance, sustained angiogenesis, and metabolic reprogramming. Clinical application of protein biomarkers including PSA, CA125, HER2, and AFP enables cancer screening, diagnosis, and disease monitoring across multiple cancer types, with CA125 particularly relevant for ovarian cancer management, though it shows limited early-stage sensitivity and cross-reactivity with benign conditions like endometriosis. Therapeutic strategies increasingly leverage proteins and peptides through monoclonal antibodies (cetuximab, trastuzumab, pertuzumab), immune checkpoint inhibitors, and peptide-based agents that target specific oncogenic pathways and improve patient survival. Advanced technologies including mass spectrometry, proteomic profiling of extracellular vesicles and exosomes, artificial intelligence, and nanomedicine platforms are accelerating discovery and development of novel protein-based diagnostics and therapeutics. Integration of synthetic biology, systems medicine, and multi-omics approaches promises to further enhance personalized cancer management through comprehensive molecular profiling and targeted intervention strategies.

title: The Imaging of Primary Fallopian Tube Carcinoma: A Literature Review
summary: Primary fallopian tube carcinomas (PFTCs) are rare malignancies frequently misclassified as ovarian cancers, presenting clinically with Latzko's triad of vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, and watery discharge, with particular concern in postmenopausal women and BRCA mutation carriers. High-grade serous carcinomas, which account for over 90% of PFTCs, originate from the tubal fimbriae and are preceded by serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC), a precursor lesion characterized by abnormal p53 expression that may progress to invasive disease. PFTCs demonstrate a greater propensity for distant metastases compared to ovarian carcinoma, with peritoneal surfaces representing the most common site of spread. Imaging plays a critical role in diagnosis, with transvaginal ultrasound identifying characteristic "sausage-shaped" solid-cystic masses, while MRI superior to CT for soft tissue detail, and contrast-enhanced ultrasound improving detection of neovascularization patterns typical of malignant lesions. Although CA-125 levels are prognostic, the lack of specific biomarkers contributes to over 50% preoperative misdiagnosis, underscoring the importance of imaging interpretation and histopathological confirmation. Prophylactic salpingectomy with ovarian preservation has emerged as a preventive strategy in BRCA mutation carriers to reduce tubal cancer risk.

title: Cancer Biomarkers: Reflection on Recent Progress, Emerging Innovations, and the Clinical Horizon
summary:
# Summary

Cancer biomarkers—including proteins like CA-125 and PSA, circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), and genetic mutations—play a central role in early cancer detection, treatment selection, and precision medicine applications. CA-125 is traditionally associated with ovarian cancer but can be elevated in non-malignant conditions such as endometriosis, highlighting the need for improved specificity. Emerging technologies including liquid biopsies, ctDNA monitoring for tumor burden assessment, and multi-omics integration are advancing non-invasive cancer diagnostics and treatment monitoring. Predictive biomarkers such as BRCA1/2 mutations, PD-L1 expression, EGFR mutations, and HER2 status guide targeted therapies including PARP inhibitors, immune checkpoint inhibitors, tyrosine kinase inhibitors, and monoclonal antibodies. Artificial intelligence and machine learning are revolutionizing biomarker discovery and analysis by identifying complex patterns in genomic and imaging data, while innovative platforms such as CancerSEEK and the Galleri test demonstrate the clinical potential of multi-analyte blood tests for early detection and multi-cancer screening across diverse patient populations.

title: Diagnostic Power of the Fibrinogen-to-Albumin Ratio for Estimating Malignancy in Patients with Adnexal Masses: A Methodological Study
summary:
# Summary

Fibrinogen, an acute-phase protein synthesized by the liver in response to pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6, interleukin-1β, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha, plays key roles in coagulation, thrombosis, inflammation, and tumor angiogenesis. The fibrinogen-to-albumin ratio (FAR) emerges as a novel biomarker for detecting malignancy in adnexal masses, with significantly elevated levels in malignant compared to borderline and benign masses (p < 0.001). While FAR demonstrates 47% sensitivity and 91% specificity for distinguishing borderline tumors from benign masses, it correlates with CA15-3 and lactate dehydrogenase in categorizing borderline and malignant tumors and complements traditional markers like CA125 by enhancing diagnostic specificity. High FAR is associated with poorer overall and progression-free survival in ovarian cancer, consistent with its established prognostic value across various malignancies including pancreatic and gallbladder cancers. The inflammatory pathway underlying FAR's utility involves pro-inflammatory cytokines inducing increased vascular permeability, leading to transcapillary albumin loss and hypoalbuminemia, which together elevate the FAR ratio. Integration of FAR with conventional tumor markers offers improved diagnostic accuracy for evaluating adnexal masses in patients at risk for ovarian malignancy.

title: Inflammatory Indices vs. CA 125 for the Diagnosis of Early Ovarian Cancer: Evidence from a Multicenter Prospective Italian Cohort
summary: This multicenter Italian study demonstrates that inflammatory indices, particularly the Systemic Immune-Inflammation Index (SIRI), provide superior diagnostic accuracy for early ovarian cancer compared to the traditional biomarker CA 125, with SIRI achieving an AUC of 0.71 versus CA 125's AUC of 0.59. Patients with ovarian cancer and borderline ovarian tumors exhibited significantly elevated neutrophils, monocytes, platelets, and SIRI/SIR values compared to those with benign tumors, reflecting a pro-inflammatory tumor microenvironment that promotes cancer progression. While CA 125 remains clinically relevant for ovarian cancer detection, its low specificity is problematic because elevated levels also occur in benign conditions including endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease, limiting its discriminatory utility. The biological mechanism underlying these findings suggests that tumor hypoxia and oxidative stress drive altered metabolism that modulates immune cell recruitment and activation, generating distinct inflammatory signatures across benign neoplasms, borderline tumors, and malignant cancers. These results support incorporating systemic inflammatory indices as complementary biomarkers to CA 125 for improved early ovarian cancer diagnosis, particularly in populations with high endometriosis prevalence where CA 125 specificity is compromised.

title: Special Issue “Biomarkers and Early Detection Strategies of Ovarian Tumors”
summary:
# Summary

High-grade ovarian carcinomas are characterized by frequent mutations in TP53 and homologous recombination repair genes (BRCA1, BRCA2, RAD51C/D, and BRIP1), whereas low-grade carcinomas and borderline ovarian tumors typically lack these mutations and share molecular similarities. Multiple risk factors contribute to ovarian cancer development, including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, obesity-associated chronic inflammation and elevated estrogen, incessant ovulation from early menarche or late menopause, whereas combined oral contraceptives reduce risk. Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) has emerged as a superior biomarker for early detection and relapse prediction, outperforming the traditional CA-125 marker, and the multi-biomarker OVA1 test can identify early-stage cancers missed by CA-125 alone. Current treatment approaches include platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin/carboplatin with paclitaxel), with hyperthermia enhancing efficacy and reducing drug resistance, alongside targeted therapies including bevacizumab (anti-VEGF), poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib, talazoparib, niraparib), and immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab). Prophylactic removal of ovaries and fallopian tubes remains the most effective preventive measure for high-risk individuals with BRCA mutations.

title: The Diagnostic Role of Tumor and Inflammatory Biomarkers in Ascitic Fluid: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review evaluates tumor and inflammatory biomarkers measured in ascitic fluid as diagnostic tools for malignant ascites and various cancers. Multiple biomarkers demonstrate substantial diagnostic utility, with CA 125, CEA, and CA 19-9 showing strong associations with ovarian, colorectal, and gastrointestinal cancers respectively, while inflammatory markers including IL-6, CRP, and VEGF provide added diagnostic value when conventional cytology is inconclusive. IL-6 and VEGF combined achieved perfect diagnostic accuracy (AUC 1.000) for ovarian cancer detection, and the microRNA ratio miR-21/miR-223 achieved an AUC of 0.982 for differentiating malignant from non-malignant ascites. Beyond diagnosis, elevated ascitic biomarker levels correlate with advanced disease stage, reduced progression-free survival, and worse clinical outcomes, suggesting their prognostic value. Notably, ascitic fluid biomarkers often demonstrate superior diagnostic accuracy compared to serum measurements, supporting the clinical utility of direct peritoneal fluid analysis for determining ascites etiology and predicting treatment responses.

title: Interaction between toll-like receptor 4 polymorphism and abdominal obesity on ovarian cancer risk in Chinese women
summary: This study identified toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) gene polymorphisms as significant risk factors for ovarian cancer in Chinese women, with rs4986790 and rs11536889 variants showing markedly elevated cancer risk. The rs4986790-G allele and rs11536889-C allele were more frequently observed in ovarian cancer patients, and individuals carrying the rs4986790-AG/GG and rs11536889-GC/CC genotype combinations exhibited the highest ovarian cancer risk. Critical gene-environment interactions were detected, particularly between the rs4986790 polymorphism and abdominal obesity, where obese women carrying rs4986790-AG/GG genotype showed substantially elevated cancer risk compared to non-obese carriers and those with the protective rs4986790-AA genotype. TLR4 signaling, which operates through the NF-κB pathway and regulates inflammatory responses, plays a crucial role in ovarian cancer differentiation and progression, with obesity-induced ovarian inflammation potentially amplifying this effect. These findings suggest that TLR4 polymorphisms serve as biomarkers for ovarian cancer susceptibility, with abdominal obesity acting as a potent environmental modifier that synergistically increases cancer risk.

title: The strategic role of lipidomics in biomarker identification and diagnosis of gynecological diseases
summary: Lipidomics, an analytical approach utilizing high-resolution mass spectrometry, enables comprehensive investigation of lipid metabolism and its role in gynecological diseases including endometriosis and ovarian cancer. Lipids function as critical regulators of cell proliferation, differentiation, survival, and death through involvement in signal transduction, energy metabolism, and inflammatory responses. In ovarian cancer, dysregulation of lipid metabolism creates a distinctive lipid signature characterized by elevated ceramides, fatty acids (particularly monounsaturated variants), and longer-chain triacylglycerols, alongside reduced levels of cholesteryl esters, sphingomyelins, and phosphatidylcholines. Specific lipid classes including lysophosphatidylcholines, phosphatidylcholines, and ceramides act as biomarkers for ovarian cancer diagnosis, with sphingomyelin levels showing positive correlation with cancer risk in postmenopausal women. The altered lipid profile in cancer cells supports enhanced energy demands of rapid proliferation through upregulation of desaturases and reprogrammed lipid metabolism. These findings demonstrate that lipidomics-derived lipid biomarkers hold significant potential for improving diagnosis and understanding pathogenic mechanisms in gynecological malignancies associated with endometriosis.

title: Serum CA 19 − 9 and CA 72 − 4 levels in hashimoto’s thyroiditis: a prospective case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective case-control study investigated serum tumor markers CA 19-9 and CA 72-4 in Hashimoto's thyroiditis, an autoimmune condition characterized by thyroid autoantibodies (TPOAb and TgAb) that commonly causes subclinical hypothyroidism. While CA 19-9 and CA 72-4 are traditionally used to monitor malignancies including pancreatic, gastric, and ovarian cancers, the authors found these markers are also elevated in benign inflammatory conditions, including autoimmune diseases such as Hashimoto's thyroiditis, rheumatoid arthritis, and systemic sclerosis. Notably, in patients who achieved euthyroidism following levothyroxine replacement therapy, CA 19-9 levels showed statistically significant reductions, suggesting that tumor marker elevation in Hashimoto's thyroiditis is inflammation-driven rather than malignancy-related. The study identified significant correlations between CA 72-4 and both TSH and TPOAb levels, supporting an association between thyroid dysfunction and systemic marker elevation. These findings underscore the importance of recognizing that elevated CA 19-9 and CA 72-4 in thyroid disease reflect cellular turnover and inflammation rather than malignant transformation, and that appropriate thyroid hormone replacement can reduce these markers alongside restoration of euthyroid status.

title: Laboratory and Clinical Values of the Neutrophil-to-Lymphocyte Ratio in Women With Hyperemesis Gravidarum: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Hyperemesis gravidarum (HG), characterized by severe nausea and vomiting, leads to significant maternal complications including water and electrolyte imbalance, anxiety, Wernicke's encephalopathy, and vitamin K deficiency, alongside adverse fetal outcomes such as premature delivery and low birth weight. This meta-analysis of 3,423 participants demonstrates that the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) is significantly elevated in HG patients compared with healthy pregnant women, with levels correlating strongly with disease severity and ketonuria, suggesting its utility as a simple, cost-effective biomarker for HG detection and diagnosis. The elevated NLR in HG reflects heightened systemic inflammation driven by increased cytokine concentrations, particularly TNF-α produced by Th1 and Th2 lymphocytes, alongside pregnancy-induced sympathetic nervous system activation that promotes myelopoiesis in granulocytes expressing adrenergic receptors. Beyond HG, the study contextualizes NLR as a valuable inflammatory marker across multiple gynecological conditions including endometriosis, ovarian cancer, preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and adnexal torsion, establishing its broad clinical applicability. The findings support incorporating NLR as an adjunctive laboratory criterion alongside clinical assessment to differentiate HG from healthy pregnancies and stratify disease severity.

title: Proteomic Profiling of Bacterial Extracellular Vesicles for Exploring Ovarian Cancer Biomarkers
summary: Ovarian cancer is associated with dysbiosis of the vaginal microbiota, which can lead to alterations in immune and metabolic signaling while increasing systemic inflammation. Bacterial extracellular vesicles (BEVs) produced by both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria found in the vaginal cavity can ascend into the peritoneal cavity, where they carry microbial proteins detectable in cancer ascites. Proteomic profiling of ascites from ovarian cancer patients identified 283 proteins elevated in cancer extracellular vesicles compared to non-cancer controls, including the bacterial protein P15636_Protease1, which showed high diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.88) for ovarian cancer detection. P15636_Protease1 is exclusively of bacterial origin, absent in human cells, and represents a novel biomarker candidate that distinguishes ovarian cancer patients from healthy controls. These findings suggest that dysbiotic bacterial products contribute to ovarian cancer pathogenesis and that BEV-associated proteins could serve as improved diagnostic biomarkers for early detection, potentially outperforming the current standard CA125 marker which has proven ineffective for early-stage disease identification.

title: The protective effect of sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors and the incidence of ovarian cancer: a nationwide cohort preliminary study
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide cohort study demonstrates that sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors reduce ovarian cancer incidence in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus through multiple protective mechanisms. Ovarian cancer development is strongly associated with hyperglycemia, type 2 diabetes, inflammation, and oxidative stress—all pathological states that SGLT2 inhibitors effectively target. By reducing hyperglycemia and glycated hemoglobin levels while simultaneously suppressing proinflammatory cytokine expression and decreasing reactive oxygen species production, SGLT2 inhibitors address key risk factors for ovarian cancer progression and metastasis. The study found significantly lower ovarian cancer incidence in the SGLT2 inhibitor-treated group compared to controls, supporting the therapeutic potential of these agents beyond their established cardiovascular and renoprotective benefits in diabetic patients. These findings suggest SGLT2 inhibitors may offer a dual benefit for patients with type 2 diabetes who carry additional ovarian cancer risk factors such as obesity, smoking, or endometriosis.

title: From Fallopian Tube to Ovarian Cancer: Understanding the Evaluation and Management of Serous Tubal Intraepithelial Carcinoma Lesions
summary:
# Summary

Serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) represents a critical precursor lesion to high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), found in up to 60% of ovarian cancer cases and capable of progressing to invasive disease within 5-7 years. The molecular pathogenesis involves sequential p53 mutations in fallopian tube epithelium, followed by oxidative stress, genomic instability, and inflammation that drive malignant transformation, with p53 signatures appearing as early precancerous markers even in BRCA mutation carriers. BRCA1/BRCA2 germline mutations substantially increase ovarian cancer risk and STIC prevalence, making risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy the standard preventive intervention in high-risk women, while opportunistic salpingectomy during routine gynecologic procedures offers meaningful cancer risk reduction without significant morbidity. Improved STIC detection relies on the SEE-FIM (Sectioning and Extensively Examining the FIMbriated end) pathology protocol, and genetic counseling is essential upon diagnosis due to hereditary predisposition implications. Management strategies include bilateral oophorectomy when STIC is identified, though surveillance without surgery may be considered in select cases, with recognition that occult invasive carcinoma or peritoneal disease coexist in 30-60% of STIC cases.

title: Harnessing ovarian cancer ascites for translational science: models, biomarkers, and therapeutics
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer-associated ascites represents a critical microenvironment that promotes cancer progression, peritoneal metastasis, and chemoresistance through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Tumor cells within ascites exist as spheroids and multicellular aggregates expressing hybrid epithelial-mesenchymal phenotypes that enable cellular plasticity and facilitate peritoneal implantation and transcoelomic metastases. The ascitic microenvironment suppresses immune responses while enhancing cell motility and invasion, with growth factors like VEGF promoting angiogenesis and vascular permeability that sustains fluid accumulation. Current therapeutic approaches include bevacizumab for anti-angiogenic effects and PARP inhibitors (olaparib, niraparib, rucaparib) targeting homologous recombination deficiency, with treatment selection guided by biomarkers including TP53, BRCA1/2, and other somatic mutations detectable in circulating free DNA from ascites. Diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers include specific mesothelial markers (calretinin, WT-1, podoplanin), epithelial markers (EpCAM, claudin-4), and tumor spheroid cytological characterization, with positive ascitic cytology and tumor cell presence strongly associated with poor prognosis and disease recurrence.

title: Clinical Value of Vascular Cancer Thrombus and Myometrial Invasion Combined with Tumor Markers in Predicting Sentinel Lymph Node Metastasis of Endometrial Cancer
summary: This study identifies clinical and serological predictors of sentinel lymph node metastasis in endometrial cancer patients. Vascular cancer thrombus and deep myometrial invasion (≥1/2) emerged as significant histopathological risk factors, with their incidence substantially higher in patients with metastatic disease. Among tumor markers, CA199 demonstrated the strongest predictive value with the highest odds ratio (13.599), while HE4, CA125, CA153, and CEA were all significantly elevated in metastatic compared to non-metastatic groups; notably, AFP showed no significant association. The combined detection of vascular cancer thrombus, myometrial invasion, and the five elevated tumor markers (HE4, CA153, CA125, CA199, CEA) achieved excellent diagnostic accuracy with an area under the curve of 0.90 for predicting sentinel lymph node metastasis. These findings suggest that an integrated approach incorporating both morphological features and multiple tumor marker measurements substantially enhances the clinical ability to identify endometrial cancer patients at high risk for lymph node metastasis, which is critical for treatment planning and prognostication.

title: Sister Mary Joseph's Nodule Revealing Klatskin's Cholangiocarcinoma at the Yalgado Ouedraogo University Hospital: Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of Klatskin's cholangiocarcinoma manifesting as a Sister Mary Joseph nodule, an umbilical metastasis indicating advanced primary neoplasia. The patient, a chronic hepatitis B virus carrier, presented with epigastric burning pain followed by a painful, pruritic umbilical tumefaction accompanied by clinical cholestasis syndrome and markedly elevated bilirubin levels. Umbilical biopsy revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma consistent with cholangiocarcinoma metastasis. The nodule arises through multiple mechanisms including lymphatic spread via retrograde subserosal lymphatics, arterial anastomosis, venous spread, direct peritoneal extension, and embryologic remnants, though cholangiocarcinoma metastasis to the umbilicus remains exceptionally rare. Both the Sister Mary Joseph nodule and umbilical skin metastases carry poor prognosis, and diagnosis relies on imaging such as CT scan and definitive anatomopathological examination via biopsy, with treatment options including radiotherapy.

title: Awareness about the symptoms & risk factors of ovarian cancer among women in North Urban Bengaluru: A cross-sectional study
journal: The Indian Journal of Medical Research
summary: This cross-sectional awareness study from urban Bengaluru reveals substantial gaps in women's knowledge of ovarian cancer symptoms and risk factors, with critical implications for early detection. The most commonly recognized symptoms were extreme fatigue (61.7%), pelvic pain (54.2%), and abdominal enlargement (51.4%), yet participants frequently missed key warning signs including difficulty eating, persistent bloating, and urinary changes. Well-established risk factors such as ovarian cysts, smoking, age over 50, and being overweight were identified by most respondents, whereas less familiar risk factors—including endometriosis, IVF treatment, and talcum powder use—remained underrecognized. Sociodemographic factors including age, education, marital status, and socioeconomic class significantly influenced both symptom awareness and understanding of risk factors, with younger and higher-educated women demonstrating greater knowledge. This knowledge gap is particularly concerning given that 75% of ovarian cancer diagnoses occur at late stages in low-to-medium Human Development Index nations, underscoring how limited awareness directly impacts disease progression and outcomes. Enhanced public health education targeting underserved populations is essential to improve early detection rates.

title: In Search of Ideal Solutions for Cancer Diagnosis: From Conventional Methods to Protein Biomarkers in Liquid Biopsy
summary: Liquid biopsy represents a transformative approach to cancer diagnosis and monitoring by analyzing protein biomarkers and other molecular entities in bodily fluids, enabling early detection, treatment response assessment, and prognosis across multiple cancer types including ovarian, breast, and pancreatic cancers. Key biomarkers such as circulating tumor cells, extracellular vesicles, exosomal microRNAs, and autoantibodies provide insights into tumor heterogeneity and disease progression, while established markers like CA-125 and HE4 demonstrate diagnostic utility for ovarian and endometrial cancers, though their non-specific elevation in benign conditions like endometriosis limits their clinical precision. Proteoforms—specific protein variants linked to tumor subtypes, therapeutic resistance, and early detection—offer enhanced diagnostic resolution beyond conventional protein-level analysis, particularly when detected through advanced technologies including aptamer-based platforms and integrative top-down proteomics. Proteome-based plasma tests exhibit superior diagnostic accuracy compared to traditional methods, and emerging technologies like nucleic acid aptamers provide cost-effective alternatives to antibodies for detecting cancer-associated biomarkers. The integration of liquid biopsy with personalized medicine strategies enables therapeutic target identification, drug resistance analysis, and patient stratification for tailored treatment approaches. These advances collectively position liquid biopsy as a promising tool for non-invasive cancer detection and management across diverse malignancies.

title: Utility of a Multimodal Biomarker Panel and Serum Proapoptotic Activity to Refine Diagnosis of Ovarian Adnexal Masses
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated a multimodal biomarker panel combined with functional apoptosis assays to improve discrimination between benign and malignant ovarian adnexal masses, with particular relevance to endometriosis-associated cases. Traditional biomarkers CA125 and HE4, which form the basis of the ROMA algorithm, were elevated in malignant tumors but lack specificity as they also associate with benign conditions including endometriomas. The researchers developed two enhanced classifiers: Classifier 1 incorporated CA125, HE4, MRP8/14, and serum proapoptotic activity, while Classifier 2 additionally measured OPN, SAA, IL-6, IL-8, and IGFBP-4—molecules linked to inflammation, tumor progression, and chemoresistance. Both classifiers significantly outperformed the standard ROMA index in diagnostic accuracy. Functionally, malignant sera reduced apoptosis in immune cells, suggesting tumor-mediated immune suppression, while elevated serum CD95L in ovarian cancer promoted T cell infiltration and inflammation. This multimodal approach combining traditional markers with inflammatory cytokines, damage-associated proteins, and functional apoptotic assessment provides improved diagnostic refinement for distinguishing malignant ovarian masses from benign lesions, particularly relevant for endometriosis cases where CA125 elevation alone creates diagnostic ambiguity.

title: Prognostic value of CT-PCI and CA-125 changes following neoadjuvant chemotherapy in advanced epithelial ovarian cancer: A retrospective study from Vietnam
summary: This Vietnamese retrospective study evaluates the prognostic value of CT-based Peritoneal Cancer Index (CT-PCI) and serum CA-125 as biomarkers in advanced epithelial ovarian cancer treated with neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) followed by interval debulking surgery. Both biomarkers significantly decreased following NACT (CT-PCI from 14.0 to 4.0; CA-125 from 1248 to 31.25 U/mL), with greater reductions strongly associated with optimal cytoreduction and reduced residual disease. Post-treatment CT-PCI scores and CA-125 levels greater than 35 U/mL independently predicted suboptimal surgical outcomes and the presence of residual tumors. The combined assessment of CT-PCI and CA-125 substantially improved prognostic accuracy compared to either marker alone, with biomarker reduction correlating with improved progression-free and overall survival. These findings establish CT-PCI and CA-125 as complementary dynamic markers for monitoring chemotherapy response and predicting surgical success in advanced ovarian cancer, though the authors note CA-125 elevation is not cancer-specific and also occurs in benign conditions including endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease.

title: The kinetics of CA125 levels as a prognostic marker for in-hospital mortality in patients with acute heart failure: a pilot study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study demonstrates that CA125, a biomarker traditionally associated with ovarian cancer, serves as a strong prognostic indicator in acute heart failure (AHF) patients. CA125 levels were significantly elevated in AHF patients (median 127.5 U/ml) compared to chronic heart failure and controls, and notably, an increase in CA125 after 7 days of treatment was strongly associated with in-hospital mortality (hazard ratio 37.50), with mortality risk rising exponentially as CA125 levels increased. CA125 showed moderate correlation with the standard heart failure marker NT-proBNP (r=0.59) and was superior to NT-proBNP in predicting mortality outcomes, particularly in patients with congestive patterns and venous congestion. The biomarker's elevation reflects congestion-related pathophysiology, as evidenced by its association with classic AHF symptoms including peripheral edema, dyspnea, hepatomegaly, and jugular vein distention. These findings suggest CA125 may be an independent predictor of in-hospital mortality that outperforms conventional natriuretic peptide markers in certain AHF presentations, warranting further investigation as a risk stratification tool in acute cardiovascular decompensation.

title: Serum miR-200c-3p and miR-6134 as diagnostic biomarkers for epithelial ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies serum microRNAs miR-200c-3p and miR-6134 as promising diagnostic biomarkers for epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC), with both significantly upregulated in cancer patients compared to healthy controls and benign ovarian tumors. When evaluated individually, miR-6134 demonstrated superior diagnostic performance (AUC 0.818–0.933) compared to the conventional marker CA125, and showed progressive elevation correlating with disease severity and advanced stage. Notably, the combination of miR-200c-3p and miR-6134 achieved an AUC of 0.935 in patients with low CA125 levels (<35 U/mL), providing valuable diagnostic discrimination in a subgroup where traditional markers fail. Multi-marker combinations incorporating these microRNAs with CA125, HE4, and IL-6 further enhanced diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.968), while miR-200c-3p, CA125, and IL-6 were all significantly elevated in recurrent cancer cases. These findings suggest that miR-200c-3p and miR-6134 could improve early EOC detection and better distinguish cancer from benign conditions including endometriosis, which can also elevate CA125 and confound diagnosis.

title: A novel serum-based steroid-protein panels for differentiating ovarian cancer from non-malignant adnexal masses
summary: This study identifies a novel serum-based steroid-protein diagnostic panel for distinguishing ovarian cancer from benign adnexal masses. Patients with ovarian cancer exhibit significantly lower levels of androgens including testosterone, 11β-hydroxy-testosterone (11OHT), and 11-keto-testosterone (11KT), while showing elevated cortisol compared to controls. A multivariate diagnostic model incorporating three 11-oxyandrogens (11KT, 11OHA4, and 11OHT) combined with either CA-125 or HE4 substantially outperforms traditional biomarkers alone, achieving an AUC of 0.911 when combined with HE4. The steroid-based model proves particularly effective for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, where all three 11-oxyandrogens are significantly reduced. These findings suggest that incorporating serum steroid profiling with conventional tumor markers could improve early detection of ovarian cancer, addressing the critical need for better diagnostic tools given that most cases are diagnosed at advanced stages.

title: Peritoneal Tuberculosis Mimicking Cancerous Peritonitis With Elevated Serum Cancer Antigen 125 and Carbohydrate Antigen 19‐9 Levels: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 75-year-old patient with rheumatoid arthritis receiving certolizumab who presented with peritoneal tuberculosis (PTB) that clinically and radiologically mimicked carcinomatous peritonitis. The patient displayed markedly elevated serum cancer antigens CA125 (332.0 U/mL) and CA19-9 (213.0 U/mL), along with massive ascites and peritoneal thickening on imaging, with diagnosis confirmed by detection of Mycobacterium tuberculosis in sputum and peritoneal biopsy samples showing granulomatous inflammation. The elevation of both tumor markers occurred because PTB causes mesothelial inflammation, highlighting that CA125 and CA19-9 are nonspecific markers that can be elevated in various benign conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and inflammatory processes, not solely malignancy. Following anti-tuberculous therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol, levofloxacin, and pyrazinamide, both serum biomarkers decreased significantly, demonstrating that the marker elevations were directly attributable to the infectious process rather than malignancy. This case underscores the critical diagnostic challenge of distinguishing PTB from ovarian cancer based on clinical presentation and biomarkers alone, emphasizing the importance of considering infection in patients with risk factors such as immunosuppressive therapy.

title: Risk of ovarian cancer in women with pelvic inflammatory disease and homologous recombination repair gene mutations under 55: a population-based cohort study
summary: This population-based cohort study examined how pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) and germline homologous recombination repair (gHR) gene mutations jointly influence ovarian cancer risk in women under 55 years old. The research identified pathogenic variants in five major HRR genes—BRCA1, BRCA2, RAD51C, RAD51D, and BRIP1—as significant risk factors for ovarian cancer, consistent with their recognized roles in genomic instability and high-grade serous ovarian cancer development. Notably, women under 55 with a history of PID showed a substantially elevated ovarian cancer risk, with the highest risk observed in those harboring both PID history and gHR mutations, suggesting PID as a potential precursor to ovarian cancer in genetically vulnerable individuals. The age-stratified analysis revealed this association was specific to younger women, particularly those aged 35 or younger and those with recurrent PID, while no significant PID-ovarian cancer association appeared in older age groups. The findings underscore that chronic inflammation from bacterial infections like Chlamydia trachomatis may interact with inherited genetic vulnerabilities to amplify cancer risk. These results support the clinical importance of early intervention and screening in younger women with PID, particularly those carrying HRR mutations, and highlight the therapeutic potential of PARP inhibitors for HRR-deficient ovarian cancers.

title: Recent Advances in the Applications of Biomaterials in Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This review examines biomarkers and molecular mechanisms relevant to ovarian cancer, with particular relevance to endometriosis-associated malignancies. Endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas, which arise from endometriosis, harbor characteristic mutations in ARID1A, PTEN, and PIK3CA, distinguishing them from other ovarian cancer subtypes. High-grade serous carcinomas are defined by TP53 mutations and typically respond initially to platinum-based chemotherapy, while low-grade serous carcinomas carrying KRAS, BRAF, or ERBB2 mutations progress more slowly. Multiple cell surface markers—including CXCR4, folate receptor, integrin α-5, and uPAR—are overexpressed in ovarian cancer cells and serve as therapeutic targets, with CXCR4 playing a key role in cancer progression and metastasis through CXCL12 binding. Traditional biomarkers such as CA-125 and HE4, along with emerging markers like MUC1, caveolin-1, and HER2, enable improved diagnosis and targeted therapeutic approaches, while circulating tumor cells drive distant metastasis and chemotherapy resistance mechanisms like BIRC5 limit treatment efficacy.

title: Epidemiology and risk factors for ovarian cancer incidence in the USA: a multilevel analysis
summary: This multilevel epidemiological analysis identified multiple demographic, behavioral, and medical risk factors for ovarian cancer incidence in the United States. Age emerged as the strongest predictor, with women aged 50–69 years demonstrating approximately 80–100% increased risk compared to younger cohorts, likely reflecting cumulative ovulatory cycles and age-related DNA damage accumulation. Hormone replacement therapy, particularly estrogen-only formulations, approximately doubled ovarian cancer risk through promotion of excessive ovarian cell proliferation. Significant disparities were observed across racial/ethnic groups and geographic regions, with non-Hispanic Black women and residents of the Southern United States showing substantially lower risk than non-Hispanic White women and Northeastern residents, respectively. Notable medical associations included endometriosis and Peutz-Jeghers syndrome as relevant conditions, with the latter conferring a 3.6-fold increased risk. Socioeconomic factors also influenced risk, including employment status and neighborhood educational attainment, suggesting that healthcare utilization patterns and access may contribute to the observed incidence variations across populations.

title: Clinicopathological characterization and prognostic risk modeling in patients with synchronous ovarian and endometrial cancer: a population-based study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study characterizes synchronous endometrial and ovarian cancer (SEOC), a rare gynecological malignancy accounting for approximately 2.6–10% of ovarian cancer cases, and identifies key prognostic factors. The dual-primary group exhibited significantly better outcomes than single-primary tumors, with 5-year overall survival of 79% versus 51%, driven by the predominance of endometrioid histology (77% of cases) and early-stage disease in dual-primary presentations. Favorable prognostic indicators include endometrioid rather than serous histology, well-differentiated tumors, negative CA125 and lymph node status, and early AJCC stage, whereas advanced disease, positive lymph node involvement, and distant metastases strongly predict worse survival. A prognostic risk model was developed with high predictive accuracy (AUC >0.87) for 1-, 2-, and 3-year cancer-specific mortality, incorporating AJCC stage, tumor grade, and lymph node dissection status as primary prognostic determinants. These findings underscore SEOC as a distinct clinicopathological entity requiring careful histological differentiation from metastatic disease to ensure accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment planning.

title: Ovarian Tumor Biomarkers: Correlation Between Tumor Type and Marker Expression, and Their Role in Guiding Therapeutic Strategies
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines biomarkers for distinguishing ovarian cancer subtypes and their associations with endometriosis, which serves as a direct precursor for endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas. Established serum markers CA-125 and HE4 are routinely used for screening and monitoring, though their sensitivity is limited for early-stage disease and benign conditions like endometriosis. High-grade serous carcinomas are characterized by TP53 mutations (94% of cases) with abnormal p53 expression, while the Δ133p53 isoform suppresses mutant p53 function and correlates with improved overall survival; WT1 and PAX8 provide superior diagnostic sensitivity (>90%) for this subtype. Clear cell carcinomas show strong associations with endometriosis and are effectively identified through three-marker panels (IMP3, Napsin A, HNF-1β achieving AUC=0.954), with HNF-1β driving glucose metabolism and apoptosis resistance, while IMP3 and IFITM1 promote aggressive cellular behaviors. Mucinous carcinomas frequently harbor KRAS mutations that activate the MAPK signaling cascade, driving uncontrolled proliferation and chemotherapy resistance, whereas low-grade serous carcinomas typically present with KRAS, BRAF, or NRAS mutations combined with wild-type TP53 and patchy p16 expression. These diverse molecular signatures enable stratification of ovarian cancer subtypes and guide targeted therapeutic strategies based on specific genetic and proteomic profiles.

title: Comparative study of Exosome Ovarian Cancer auxiliary diagnostic kit (Chemiluminescence) and Serum Tumor Marker CA125 in the diagnosis of Ovarian Epithelial Cell Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary
