This comparative study evaluates an exosomal ovarian cancer assay (OCS) against conventional serum biomarkers for epithelial ovarian cancer diagnosis. The exosomal assay, which detects elevated CA125, HSP70, and CD63 within exosomes, demonstrated superior diagnostic performance with 74.0% sensitivity and 99.3% specificity compared to serum CA125 alone (40.0% sensitivity, 97.3% specificity). The OCS approach achieved an area under the curve of 0.911, significantly outperforming traditional serum CA125 (0.670) and HE4 (0.799) measurements. A critical clinical advantage of the exosomal assay is its ability to overcome CA125's limited specificity, as the marker is also elevated in benign conditions including endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease, leading to false-positive diagnoses with conventional testing. The study identified upregulated microRNAs miR-373 and miR-205 in patient-derived exosomes as potential contributors to cancer detection. These findings suggest that exosomal biomarker detection offers a more reliable diagnostic tool for epithelial ovarian cancer, particularly high-grade serous carcinoma, potentially improving early detection and reducing unnecessary procedures in patients with benign pelvic disease.

title: Pregnancy with vitrified oocytes after fertility sparing surgery in a patient with fallopian tube cancer and a BRCA2 mutation: A case report and literature review
summary: This case report describes a BRCA2-positive patient with fallopian tube endometrioid carcinoma who achieved a successful pregnancy using vitrified oocytes after undergoing fertility-sparing surgery. BRCA2 mutations significantly increase the lifetime risk of fallopian tube and ovarian cancer, and carriers exhibit diminished oocyte reserves due to defective DNA repair pathways leading to oocyte damage and apoptosis. The patient underwent controlled ovarian stimulation using aromatase inhibitors (letrozole) combined with recombinant FSH and GnRH antagonists to suppress estrogen levels while preserving oocyte quality, followed by oocyte cryopreservation to enable future fertility without increasing cancer recurrence risk. Fertility-sparing surgery for early-stage ovarian and fallopian tube cancers demonstrates an acceptable recurrence rate (~11%) comparable to radical surgery, with high spontaneous pregnancy rates (~84%) in selected patients. The case demonstrates that oocyte cryopreservation from antral follicles—which are devoid of cancer cell infiltration—provides a safe and reliable option for fertility preservation in cancer patients, including those with BRCA mutations. The authors propose that fertility preservation should be systematically offered to BRCA mutation carriers undergoing risk-reduction surgery, particularly given the successful outcomes demonstrated in this case.

title: MEGF8-driven metabolic reprogramming and immune evasion define a high-risk subtype of endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer
summary: Endometriosis elevates ovarian cancer risk through multiple mechanisms including genetic mutations, chronic inflammation, and hormonal dysregulation. The study identified three molecular subtypes of endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer, with subtype A exhibiting aggressive features including enhanced epithelial-mesenchymal transition, immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment activation, and TGF-β signaling, while subtype B demonstrated superior overall survival. MEGF8 emerged as a critical driver of cancer progression, regulating cancer stem cell properties, metabolic reprogramming, and extracellular matrix remodeling, with MEGF8 knockdown effectively suppressing cell proliferation and migration. An 8-gene prognostic signature incorporating MEGF8 stratified patients into high- and low-risk groups, with high-risk patients exhibiting enhanced immune evasion, elevated stromal scores, and worse survival outcomes. Low-risk patients showed enhanced sensitivity to PI3K/AKT/mTOR and VEGFR inhibitors, suggesting targeted therapeutic strategies based on molecular subtype classification.

title: Preoperative Risk Stratification of Adnexal Masses: A Narrative Review of Diagnostic Models and the International Ovarian Tumor Analysis (IOTA) Assessment of Different Neoplasias in the Adnexa (ADNEX) Performance
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review evaluates preoperative risk stratification models for adnexal masses, with emphasis on the International Ovarian Tumor Analysis (IOTA) ADNEX model, which demonstrates superior diagnostic accuracy compared to earlier indices like the Risk of Malignancy Index and Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm. The ADNEX model achieves area under the curve values between 0.82 and 0.90 by integrating ultrasound morphology with clinical parameters including menopausal status and CA-125 levels, enabling detailed stratification of benign, borderline, and malignant lesions rather than simple dichotomous classification. While ADNEX shows high specificity for advanced ovarian malignancies and effectively classifies serous and mucinous tumors, it demonstrates reduced sensitivity for borderline tumors, early-stage disease, and fibromas, with optimal performance using a 20%-30% malignancy threshold. The model's clinical utility extends to patient triage, surgical decision-making, and reduction of unnecessary procedures, though limitations include operator dependence, decreased accuracy in pregnant women and those with endometriosis, and challenges with rare tumor types like Brenner tumors. Integration of ADNEX with complementary approaches such as IOTA Simple Rules, O-RADS, or MRI descriptors, combined with additional biomarkers like HE4, can enhance diagnostic precision and support timely referral of malignant cases to gynecologic oncologists for optimal treatment outcomes.

title: Huge mucinous ovarian borderline tumor: a case report
summary: Mucinous borderline ovarian tumors represent a rare ovarian malignancy typically occurring in younger patients and characterized by epithelial proliferation without stromal invasion. These tumors present clinically with abdominal compressive symptoms, ascites, weight loss, and elevated CA19.9 levels, though CA125 is not a reliable diagnostic marker. Two distinct subtypes exist: intestinal-like mucinous borderline tumors (IMBT), which are larger, typically unilateral, occur in older patients, and show less association with endometriosis, versus endometriosis-associated mucinous borderline tumors (EMBT) with different histological and cellular profiles. Diagnosis relies on histopathological testing and MRI imaging to differentiate borderline from malignant forms, with immunohistochemical markers like CK7/CK20 status helping exclude metastatic disease from the intestines, appendix, or other organs. Adequate debulking surgery improves prognosis, while chemotherapy is generally not recommended unless advanced disease is present, and conservative surgical approaches carry approximately 30% recurrence risk. Clinicians must consider the possibility of pseudomyxoma peritonei and evaluate for primary tumors in other organs, as mucinous ovarian masses may represent secondary metastatic disease.

title: Management of Patients with Epithelial Ovarian Cancer: A Systematic Comparison of International Guidelines from Scientific Societies (AIOM-BGCS-ESGO-ESMO-JGSO-NCCN-NICE)
summary:
# Summary

Epithelial ovarian cancer remains a lethal gynecological malignancy primarily due to late-stage diagnosis and limited early screening tools, with established associations between BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, Lynch syndrome, and homologous recombination gene panel mutations with increased ovarian cancer risk. While CA-125 serves as the primary serum marker for ovarian cancer detection, its utility is limited by low sensitivity in early disease and elevation in benign conditions including endometriosis and fibroids, necessitating complementary diagnostic approaches such as transvaginal ultrasound and imaging modalities like contrast-enhanced CT. Emerging liquid biopsy technologies including circulating tumor DNA and microRNA detection show promise for improved screening strategies. For individuals carrying germline mutations, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy represents the standard risk-reducing intervention, though residual risk of primary peritoneal carcinoma persists; hormone replacement therapy with estrogen-progestin combinations is recommended post-surgically to manage symptoms while preventing endometrial hyperplasia. Standardized ultrasound risk stratification models, particularly the IOTA algorithm variants, offer superior diagnostic accuracy compared to traditional indices for characterizing adnexal masses. Additionally, BRCA1/BRCA2 mutation carriers demonstrate accelerated ovarian aging with lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels, making oocyte cryopreservation an important fertility preservation option.

title: Synolitic Graph Neural Networks of High-Dimensional Proteomic Data Enhance Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Early detection of ovarian cancer remains clinically challenging, as traditional single-protein biomarkers like CA125 and HE4 show limited efficacy due to overlap with benign conditions such as endometriosis. This study applied a synolitic Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework to high-dimensional proteomic data from pre-diagnostic serum samples to improve early cancer detection. The approach captures protein-protein interaction patterns rather than relying on individual protein concentrations, identifying key nodes such as MUC16 alongside novel proteins including CHI3L1, DNAH17, FSTL1, PEBP4, AGR2, and SLPI. Graph Convolutional Networks maintained robust performance across different detection windows (ROC-AUC ~71-74%), significantly outperforming conventional machine learning models like XGBoost, which declined sharply when predicting cancer 1-2 years before diagnosis (ROC-AUC 46%). The synolitic GNN framework incorporates graph-based regularization and edge-weight shrinkage to mitigate overfitting, enabling strong generalization even under data scarcity. These network-informed approaches highlight the clinical potential of capturing subtle proteomic relationship patterns for early ovarian cancer detection and may help distinguish cancer from benign conditions in populations at risk, such as endometriosis patients.

title: CA19-9 as a Dynamic Biomarker for Continuous Monitoring of Therapeutic Efficacy in Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma
summary: CA19-9, a carbohydrate antigen synthesized by pancreatic and biliary ductal epithelial cells, serves as the most widely used serum biomarker for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), with markedly elevated levels strongly associated with disease presence. The biomarker is produced through a multi-step glycosylation process involving mucin glycoproteins (MUC1 and MUC16) and enzymatic modifications by fucosyltransferase 3 and sialyltransferases to form the characteristic Lewis A epitope. Beyond PDAC, elevated CA19-9 occurs in cholangiocarcinoma, colorectal cancer, gastric cancer, and other malignancies including ovarian cancer, as well as benign conditions such as endometriosis, pancreatitis, and diabetes mellitus, necessitating careful clinical interpretation. CA19-9 correlates strongly with tumor burden and TNM staging, with elevated preoperative levels (>100 U/mL) predicting metastatic disease and poor overall survival, while normal preoperative levels (<37 U/mL) indicate significantly better prognosis in resectable PDAC. The biomarker's clinical utility is enhanced by its dynamic responsiveness to therapy and its elevation preceding radiological evidence of recurrence by months, making it invaluable for continuous monitoring of therapeutic efficacy. Mechanistically, CA19-9 promotes a pro-tumorigenic microenvironment through chronic inflammation, interacts with E-selectin to facilitate tumor cell adhesion, and associates with M2-like tumor-associated macrophages, while hepatic clearance involves lectin-mediated mechanisms dependent on sialylation status.

title: Early Detection and Prevention of Ovarian Cancer
summary: This review examines biomarkers and risk factors for early detection and prevention of ovarian cancer, with particular relevance to endometriosis-associated malignancy. The study identifies established risk factors including advanced age and hereditary mutations in BRCA1, BRCA2, and emerging genes such as BRIP1, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D, and SMARCA4, while protective factors include pregnancy, breastfeeding, oral contraceptive use, bilateral tubal ligation, and hysterectomy. Type I ovarian cancers are characterized by somatic mutations in KRAS, BRAF, ERBB2, CTNNB1, PTEN, PIK3CA, and ARID1A, and develop stepwise from precursor lesions including borderline tumors and endometriosis, whereas Type II cancers harbor TP53 mutations and arise from serous tubal intraepithelial carcinomas (STIC lesions). Multiple promising serum biomarkers are identified, including the protein markers CA-125 and HE4 (which together achieve 95% specificity), microRNAs (miR-182, miR-200 family, and miR-205/let-7f combinations), and TP53 autoantibodies, with emerging urine-based biomarkers such as eosinophil protein X and osteopontin. Additionally, dysbiosis and pathogenic bacteria such as Salmonella enterica, Helicobacter pylori, and Fusobacteria upregulate oncogenic B-catenin signaling, linking microbiome alterations to malignant transformation in the context of endometriosis.

title: A Rare Case of Polyserositis With Elevated Serum Cancer Antigen (CA)-125 in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: A Case Report of Pseudo-Pseudo Meigs Syndrome
summary: This case report describes pseudo-pseudo Meigs syndrome (PPMS) in a systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) patient, characterized by the triad of ascites, pleural effusion, and elevated serum CA-125. The underlying mechanism involves SLE-induced immune dysregulation, where autoantibodies and Type I interferon activation trigger chronic inflammation and proinflammatory cytokine production by B lymphocytes, including interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor. These inflammatory cytokines activate mesothelial cells lining serous tissues, stimulating CA-125 production and promoting fluid leakage into body cavities, while the resulting ascites further irritates mesothelial cells in a self-perpetuating cycle. Notably, CA-125 is not specific to malignancy and is elevated across multiple non-malignant conditions including endometriosis, nephrotic syndrome, and other connective tissue diseases, making differential diagnosis challenging. The patient responded favorably to immunosuppressive therapy with corticosteroids (methylprednisolone and prednisone) combined with hydroxychloroquine and prednisolone, highlighting that steroid-based treatment effectively resolves PPMS by suppressing the underlying inflammatory cascade in SLE.

title: A Preoperative Scoring System Based on Clinical Characteristics and Hematologic Parameters for Differentiating Uterine Leiomyosarcoma from Leiomyoma
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a preoperative scoring system to differentiate uterine leiomyosarcoma (ULMS), a rare aggressive smooth muscle tumor, from benign leiomyoma. The scoring system integrates four independent clinical and hematologic risk factors: age greater than 48 years, tumor size exceeding 9.7 centimeters, systemic immune-inflammation index (SII) above 500, and Controlling Nutritional Status (CONUT) score of 3 or higher. These biomarkers reflect underlying biological mechanisms including systemic inflammation and malnutrition, which promote cancer progression through altered immune competence, metabolic dysfunction, and tumor microenvironment remodeling. The validated scoring system demonstrates strong diagnostic performance, with high specificity effectively ruling out non-ULMS cases and reducing unnecessary invasive evaluations in low-risk patients. This approach addresses the critical clinical challenge of preoperatively distinguishing aggressive sarcomas from benign uterine masses, reducing the risk of inadvertent morcellation and unexpected sarcoma dissemination during hysterectomy procedures.

title: Ovarian cancer and healthy lifestyle awareness among women of reproductive age
summary: This study examined how healthy lifestyle awareness and education level influence ovarian cancer knowledge among reproductive-aged women. Women with higher healthy lifestyle awareness scores and education levels demonstrated significantly greater awareness of ovarian cancer risk factors and symptoms, with education level and lifestyle awareness each independently predicting risk factor knowledge (β = 0.22, p < .001). Established ovarian cancer risk factors identified include genetic mutations (BRCA1/BRCA2), endometriosis, nulliparity, hormone replacement therapy, age over 40, obesity, and metabolic syndrome—with increased estrogen exposure linking these conditions to cancer development. Modifiable lifestyle factors including smoking cessation, reduced alcohol consumption, regular physical activity, healthy eating, and maintenance of healthy body weight all reduce ovarian cancer risk, while cigarette use paradoxically correlated negatively with risk factor awareness. The study emphasizes that women presenting with abdominal bloating, indigestion, pelvic pain, and fatigue warrant clinical attention for early diagnosis. Overall, these findings highlight that education and lifestyle awareness are key determinants in increasing early detection behaviors and informed prevention strategies among women at risk.

title: High-Mannose N‑Glycans To
summary:
# Summary

Epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) exhibits characteristic N-glycosylation alterations that serve as superior biomarkers compared to conventional CA125 measurement. The study demonstrates that EOC serum glycoproteins show decreased high-mannose and bisecting N-glycans alongside elevated tri- and tetraantennary complex-type sialylated and fucosylated N-glycans. The GLYCOV glycan-based index, which integrates these N-glycosylation changes, achieved higher sensitivity and specificity than CA125 for detecting primary EOC (AUC 0.94) and could accurately differentiate pretreatment EOC patients from those undergoing chemotherapy. Notably, high-mannose N-glycans increased during early chemotherapy cycles while complex-type sialylated fucosylated N-glycans decreased with continued treatment, suggesting N-glycan profiles track therapeutic response. These N-glycosylation patterns are driven by overexpression of specific glycosyltransferases and appear consistent across EOC patients of different ethnic backgrounds, supporting the utility of N-glycan profiling as a robust, ethnicity-independent biomarker for EOC detection and treatment monitoring.

title: Clinical significance of differential plasma proteins levels in the diagnosis of epithelial ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates differential plasma protein biomarkers for epithelial ovarian cancer diagnosis, particularly in the context of distinguishing malignant disease from benign ovarian masses and endometriosis. Multiple proteins show diagnostic significance: CA125 is elevated in epithelial ovarian cancer with 50-62% sensitivity but also increases in endometriosis, limiting specificity, while HE4 demonstrates higher specificity for ovarian cancer diagnosis and was FDA-approved as a monitoring marker. IGFBP2, which regulates glucose metabolism, cell survival, proliferation, and migration, achieved exceptional diagnostic performance with 98% sensitivity and 100% specificity for epithelial ovarian cancer detection and showed positive correlation with TSP1. SPP1, produced by multiple cell types and elevated across multiple cancer types, promotes cancer progression through vascular endothelial growth factor modulation and demonstrated 86.7% sensitivity and 100% specificity for ovarian cancer diagnosis. TSP1 plays a pivotal role in cell proliferation, adhesion, apoptosis, and angiogenesis and was significantly downregulated in ovarian cancer patients. When combined, SPP1 and CA125 or TSP1 achieve 93.3% sensitivity and 100% specificity for epithelial ovarian cancer detection, suggesting that multi-marker panels offer improved diagnostic accuracy over individual biomarkers.

title: Bidirectional Association between Atrial Fibrillation and Ovarian Cancer: Evidence from the UK Biobank and Mendelian Randomization
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a bidirectional association between atrial fibrillation (AF) and ovarian cancer, with AF increasing ovarian cancer risk by 30% and ovarian cancer increasing AF risk by 75%, particularly for serous histotypes. Mendelian randomization analysis identified nucleoporin 50 (NUP50) and synaptotagmin-like 2 (SYTL2) as shared genetic susceptibility factors for both conditions, and experimental knockdown of these genes inhibited ovarian cancer cell proliferation. The two conditions share multiple risk factors including age, obesity, metabolic syndrome, and hormonal changes, which may serve as mediators of this relationship. Additional mechanisms linking AF to ovarian cancer include prothrombotic states promoting angiogenesis, physical effects of ascites and pelvic masses increasing cardiac load, and chemotherapy-induced atrial remodeling from cisplatin, gemcitabine, and 5-fluorouracil. These findings suggest shared genetic architecture and common pathogenic pathways between AF and ovarian cancer, highlighting potential implications for patient surveillance and therapeutic strategies.

title: Role of Imaging Techniques in Ovarian Cancer Diagnosis: Current Approaches and Future Directions
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer remains a leading gynecological malignancy with poor prognosis, particularly high-grade serous carcinoma which accounts for approximately 70% of cases. Advanced imaging techniques, especially contrast-enhanced MRI paired with standardized scoring systems like O-RADS MRI, have substantially improved diagnostic accuracy in differentiating benign from malignant lesions, achieving sensitivity and specificity exceeding 90% and reducing unnecessary surgeries. While transvaginal ultrasound serves as the first-line diagnostic tool, up to 30% of cases yield inconclusive findings, making contrast-enhanced MRI the modality of choice for characterizing indeterminate ovarian masses. Emerging artificial intelligence approaches, including machine learning and deep learning algorithms applied to imaging data, show significant potential for further enhancing diagnostic precision and clinical decision-making. Treatment combines cytoreductive surgery with platinum-taxane chemotherapy, supplemented by targeted therapies such as PARP inhibitors for BRCA-mutated tumors and bevacizumab for anti-angiogenic effects. Integration of advanced imaging with AI and careful staging using contrast-enhanced CT represents the future direction for optimizing both diagnostic accuracy and surgical planning in ovarian cancer management.

title: Diagnostic Value of CA-125 and Other Tumor Biomarkers in Children with Suspected Malignancy: A Retrospective Cohort Study †
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that CA-125 serves as a significant diagnostic biomarker for malignancy in children, particularly among postpubertal females, with elevated levels showing a strong statistical association with cancer (p = 0.002). While CA-125 is classically linked to epithelial ovarian cancer (representing 37.5% of malignancies in CA-125-positive cases), the biomarker is also elevated in benign conditions including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, ovarian cysts, and mature cystic teratomas, as well as non-gynecologic malignancies such as lymphomas and neuroblastoma, reflecting tumor-related peritoneal irritation and cytokine-mediated serosal activation. In contrast, other established adult oncology biomarkers (CA 19-9, CA 15-3, and CEA) showed no significant association with malignancy in this pediatric cohort, suggesting that CA-125 may be more diagnostically relevant for pediatric tumors. Physiological factors including puberty, hormonal fluctuations, and inflammatory responses influence CA-125 levels in children, necessitating age-stratified interpretation. The study supports integrating CA-125 into a multimodal diagnostic framework combining clinical findings, imaging abnormalities, and laboratory markers, with particular utility in postpubertal girls suspected of malignancy.

title: Disposable electrochemical panel immunosensing systems for the simultaneous detection of potential biomarkers of ovarian cancer
summary: This study developed disposable electrochemical panel immunosensors for simultaneous detection of four protein biomarkers elevated in ovarian cancer: AGR2, glycodelin (GLY), folate receptor alpha (FOLR1), and soluble mesothelin-related protein (SMRP). All four biomarkers are significantly overexpressed in ovarian cancer patients compared to healthy tissues, with AGR2 also associated with poor prognosis across multiple cancer types. The immunosensors utilized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to enhance electron transfer and conductivity, EDC-NHS crosslinkers to facilitate antibody immobilization on electrode surfaces, and printed electrodes with superior electrochemical performance compared to commercial alternatives. Notably, glycodelin was also identified as elevated in endometriosis, suggesting potential clinical relevance beyond ovarian cancer detection. The multi-biomarker approach increases diagnostic sensitivity and specificity, as demonstrated by previous findings that SMRP combined with CA125 improves ovarian cancer diagnosis. This disposable panel system offers a cost-effective platform for simultaneous biomarker measurement in early-stage ovarian cancer diagnosis and patient monitoring.

title: Development and validation of a nomogram model for predicting natural pregnancy after hysteroscopy and laparoscopy in patients with tubal infertility complicated with pelvic endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a nomogram model to predict natural pregnancy outcomes following combined hysteroscopy and laparoscopy in patients with tubal infertility complicated by pelvic endometriosis. Four independent factors significantly influenced pregnancy success: advanced age (OR = 1.294), prolonged infertility duration (OR = 1.065), higher American Society for Reproductive Medicine stage (OR = 1.773), and elevated cancer antigen 125 levels (OR = 2.431). The surgical procedures improved pregnancy conditions by removing tubal adhesions and endometrial lesions, though long-standing endometriosis causes persistent pelvic microenvironment destruction through chronic inflammation and altered endometrial receptivity. Elevated CA125 contributes to infertility by inducing peritoneal immune dysregulation and macrophage overactivation, with CA125 serving as a biomarker of lesion activity and inflammatory status. For patients with markedly elevated CA125, postoperative GnRH-a therapy may further improve conception rates by suppressing lesion recurrence and optimizing the pelvic inflammatory environment.

title: Vascular graph network for ovarian lesion classification using optical-resolution photoacoustic microscopy☆
summary: Endometriosis presents a significant diagnostic challenge in ovarian imaging because its vascular characteristics closely mimic those of malignant lesions. Both endometriosis and ovarian cancer exhibit increased vascular supply and convoluted vessel morphology compared to normal ovaries, which typically show thin and sparsely distributed superficial vasculature. Machine learning approaches using vascular graph networks analyze these morphological alterations to differentiate ovarian lesions, but endometriosis remains particularly problematic, with approximately 27% of cases misclassified as cancer due to overlapping vascular signatures. The study demonstrates that vascularization alone is insufficient for distinguishing endometriosis from malignancy, as benign cystic lesions also frequently present large and malformed blood vessels that complicate classification. This diagnostic difficulty has clinical implications, as many women undergo unnecessary oophorectomies when more conservative treatment options could be pursued. Improved classification algorithms must account for the heterogeneous clinical presentations of endometriosis and the broad spectrum of its vascular manifestations to reduce both false-positive cancer diagnoses and overtreatment.

title: Increased first-trimester CA125 levels associated with the development of preeclampsia
summary: Elevated first-trimester serum CA125 (carbohydrate antigen 125), a glycoprotein produced by epithelial cells, emerged as a robust independent predictor of preeclampsia and was particularly elevated in cases complicated by fetal growth restriction. CA125 levels were significantly associated with both systolic and diastolic blood pressure trajectories throughout pregnancy, and when combined with clinical factors such as maternal age, parity, and mean arterial pressure, improved prediction accuracy to an AUC of 0.841. The association between CA125 and preeclampsia likely reflects placental dysfunction, which initiates systemic vascular inflammation and endothelial injury—pathophysiological mechanisms shared with cardiovascular diseases where CA125 serves as a biomarker of congestion and inflammatory processes. These findings position CA125 as a promising practical biomarker for early risk stratification and identification of preeclampsia during the first trimester, potentially facilitating timely clinical intervention to reduce maternal and fetal mortality and morbidity. Although CA125 is traditionally recognized as a tumor marker for ovarian cancer, this study demonstrates its clinical utility in pregnancy-related vascular pathology through its detection in multiple gestational tissues including amniotic fluid and decidual tissues.

title: Evaluation of drug options for epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) treatment using the analytical hierarchy process improves decision-making transparency
summary:
# Summary

Epithelial ovarian cancer encompasses distinct molecular subtypes with different genetic drivers and treatment responses. High-grade serous carcinoma, accounting for 75% of cases, is characterized by p53 and BRCA mutations, while Type I cancers such as endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas are associated with endometriosis and harbor KRAS/BRAF mutations. BRCA1/2 mutations confer homologous recombination deficiency, predicting sensitivity to both platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors like olaparib. The PI3K pathway, frequently upregulated in EOC, drives chemoresistance and cell proliferation, though its inhibition risks genomic instability. Current therapeutic approaches include platinum agents (carboplatin, cisplatin) and taxanes (paclitaxel, docetaxel) as standard chemotherapy, alongside emerging strategies with bevacizumab for angiogenesis inhibition and PARP inhibitors for HRD-positive tumors. Treatment selection prioritizes therapeutic efficacy and safety profile, with tumor biomarkers and molecular profiling essential for personalizing therapy and optimizing patient outcomes.

title: Global, regional, and national burden of ovarian cancer, 1990–2021, and projections to 2050: a cross-sectional analysis of the Global Burden of Disease Study 2021
summary:
# Summary

Between 1990 and 2021, ovarian cancer burden demonstrated significant global heterogeneity, with age-standardized incidence, mortality, and disability-adjusted life year rates strongly associated with advancing age and social development index levels. High body mass index and occupational asbestos exposure emerged as primary risk factors, while transvaginal ultrasound, computed tomography, and the Risk of Malignancy Index—incorporating serum cancer antigen 125 measurements—serve as key diagnostic and staging tools. Developed European countries and regions with high social development indices, particularly Central Europe and the United Arab Emirates, exhibited the highest incidence rates, whereas Western Sub-Saharan Africa and low social development index regions showed notably lower rates. Population growth and aging contributed substantially to rising incidence rates, though epidemiological changes in high-development regions produced declines in multiple age-standardized rates, with Australia and Australasia demonstrating the most notable improvements while Andean Latin America and Ecuador experienced the steepest increases. Importantly, later birth cohorts exhibited lower age-standardized rates than earlier cohorts, suggesting potential shifts in exposure or protective factors across generations.

title: Ovarian cancer metastasis to axillary node without breast involvement: a rare case
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with isolated axillary lymph node metastasis without breast involvement, representing stage IV disease at initial diagnosis. The patient presented with markedly elevated Ca-125 (2563), and immunohistochemical markers PAX-8 and WT1 confirmed ovarian origin rather than breast primary, with p53 mutations characteristic of high-grade serous carcinomas. The multidisciplinary treatment strategy included neoadjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel, interval cytoreductive surgery with pelvic and retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, and secondary cytoreduction with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) and axillary lymph node dissection, followed by maintenance bevacizumab and additional salvage chemotherapy lines. This case highlights the importance of careful evaluation of axillary lymphadenopathy to distinguish ovarian cancer metastases from breast primaries, particularly given that serous ovarian carcinomas commonly metastasize to liver, peritoneum, and para-aortic nodes, though supradiaphragmatic metastases remain uncommon. Optimal cytoreduction achieving minimal residual disease significantly improves cure rates and survival outcomes in advanced ovarian cancer.

title: A diagnostic scoring system for differentiating between benign and malignant cystic ovarian tumors, utilizing imaging features and biomarkers
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a diagnostic scoring system combining serum biomarkers and imaging features to differentiate malignant from benign cystic ovarian tumors. Elevated levels of CA125 and CA15-3 were significantly associated with malignant cystic ovarian tumors, while CA19-9, AFP, and CEA showed no discriminatory value. Multiple imaging characteristics including septum thickness ≥4 mm, moderate to prominent enhancement, irregular or lobulated margins, thickened septa, and increased solid component proportion were strongly associated with malignancy, contrasting with the smooth margins and well-defined interfaces typical of benign lesions. The integrated scoring system utilizing these four independent predictive factors—CA125 elevation combined with key imaging features—proved reliable and convenient for distinguishing malignant from benign cystic ovarian tumors. This multimodal approach provides clinicians with a reproducible method for improving early diagnosis and patient risk stratification in ovarian cancer.

title: Diagnostic tests for ovarian cancer in premenopausal women with non-specific symptoms (ROCkeTS): prospective, multicentre, cohort study
summary:
# Summary

The ROCkeTS study evaluated multiple diagnostic tests for triaging ovarian cancer in premenopausal women presenting with non-specific symptoms such as abdominal distension, pelvic pain, and fatigue. Among the tested biomarkers and algorithms—including CA 125, Risk of Malignancy Index 1, Risk of Malignancy Algorithm, and various IOTA (International Ovarian Tumor Analysis) models—the IOTA ADNEX ultrasound model demonstrated superior diagnostic performance with 89.1% sensitivity and a C-index of 0.89, substantially outperforming the widely used Risk of Malignancy Index 1 (42.6% sensitivity). While CA 125 alone showed limited utility due to elevation in benign conditions including endometriosis, the Risk of Malignancy Algorithm combining CA 125 and human epididymis protein 4 achieved 79.2% sensitivity. The findings support adoption of IOTA ADNEX at 10% probability threshold as the new standard for ovarian cancer triage in premenopausal women, offering improved early detection rates that could reduce emergency presentations and mortality associated with delayed diagnosis.

title: Incidence of different histologic subtype of ovarian cancer in China: a population-based cancer registry study
summary: This population-based registry study from China reveals distinct geographic and demographic patterns in ovarian cancer histologic subtypes, with important implications for understanding endometriosis-associated malignancies. Serous and clear cell carcinomas predominate in northeastern and northern China with higher incidence in economically developed regions, while endometrioid and mucinous subtypes show different geographic distributions. Endometriosis emerges as a significant risk factor specifically for clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas, alongside older age at menopause, whereas reproductive factors and tubal sterilization differentially influence these subtypes. Environmental and lifestyle factors also demonstrate subtype-specific associations, with smoking correlating with mucinous carcinoma risk and protective effects observed for black tea and calcium intake. The findings underscore that clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas, the histologic subtypes most strongly linked to endometriosis, represent distinct disease entities with unique epidemiologic profiles and risk factor patterns that differ markedly from high-grade serous carcinomas.

title: Multidimensional liquid biopsy in bladder cancer: advances in circulating tumor cells, circulating tumor DNA, exosomes, and metabolomics
summary:
# Summary

This review examines liquid biopsy approaches for bladder cancer diagnosis and prognosis, focusing on circulating tumor cells (CTCs), circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), exosomes, and metabolomic markers. Key risk factors for bladder cancer include tobacco smoking, occupational chemical exposure, chronic bladder inflammation, and cyclophosphamide exposure, with hematuria as the primary clinical symptom. The presence of CTCs and ctDNA in patient blood correlates with disease recurrence, metastatic progression, and reduced overall survival, making them valuable prognostic indicators for treatment response and pathological staging. Exosomes and their cargo—including specific microRNAs (miR-375, miR-152-3p, miR-184) and proteins (HOTAIR, circTRPS1, MMP7, MMP12)—regulate tumor immune evasion, angiogenesis, and chemoresistance, offering potential non-invasive urinary biomarkers for disease stratification. Current therapeutic strategies range from transurethral resection and bacillus Calmette-Guérin for non-muscle-invasive disease to radical cystectomy, cisplatin-based chemotherapy, and checkpoint inhibitors for advanced disease, with liquid biopsy biomarkers increasingly important for treatment selection and monitoring in metastatic bladder cancer patients.

title: MOCRA: A multi-algorithm clinical decision support system for the early detection of ovarian cancer
summary: MOCRA is a multi-algorithm clinical decision support system that harmonizes six validated diagnostic approaches—NICE NG12, HSE pathways, IOTA Simple Rules, O-RADS v2022, RMI2, and ROMA—to improve early detection of ovarian cancer in patients presenting with adnexal masses. The system integrates symptom-based risk assessment, ultrasound morphology, and serum biomarkers (CA-125 and HE4) while accounting for menopausal status to stratify patients into risk categories. MOCRA demonstrated superior diagnostic performance compared to individual algorithms, achieving perfect sensitivity by reducing false negatives while maintaining high specificity, with all malignant cases assigned to the high-risk category and no malignancies occurring in the low-risk category. The system addresses a critical clinical gap where early symptoms such as bloating, abdominal discomfort, and early satiety are frequently misattributed to benign conditions, leading to diagnostic delays. Notably, early detection significantly improves five-year survival rates to over 90% for stage I disease, contrasting sharply with the poor prognosis of advanced-stage presentations where most patients are currently diagnosed. MOCRA's high clinician usability and diagnostic consistency suggest substantial clinical utility in enabling earlier cancer detection and more informed referral decisions.

title: Hereditary ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Mutations in BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes are the primary drivers of hereditary ovarian cancer, accounting for the majority of cases and conferring a 35–60% lifetime risk when inherited in germline form. These tumor suppressor genes function in DNA double-strand break repair through homologous recombination; mutations lead to loss of heterozygosity and accumulation of DNA damage, ultimately promoting carcinogenesis. BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations demonstrate highest frequency in high-grade serous ovarian cancer and also elevate risk for breast, peritoneal, and fallopian tube cancers. Beyond BRCA genes, approximately 5–10% of hereditary ovarian cancer patients carry pathogenic variants in moderate or low-penetrance genes involved in homologous recombination, including ATM, CHEK2, PALB2, BARD1, RAD51C, RAD51D, and BRIP1, as well as Lynch syndrome-associated mismatch repair genes. Somatic BRCA mutations occur in 5–9% of sporadic ovarian cancers, indicating these pathways remain critical even outside the hereditary context. Population-specific founder mutations exist, such as c.185delAG and c.5382insC in Ashkenazi Jewish populations and c.5266dupC in Polish populations.

title: Survival prediction and risk stratification in R0-resected ovarian cancer: a multi-modal deep learning approach
summary:
# Summary

This study presents OvcaSurvivor, a multi-modal deep learning framework that integrates whole-slide histopathology images, ultrasound imaging, and clinical data to improve survival prediction and risk stratification in R0-resected ovarian cancer patients. The model achieved superior performance compared to single-modality approaches, with concordance indices of 0.81 internally and 0.76-0.70 on external validation cohorts, effectively stratifying patients into high- and low-risk groups with significant survival differences. Whole-slide image features contributed most heavily to predictions, while the model dynamically weighted imaging versus clinical modalities depending on disease stage—prioritizing clinical indicators in early-stage disease and ultrasound/histological features in advanced stages through a gating mechanism. Key pathological features driving the model's predictions included tumor-stroma interface characteristics, nuclear pleomorphism, and solid tumor components. The framework demonstrates that multimodal fusion addresses limitations of traditional staging systems like FIGO classification by capturing tumor heterogeneity at both cellular and macroscopic scales, advancing precision oncology approaches for improved postoperative management of ovarian cancer patients.

title: Establishing reference interval of serum CA-125 for the healthy Han population in China
summary:
# Summary

This study establishes reference intervals for serum CA-125 in a healthy Han Chinese population, revealing that CA-125 serves as a clinically important biomarker despite its lack of cancer specificity. CA-125 is primarily associated with epithelial ovarian cancer detection and treatment monitoring, where decreasing levels indicate favorable responses while increasing levels signal treatment resistance or disease recurrence. However, the marker is also significantly elevated in multiple benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids, as well as in physiological states such as menstruation and pregnancy and non-malignant conditions like heart failure. The study demonstrates important population-specific variations, with females showing significantly higher CA-125 levels than males, and women exhibiting an age-dependent negative correlation where concentrations decrease with advancing age. These findings underscore the necessity of establishing population and age-appropriate reference intervals for CA-125 interpretation to improve diagnostic accuracy and reduce false-positive results in clinical practice.

title: Mendelian randomization study of GLP-1R effects on ovarian cancer subtypes mediated by metabolic factors
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor (GLP-1R) expression in the pancreas is associated with reduced risk of overall ovarian cancer, with particularly strong protection against endometrioid ovarian cancer (ENOC), with odds ratios of 0.8 for gene expression and 0.13 for a validated GLP-1R splicing event. The protective mechanism appears to be mediated through metabolic and body composition factors, including 18:2 linoleic acid, suggesting that GLP-1R's established benefits in weight management and metabolic control may extend to ovarian cancer prevention. While GLP-1R agonists are currently used therapeutically for type 2 diabetes and obesity and show potential for reducing ovarian cancer risk, the study identifies previously unreported genetic variants (rs77247401 and rs56159231) associated with ovarian cancer susceptibility. The findings suggest that GLP-1R-based interventions warrant further investigation as potential preventive strategies specifically for ENOC, though the multifaceted effects of GLP-1R on cancer progression across different malignancies and potential associations with thyroid cancer require careful evaluation in future clinical trials.

title: Development and validation of gene expression-based signature for high-grade serous ovarian cancer
summary: This study developed and validated a 10-gene expression signature for diagnosing high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) by identifying genes involved in critical cellular processes including DNA repair (EXO1, RAD50), cell replication (CDCA5), lysosome activity (VPS33B, PPT2), gene expression regulation (LUC7L2, ZFPL1, TCEAL4), and cell-to-cell signaling (GRB7, PKP3). The signature genes showed significantly altered expression patterns in HGSOC compared to benign tissues, with PKP3 markedly elevated and RAD50 substantially reduced, achieving strong diagnostic accuracy (AUC = 0.815 for 5-year survival prediction). Beyond diagnosis, three genes—RAD50, VPS33B, and GRB7—correlated with disease stage and overall survival, with RAD50 and VPS33B showing negative correlation with advancing FIGO stage, suggesting potential prognostic value. TCEAL4 expression was particularly associated with tumor grade, being significantly reduced in grade 3 cases. This molecular signature complements existing protein biomarkers like CA125 and HE4, offering improved stratification for patient management and treatment selection, particularly for identifying candidates for PARP inhibitor therapy in homologous recombination-deficient HGSOC.

title: Epidemiological Profile, Clinical Characteristics, and Management of Ovarian Cancer in Eastern India: A Hospital-Based Study
summary: This hospital-based study from Eastern India identifies multiple risk factors and clinical characteristics associated with ovarian cancer, with particular emphasis on endometriosis as a significant contributor. Endometriosis increases ovarian cancer risk 1.5-2 fold, especially for endometrioid and clear-cell subtypes, while established risk factors including advancing age, nulliparity, infertility, family history, and genetic mutations (particularly BRCA1/2 and Lynch syndrome-associated genes) are strongly associated with disease development. Protective factors include breastfeeding, oral contraceptive use, and tubal ligation, the latter showing specific protective effects against serous epithelial cancers. The study found that serous adenocarcinoma was the predominant histological subtype (58.5%), occurring more frequently in postmenopausal women, while germ cell tumors predominated in younger age groups. Diagnostic and prognostic markers including CA-125 (median 552.5 U/mL in 95% of patients) and Risk of Malignancy Index scores demonstrated high utility in patient triage and disease monitoring. Management approaches included cytoreductive surgery, neoadjuvant chemotherapy with interval debulking, palliative chemotherapy, and fertility-sparing surgery, with socioeconomic status identified as a barrier to early diagnosis.

title: Alterations in lipid metabolism and blood profile in gynecological cancers – potential strategies in diagnosis and treatment
summary:
# Summary

Lipid metabolism reprogramming plays a critical role in gynecological cancer development, with dysregulation of fatty acid uptake, β-oxidation, and upregulation of lipogenic enzymes driving tumor progression. Serum lipid profiles serve as diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers across endometrial, cervical, and ovarian cancers, characterized by elevated total cholesterol, LDL-C, and triglycerides alongside reduced HDL-C levels, which consistently associates with increased mortality risk. The oxysterol 27-hydroxycholesterol promotes endometrial cancer cell proliferation through Liver X receptor activation, while cholesterol transport mechanisms involving LDL receptor overexpression contribute to drug resistance in epithelial ovarian cancer. Multiple lipid metabolism inhibitors—including FASN inhibitors (TVB-2640), SCD1 inhibitors (CAY10566, MF-438), and CPT1A inhibitors (etomoxir, perhexiline)—represent emerging therapeutic strategies, with SCD1 inhibition sensitizing tumors to ferroptosis. Fatty acid composition analysis reveals cancer-specific alterations, including decreased linoleic acid and DHA in cervical cancer, and elevated saturated fatty acids and DPA in both cervical and ovarian cancers, establishing lipid signatures as potential diagnostic tools for gynecological malignancies.

title: Serum long noncoding RNA FAM83H-AS1 serves as a potential noninvasive diagnostic biomarker for ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies serum FAM83H-AS1, a long noncoding RNA, as a promising noninvasive diagnostic biomarker for ovarian cancer with superior performance compared to established markers. Serum FAM83H-AS1 levels are significantly elevated in ovarian cancer patients compared to benign ovarian disease and healthy controls, and correlate strongly with advanced TNM stage, lymphatic metastasis, distal metastasis, and peritoneal involvement. As an independent diagnostic predictor, FAM83H-AS1 alone achieves an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.824 for ovarian cancer detection, and when combined with the ROMA panel (CA125 and HE4), diagnostic accuracy improves substantially to an AUC of 0.909. Additionally, elevated FAM83H-AS1 is associated with poor progression-free survival and serves as a tumor-derived marker, as evidenced by significant postoperative decline in serum levels. The findings suggest that serum FAM83H-AS1 has clinical utility for both early-stage ovarian cancer detection and monitoring disease stage and progression, particularly when integrated with traditional tumor markers.

title: Serum levels of alpha1-antitrypsin isoforms in patients with ovarian clear cell carcinoma: An exploratory study
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

This exploratory study investigates serum alpha-1 antitrypsin isoforms (isoAAT) as a potential biomarker for ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), a malignancy frequently associated with and potentially caused by endometriosis. The researchers found that serum isoAAT levels were significantly elevated in OCCC patients compared to those with benign ovarian tumors or endometriosis alone, and correlated positively with CA125 levels. Notably, debulking surgery resulted in a significant decrease in serum isoAAT levels, and patients with preoperative isoAAT levels exceeding 130 ng/mL exhibited less favorable cancer-specific survival, suggesting prognostic value. These findings support the diagnostic utility of serum isoAAT in distinguishing OCCC from benign disease and warrant larger investigations into its role as a biomarker for endometriosis-associated OCCC, potentially addressing the limited clinical value of CA125 in this malignancy.

title: Diagnostic accuracy of artificial intelligence in detection of ovarian cancer—a pilot study
summary: This pilot study evaluated artificial intelligence approaches for preoperative diagnosis of ovarian cancer by integrating radiological imaging findings, blood biomarkers, and clinical parameters. Machine learning algorithms, particularly random forest and support vector machines, achieved high diagnostic accuracy with AUROC values of 0.92 and 0.90 respectively, while deep learning models ResNet50 and Inception Net v3 demonstrated superior performance with AUROC of 0.99. Radiological features including locularity, peritoneal metastases, papillary projections, septations, and ascites were strongly associated with epithelial ovarian cancer, while O-RADS score, tumor size, and laterality characterized benign cases. Serum biomarkers CA125, CEA, and CA19.9, along with demographic factors such as age and menopausal status, significantly contributed to cancer detection. The study identified distinct pathological subtypes, with high-grade serous carcinoma representing 52% of malignant cases and serous cystadenoma comprising 40% of benign tumors. These findings demonstrate that multimodal AI integration substantially improves the accuracy of ovarian cancer preoperative characterization, potentially reducing diagnostic uncertainty and improving patient outcomes.

title: A panel of machine learning approaches for diagnostic model development and validation in ovarian cancer
summary: This study developed and validated machine learning models for ovarian cancer diagnosis, addressing the diagnostic limitations of conventional biomarkers like CA125, which shows insufficient sensitivity and specificity and is also elevated in benign conditions such as endometriosis. The Lasso + NaiveBayes model demonstrated superior diagnostic performance by identifying twelve key genes (Mgenes) associated with ovarian cancer, with ceruloplasmin (CP) showing the highest diagnostic value (AUC = 0.966). These Mgenes function as both diagnostic biomarkers and mediators of tumorigenesis, with functional enrichment analyses revealing their involvement in cell cycle regulation and DNA replication pathways. The identified genes exhibited significant correlations with immune microenvironment components, including associations between MAOB and regulatory T cells and between STAR and CD8+ T cells, suggesting the immune system's role in ovarian cancer biology. Early detection is critical, as patients diagnosed at early stages (FIGO I–II) achieve 5-year survival rates exceeding 90%, whereas approximately 70% of patients present with advanced-stage disease associated with poor outcomes. This machine learning approach offers a robust, non-invasive alternative to imaging techniques and invasive histopathological examination for ovarian cancer diagnosis.

title: Distinct immune microenvironments in ovarian cancer subtypes indicate potential for immunotherapies
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies distinct immune microenvironments across ovarian cancer subtypes, revealing critical implications for immunotherapy responsiveness. CD8+ T cell infiltration and proximity to tumor cells associates with improved prognosis, yet tumors employ multiple immune evasion mechanisms including upregulation of checkpoint molecules (PD-L1, B7-H3, CTLA-4) and immunomodulatory enzymes (IDO1, ARG1). Different histological subtypes display characteristic immune signatures: low-grade serous tumors feature STING association, mucinous cancers show elevated CTLA-4 and PD-L1, clear cell carcinomas express CD40, IDO1 and VISTA, while endometrioid tumors express B7-H3. Spatially, immune markers localize distinctly—most T cells and myeloid cells concentrate in stromal regions, whereas regulatory T cell markers and IDO1 preferentially locate in tumor regions, suggesting a spatially segregated immunosuppressive microenvironment. Type II ovarian cancers demonstrate superior immune activation with higher CD8+ T cells, dendritic cell markers, and cytotoxic granzyme B compared to Type I tumors. These findings support combination immunotherapy approaches, as single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 blockade shows modest responses, while combination strategies with PARP inhibitors or anti-angiogenic agents demonstrate improved efficacy.

title: Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) in ovarian cancer: transforming detection, treatment, and prevention
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer remains a highly lethal malignancy characterized by advanced-stage diagnosis, chemoresistance, and molecular heterogeneity, particularly in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC). Traditional biomarkers such as CA-125 and BRCA1/2 status provide only partial insights into disease biology and exhibit limited sensitivity in early detection, highlighting the need for more comprehensive diagnostic approaches. Artificial intelligence and machine learning models, particularly radiomics-based prognostic value (RPV) and enhanced RPV (eRPV) models, significantly improve prediction of treatment response, recurrence, and survival outcomes by integrating multimodal data including imaging, clinical records, and genomic profiles. These advanced models identify molecular features such as BRCA mutation status, homologous recombination deficiency, and copy-number alterations associated with chemoresistance, enabling personalized treatment selection including platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitor eligibility. Deep learning architectures including convolutional neural networks, graph neural networks, and transformer-based models enhance the analysis of complex, multi-layered datasets while explainable AI approaches ensure clinical transparency. Overall, multi-modal AI-driven clinical decision support systems that integrate diverse patient data outperform single-modality approaches and represent a transformative approach to precision medicine in ovarian cancer detection, treatment prediction, and prevention.

title: Integrative multi-stage deep learning framework for ovarian tumor ultrasound classification with explainability and confidence estimation
summary: This study presents an integrative deep learning framework for classifying ovarian tumors on ultrasound imaging while addressing the clinical challenge of distinguishing benign from malignant lesions, with particular relevance to endometriosis-associated ovarian pathology. The research identifies multiple risk factors for ovarian cancer including BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, endometriosis, obesity, and post-menopausal hormone therapy, while acknowledging that serum biomarkers like CA-125 have limited diagnostic specificity. The proposed EfficientOvaNet system employs a two-branch EfficientNet-B3 architecture combined with advanced segmentation models (U-Net++, DeepLabV3+, SegFormer, DANet, and PSANet) to improve diagnostic accuracy and reduce subjective interpretation bias in ultrasound-based tumor classification. The framework incorporates sophisticated preprocessing techniques including data augmentation, focused sampling, and weighted Focal Loss to manage class imbalance in the training dataset. The study validates its approach using the publicly available Multi-Modality Ovarian Tumor Ultrasound (MMOTU) dataset, with specialized models achieving notable performance metrics such as DANet's 71.65% average accuracy and PSANet's 96.33% IoU for chocolate cyst segmentation. These results demonstrate that deep learning can substantially enhance the diagnostic accuracy of ultrasound imaging for ovarian tumor classification while providing explainability and confidence estimates for clinical decision-making.

title: Hypertension as a Predictor of Ovarian Cancer Risk: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
summary: This meta-analysis examined hypertension as a predictor of ovarian cancer risk and identified multiple factors affecting ovarian cancer susceptibility. Contrary to initial hypothesis, the meta-analysis found no statistically significant association between hypertension and ovarian cancer risk, though hypertension does correlate with other cancer types including endometrial, breast, prostate, and colorectal cancers. For ovarian cancer specifically, established risk factors include obesity (BMI ≥25 kg/m²), nulliparity (1.43-fold increased risk), BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, endometriosis, diabetes, tobacco use, and hormone replacement therapy. Protective factors include oral contraceptive use, metformin treatment, and parity, with pregnancy and breastfeeding reducing ovarian cancer incidence. The study highlights that ovarian cancer risk is primarily mediated through hormonal and metabolic pathways rather than the hypertensive mechanisms (vascular endothelial growth factor, extracellular matrix remodeling, renin-aldosterone-angiotensin system, and matrix metalloproteinases) that link hypertension to other malignancies.

title: Clinical perspectives of BET inhibition in ovarian cancer
summary: BET proteins function as epigenetic regulators that bind acetylated histones to control gene transcription, including the expression of oncogenic drivers like MYC and Neuregulin 1 in ovarian cancer. BRD4, the most studied BET family member, is frequently amplified in high-grade serous carcinomas, establishing BET proteins as promising therapeutic targets. BET inhibitors effectively suppress ovarian cancer progression by blocking cell proliferation and dissemination through inhibition of critical signaling pathways including FOXM1 and JAK/STAT. Additionally, BET inhibition substantially enhances sensitivity of resistant ovarian cancer cells to conventional chemotherapies, including cisplatin and PARP inhibitors, suggesting synergistic potential when combined with standard treatments. Clinical development of BET inhibitors is currently underway in Phase I/II trials evaluating their safety and efficacy in solid tumors, with preclinical evidence supporting their application in ovarian cancer treatment and their capacity to enhance antitumor activity in combination regimens.

title: Targeting VEGF, PARP, and FRα Pathways in Ovarian Cancer: Clinical Advances with Bevacizumab, Rucaparib, and Mirvetuximab Soravtansine
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews three targeted therapeutic advances for ovarian cancer that exploit distinct molecular pathways. Bevacizumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting VEGF-A, inhibits tumor angiogenesis by preventing VEGF receptor activation on endothelial cells, improving progression-free and overall survival when combined with platinum-taxane chemotherapy or carboplatin-gemcitabine. Rucaparib and other PARP inhibitors exploit homologous recombination deficiency in BRCA-mutated tumors through synthetic lethality, with demonstrated efficacy in both treatment and maintenance settings. Mirvetuximab soravtansine represents a folate receptor alpha–targeted immunoconjugate effective against platinum-resistant disease with high FRα expression. The article emphasizes that endometrioid and clear cell ovarian carcinomas, which are associated with endometriosis, harbor distinct mutations (PTEN/ARID1A and PI3K/AKT pathway alterations, respectively) that influence treatment selection. Emerging data support combination strategies integrating bevacizumab with PARP inhibitors, as VEGF inhibition-induced hypoxia may downregulate homologous recombination repair genes and enhance PARP sensitivity, though overlapping toxicities including hypertension, anemia, fatigue, and gastrointestinal effects require careful monitoring.

title: The cervico‐vaginal DNA methylation WID‐qEC test: An epigenetic marker associated with ovarian cancer in the absence of endometrial and cervical cancer
summary: The WID-qEC test, a DNA methylation-based biomarker originally developed for endometrial cancer detection, demonstrates significant association with ovarian cancer presence when applied to cervico-vaginal samples in the absence of endometrial or cervical malignancy. Among 1,269 women screened, 54% of WID-qEC positive results were from ovarian cancer patients, with an adjusted odds ratio of 2.93, making ovarian cancer presence the strongest predictor of test positivity. The test's association with ovarian cancer appears mediated through cumulative ovulatory exposure, as WID-qEC positivity correlated with higher lifetime ovulatory cycles (adjusted OR 2.67), a well-established ovarian cancer risk factor, and this relationship was particularly pronounced in serous epithelial ovarian cancers. The mechanistic basis may involve tumor DNA drainage through the fallopian tubes and uterus, explaining detection of ovarian-derived methylation patterns in cervico-vaginal specimens. These findings suggest the WID-qEC test could serve as a biological readout of ovulatory burden in women predisposed to ovarian cancer and support combining it with plasma-based cell-free DNA methylation assays for improved detection in women presenting with abnormal or postmenopausal bleeding.

title: Emerging diagnostic, prognostic and therapeutic biomarkers for ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Recent advances in genomic, proteomic, and metabolomic technologies have identified multiple molecular biomarkers that enable early detection and personalized treatment of ovarian cancer. BRCA mutations represent a particularly significant diagnostic biomarker strongly associated with ovarian cancer risk and prognosis. Multi-biomarker panels, when rigorously evaluated for sensitivity and specificity, show promise for improving early cancer detection and enabling tailored therapeutic approaches. These biomarker-driven strategies represent an emerging paradigm shift toward precision oncology in ovarian cancer management, allowing clinicians to identify at-risk patients earlier and customize treatment based on individual tumor biology. The integration of multiple biomarker signatures rather than single markers substantially enhances diagnostic accuracy and clinical utility. Continued validation and standardization of these biomarker panels will be essential for translating these discoveries into routine clinical practice for improved patient outcomes.

title: Trousseau's Syndrome With Non-bacterial Thrombotic Endocarditis Associated With Endometrioid Carcinoma in a Young Patient
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents a rare but clinically significant association between endometrioid ovarian carcinoma arising from endometriosis and Trousseau's syndrome in a young patient. A 29-year-old woman presented with multiple life-threatening thrombotic complications including deep venous thrombosis, bilateral pulmonary embolism, ischemic stroke, and non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis, which were subsequently found to be caused by an underlying stage IC2 endometrioid carcinoma. The malignancy-associated hypercoagulable state is driven by cancer cell production of procoagulant factors including tissue factor, cancer procoagulant, and coagulation factors VII and VIII, with elevated biomarkers such as D-dimer, CRP, and cancer antigen 125 serving as indicators of thrombotic risk. While Trousseau's syndrome is predominantly associated with adenocarcinomas and clear cell ovarian tumors, its association with endometrioid carcinoma is exceptionally rare. The case underscores the critical clinical importance of maintaining high suspicion for occult malignancy in young patients presenting with unexplained or recurrent thrombotic phenomena, particularly when non-bacterial valvular vegetations are present, and demonstrates that heparin-based anticoagulation and direct oral anticoagulants are preferred over warfarin for managing cancer-associated thrombosis.

title: Clinical value of clinical symptoms, serum biomarkers, and coagulation parameters for ovarian endometrioma diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometriomas can be effectively diagnosed using a combined model integrating clinical symptoms, serum biomarkers, and coagulation parameters. Dysmenorrhea, elevated serum CA125 and HE4 levels, and prolonged prothrombin time (PT) are all significantly associated with endometriosis, with dysmenorrhea occurring in 68% of endometriosis patients compared to 40% of controls. The underlying mechanism involves ectopic endometrial tissue triggering chronic local inflammation, neutrophil recruitment, and coagulation factor consumption from repeated bleeding and tissue damage. A multiparameter diagnostic model combining dysmenorrhea, CA125, HE4, and PT achieved superior diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.925) compared to individual markers, with CA125 alone showing an AUC of 0.895. Additional inflammatory markers including white blood cell count, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, and platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio were also elevated in endometriosis patients. This non-invasive diagnostic approach provides a practical complementary tool to imaging that may enhance preoperative decision-making and differentiate ovarian endometriomas from other benign ovarian cysts such as mature teratomas and cystadenomas.

title: Women with suspected diagnosis of ovarian cancer in Ghana: how much do we know about them?
summary:
# Summary

This Ghanaian study characterized clinical and demographic profiles of women with suspected ovarian cancer, identifying 369 malignant tumors among 495 symptomatic patients, predominantly epithelial ovarian cancer (199 cases). Women with ovarian cancer presented with distinct clinical signatures including advanced age (median 50 years), postmenopausal status, and characteristic symptoms such as abdominal distension, ascites, and elevated serum CA-125 levels (median 226.5 U/ml), alongside imaging features including solid tumor components and vascular flow on Doppler ultrasound. Associated comorbidities including anemia, hypertension, and diabetes were significantly more prevalent in cancer cases, while genetic risk factors including BRCA1/BRCA2 and homologous recombination repair gene mutations were highlighted as important biomarkers. Socioeconomic factors influenced cancer presentation, with unemployed women and those traveling greater distances to seek care being more likely to have cancer, suggesting delayed diagnosis in resource-limited settings. The findings underscore the importance of integrating clinical, biochemical, and imaging biomarkers for early cancer detection while addressing access barriers to timely healthcare in sub-Saharan African populations.

title: The Impact of Osteopontin and Galectin-7 on the Preoperative Diagnosis of Ovarian Tumors: A Case–Control Study
summary: This case–control study investigated osteopontin and galectin-7 as potential biomarkers for distinguishing ovarian cancer from benign ovarian tumors and healthy controls. While both proteins are biologically implicated in ovarian tumorigenesis—osteopontin promotes cancer cell survival and invasion through PI3K/Akt signaling activation and modulates cell adhesion and angiogenesis, while galectin-7 is associated with aggressive tumor behavior and high-grade disease—serum levels of neither biomarker showed statistically significant differences between healthy controls and ovarian cancer cases. The study found that galectin-7 concentrations were higher in premenopausal women and inversely correlated with age, whereas CA-125 remained the only biomarker significantly associated with malignancy in the cohort. These findings suggest that despite their established roles in tumor progression and their expression patterns in tissue samples, circulating osteopontin and galectin-7 levels lack sufficient diagnostic specificity for ovarian cancer detection. The results highlight the challenge of translating mechanistically relevant biomarkers into clinically useful diagnostic tools and underscore the continued dependence on conventional markers like CA-125 for ovarian cancer screening.

title: Infertility and Risk of Ovarian Cancer in the Women’s Health Initiative
summary: This Women's Health Initiative study reveals a nuanced relationship between infertility, endometriosis, and ovarian cancer risk that varies by histological subtype. Women reporting infertility demonstrated a 90% increased risk specifically for endometrioid and clear cell ovarian cancers, while no significant association was observed for high-grade serous or mucinous subtypes, suggesting that reproductive factors differentially influence ovarian cancer pathogenesis. Endometriosis emerged as a particularly important risk factor, conferring a two to threefold increased risk for endometrioid, clear cell, and low-grade serous ovarian cancers, with the condition contributing to infertility itself. Female factor infertility showed the strongest associations with endometrioid and clear cell cancers, whereas male factor infertility and other specific diagnoses like ovulatory disorders or tubal factors showed no statistically significant associations. Interestingly, polycystic ovary syndrome, which causes ovulatory infertility, was associated with lower ovarian cancer risk, indicating that the mechanism driving infertility influences cancer risk direction. These findings suggest that endometriosis-related infertility may be a key biomarker for identifying women at elevated risk of specific ovarian cancer subtypes.

title: Diagnostic value of a multimodal approach combining inflammatory biomarkers, tumor markers, and ultrasonographic features for borderline ovarian epithelial tumors
summary: Borderline ovarian epithelial tumors (BOETs) demonstrate elevated inflammatory biomarkers (neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio) and tumor markers (CA125, HE4) that occupy an intermediate position between benign and malignant ovarian tumors, reflecting their intermediate biological nature. Specific ultrasonographic features including microcystic patterns, predominantly solid components, rich blood supply, irregular morphology, and thick septa are significantly associated with BOETs and provide valuable diagnostic information. The inflammatory microenvironment within these tumors promotes angiogenesis and metastatic potential through inflammatory cell activation. A multimodal diagnostic approach integrating inflammatory biomarkers, tumor markers, and ultrasonographic findings yields superior diagnostic accuracy compared to single modalities, with the microcystic pattern being particularly specific for BOETs. This combined strategy effectively differentiates BOETs from benign ovarian lesions and helps stratify risk for malignant transformation, providing clinicians with a comprehensive assessment tool for more precise diagnosis and clinical management.

title: A dual-center study: multimodal fusion-based deep learning approach for pathological subtype prediction of type I and type II ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This dual-center study developed a multimodal deep learning model (USECNC + CL) integrating transvaginal ultrasound, enhanced CT, and non-contrast CT imaging with ResNet-50 architecture and cross-attention mechanisms to accurately predict epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) pathological subtypes, achieving an AUC of 0.8. Type I EOC, characterized by genetic stability and indolent progression, typically responds favorably to early cytoreductive surgery but shows reduced chemotherapy responsiveness, while Type II EOC exhibits genomic instability, aggressive behavior, and greater sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors. Enhanced CT vascular features and CA125 levels emerged as the strongest predictive biomarkers, with CA125 demonstrating superior diagnostic contribution compared to imaging features alone. Preoperative prediction of EOC subtype enables clinicians to formulate personalized treatment strategies by guiding both surgical planning and chemotherapy regimen selection, addressing the distinct biological and clinical characteristics of each tumor type.

title: Longer-term aspirin use and subsequent ovarian cancer risk in the Ovarian Cancer Cohort Consortium
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study demonstrates that long-term aspirin use, particularly low-dose formulations, is associated with reduced ovarian cancer risk, with users experiencing a 14% risk reduction after more than 6 years of use. The protective effect is mechanistically attributed to aspirin's inhibition of cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes, which suppress pro-inflammatory mediators involved in cellular proliferation, angiogenesis, invasion, and metastasis, as well as COX-independent pathways including inhibition of Wnt/β-catenin, NF-κB, and mTOR signaling. The risk reduction is particularly pronounced in women with three or more established ovarian cancer risk factors—including endometriosis, obesity, nulliparity, absence of oral contraceptive use, absence of tubal ligation, and family history—where long-term frequent aspirin use confers a 35% risk reduction. Notably, the protective association appears stronger for non-high-grade serous cancers than high-grade serous subtypes, and benefits are evident across multiple age groups, especially women aged 50-60 years. These findings suggest aspirin may enhance immune surveillance against ovarian tumors and represent a potentially modifiable intervention for high-risk populations.

title: Insight into the potential significance of miR-760 and miR-1973 in breast cancer: a comprehensive analysis
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive analysis identifies miR-760 and miR-1973 as significantly elevated microRNAs in breast cancer patients, with miR-760 and miR-1973 upregulated by 51.6% and 358% respectively compared to controls. Both microRNAs are associated with increased breast cancer susceptibility and development, with particularly pronounced elevation in early-stage disease and strong association with late-stage progression and shortened overall survival. MiR-760, located in the intronic region of the BCAR3 gene on chromosome 1 and regulated by estrogen, demonstrated especially robust prognostic value, as patients with elevated miR-760 expression exhibited significantly lower overall survival compared to those with low expression. These microRNAs regulate critical cancer-related biological processes including cell proliferation, differentiation, migration, and apoptosis, and show tissue-specific expression patterns across multiple cancer types. The study establishes miR-760 and miR-1973 as promising non-invasive circulating biomarkers for breast cancer diagnosis, prognosis, and risk stratification, with potential clinical utility in early detection and treatment monitoring.

title: The mortality and blood transfusion
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis demonstrates a strong association with the development of epithelial ovarian cancer, representing a significant clinical concern for patient outcomes.
