The research indicates that endometriosis functions as a risk factor for malignant transformation, with individuals experiencing endometriosis facing elevated susceptibility to epithelial ovarian cancer development. This association underscores the importance of recognizing endometriosis not merely as a benign gynecological condition but as a precursor state requiring heightened surveillance for malignant transformation. The study contributes to understanding the pathological pathways linking benign endometrial tissue growth to ovarian malignancy. These findings suggest that biomarker identification in endometriosis patients could enhance early detection strategies and improve prognostic outcomes for women at increased risk of epithelial ovarian cancer.

title: Aberrant sialylation in ovarian cancers
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: Sialylation, the addition of sialic acids to glycoproteins and glycans, plays a critical role in maintaining normal physiology, but aberrant sialylation is highly prevalent in ovarian cancers and contributes to tumorigenesis, tumor growth, and dissemination. Glycans regulate multiple hallmarks of cancer including sustained proliferative signaling, angiogenesis, tumor immune evasion, and cell-cell interactions, with their sialylation patterns controlled by sialyltransferases (STs) and sialidases (NEUs). Altered expression and activity of these enzymatic regulators leads to aberrant sialylation patterns characterized by increased sialyl Lewis A, sialyl Lewis X, and sialyl Tn antigens, which serve as tumor-associated biomarkers. Increased sialylation on proteins like α1-acid glycoprotein (AGP) enhances immune suppression by inhibiting lymphocyte proliferation, while sialyl Tn-positive mucins impair dendritic cell maturation and promote T-regulatory cell differentiation, facilitating immune evasion. These aberrantly sialylated glycoproteins, including CA125, HE4, and fucosylated α1-antitrypsin, represent promising serum biomarkers for ovarian cancer detection, prognosis assessment, and monitoring therapeutic response. Understanding the dysregulation of the sialylation machinery in epithelial ovarian cancers provides mechanistic insights into cancer biology and identifies novel biomarkers and potential therapeutic targets beyond conventional platinum-paclitaxel chemotherapy.

title: A disposable biosensing system for analysis of CA125 in real human serum samples
summary:
# Summary

This study describes a disposable electrochemical biosensor for detecting CA125, a cancer biomarker with significant clinical relevance to ovarian cancer screening and monitoring. While CA125 is primarily associated with ovarian cancer, the research emphasizes its lack of specificity, as it is also elevated in bladder, breast, lung, liver, pancreatic cancers, endometriosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease. The biosensor was constructed using an indium tin oxide polyethylene (ITO-PET) electrode modified with p-benzoquinone to immobilize CA125 antigens, with anti-CA125 antibodies enabling specific binding detection through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Optimal biosensor performance was achieved using 0.01 M p-benzoquinone and 12 ng/mL anti-CA125 concentration, with at least 45 minutes incubation time required for stable protein immobilization. The developed system demonstrated clinical utility for direct analysis of CA125 in human serum samples and showed capability for simultaneous detection of multiple tumor markers including HE4 and AFP, establishing its potential application in cancer diagnostics and disease monitoring.

title: IgG Gal-ratio, a promising serum biomarker for detecting ovarian cancer in as early as stage I disease
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies IgG galactosylation ratio (IgG Gal-ratio) as a promising serum biomarker for detecting epithelial ovarian cancer, particularly in early-stage disease. The IgG Gal-ratio is significantly elevated in epithelial ovarian cancer patients compared to those with borderline tumors or benign gynecological conditions including endometriomas and adenomyomas, demonstrating superior specificity for distinguishing malignant from non-malignant pelvic disease. Notably, IgG Gal-ratio outperforms CA125 in detecting stage I ovarian cancer (AUC = 0.835 vs. 0.761), and combining both markers into a Gal-CA125 composite further improves diagnostic specificity. The biological mechanism involves decreased IgG galactosylation, an N-glycosylation modification in the IgG-Fc region, which is associated with inflammation, infection, and aging—processes implicated in cancer development. Estrogen and galactosyltransferase activity regulate IgG galactosylation, explaining correlations with post-menopausal status and age. While elevated ratios appear in some inflammatory conditions like tuberculosis, the marker's clinical utility lies in its ability to provide early, pre-operative discrimination of ovarian cancer from the benign gynecological conditions that commonly confound CA125-based diagnosis.

title: Translating Liquid Biopsy into Practice- The Promise and Challenges of Circulating Tumor Cells in Ovarian and Lung Cancer Management
summary: This article examines liquid biopsy approaches for cancer management, with particular relevance to ovarian cancer and its distinction from endometriosis. While serum biomarkers CA-125 and HE4 are standard for ovarian cancer monitoring, CA-125 elevation is not specific to malignancy, as it can also be elevated in benign gynecological conditions such as endometriosis, complicating differential diagnosis. Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) offer improved prognostic value, particularly in advanced ovarian and early-stage lung cancer, where elevated baseline CTC counts correlate with platinum resistance, shorter progression-free survival, and reduced overall survival. Ovarian and lung cancers share common oncogenic mechanisms including PI3K/AKT/mTOR and MAPK pathway activation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and distinct mutational profiles: low-grade serous ovarian carcinomas harbor BRAF, ERBB2, and KRAS mutations, while high-grade serous carcinomas are characterized by p53, BRCA1/2, CTNNB1, and PTEN mutations. CTC-based detection enables real-time monitoring of clinically relevant genomic alterations, resistance mechanisms, and stemness markers (CD44, ALDH1) enriched in platinum-resistant disease, establishing liquid biopsy as a promising tool for personalized cancer management and treatment response prediction.

title: Clinical application of PARP inhibitors and emerging strategies to overcome resistance: a pan-cancer perspective
summary: PARP inhibitors have emerged as a paradigm-shifting therapeutic class approved for treating multiple malignancies including ovarian, breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancers. These agents function by inhibiting PARP1-mediated single-strand break repair, leading to accumulation of double-strand breaks that induce synthetic lethality in homologous recombination-deficient (HRD) tumors, particularly those harboring BRCA1/2 mutations. In ovarian cancer, PARP inhibitors such as olaparib, niraparib, and rucaparib significantly improve progression-free and overall survival; notably, endometriosis-associated Type I epithelial ovarian cancers have distinct molecular characteristics compared to high-grade serous cancers, which are frequently associated with TP53 and BRCA mutations. Clinical trials have demonstrated olaparib's efficacy as maintenance therapy in advanced ovarian cancer and metastatic breast cancer with germline BRCA1/2 mutations, with durable survival benefits observed at extended follow-up periods. Combination strategies are being explored to overcome resistance, including pairing PARP inhibitors with bevacizumab to induce hypoxia-mediated downregulation of homologous recombination repair genes, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy across multiple cancer types.

title: Study of Estrogen Receptor Alpha Gene Polymorphisms (ERα, ESR1) in Women with Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates ESR1 gene polymorphisms (rs2234693 and rs9340799) as biomarkers for ovarian cancer risk, revealing that specific genetic variants significantly increase disease susceptibility. The CC genotype at rs2234693 and GG genotype at rs9340799 each more than double ovarian cancer risk and associate with earlier tumor grades and smaller sizes. These polymorphisms function through altered estrogen receptor alpha (ER-α) expression: the C allele enhances ER-α transcription via B-myb binding, driving proliferation through cyclin D1 upregulation, whereas loss of the protective ER-β isoform—which inhibits proliferation and promotes apoptosis—accelerates cancer development. Notably, patients carrying the CC genotype exhibit worse survival outcomes due to elevated ER-α levels, while T allele carriers show improved survival, establishing ESR1 polymorphisms as functional biomarkers that influence both ovarian cancer risk and prognosis. The study also identifies an inverse relationship between these same polymorphisms and endometriosis risk, suggesting shared hormonal mechanisms underlying the epidemiological link between endometriosis and ovarian cancer.

title: Circulating extracellular vesicle DNA (EV-DNA) and cell-free DNA in ovarian cancer: pathological relevance and diagnostic applications
journal: Extracellular Vesicles and Circulating Nucleic Acids
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer presents a significant clinical challenge due to late-stage diagnosis and peritoneal metastasis, motivating the development of liquid biopsy approaches that detect circulating nucleic acid biomarkers. Extracellular vesicle DNA (EV-DNA) demonstrates superior diagnostic and prognostic utility compared to cell-free DNA because it is more protected, longer in length, and more abundant in tumor-derived vesicles, with whole-genome sequencing revealing cancer-associated mutations in TP53, RTK-RAS, and Wnt pathways in high-grade serous ovarian cancer. The CD63 protein plays a critical role in packaging nuclear DNA into exosomes, and elevated EV-mitochondrial DNA (EV-mDNA) correlates with late-stage disease and chemoresistance, particularly in cisplatin-resistant cells that release higher quantities of DNA-laden vesicles conferring chemoresistant phenotypes to sensitive cells. Chemotherapy exposure and DNA damage further increase EV-DNA release, which when internalized by phagocytes triggers inflammatory responses marked by elevated IL-1β and IL-18 that promote treatment resistance and cancer progression. Optimized isolation methods combining ultracentrifugation with density gradient centrifugation enhance EV-DNA recovery and purity, enabling targeted next-generation sequencing to identify clinically actionable mutations for improved ovarian cancer monitoring and therapeutic management.

title: Oral contraceptives exposure may reduce the risk of ovarian cancer: a meta-analysis based on cohort studies
summary: This meta-analysis of cohort studies demonstrates that oral contraceptive (OC) use significantly reduces ovarian cancer risk, with protection persisting even after discontinuation. The preventive effect is dose- and duration-dependent, occurring primarily with exposure exceeding five years and reducing ovarian cancer incidence by approximately 34% in European and Asian cohorts, though the benefit is less pronounced in American populations. The underlying mechanism involves OC-mediated suppression of ovulation through estrogen and progesterone-induced inhibition of luteinizing and follicle-stimulating hormone surges, thereby reducing lifetime ovulation cycles and decreasing DNA damage accumulation in ovarian epithelial cells caused by reactive oxygen species during repeated ovulatory events. Additional protective mechanisms include OC-induced necrosis of fallopian tube epithelial cells and inhibition of inflammatory signaling pathways. The study also identifies key ovarian cancer risk factors including advanced age at menopause, early menarche, nulliparity, and mutations in TP53, BRCA1, BRCA2, and PTEN genes, while lifestyle interventions including Mediterranean diet adherence, regular exercise, and healthy body weight further reduce cancer risk.

title: Somatic mutations in cervicovaginal samples: assessing their role in ovarian cancer detection and prognosis
summary: This study evaluated somatic DNA variants in cervicovaginal samples as potential biomarkers for ovarian cancer detection and prognosis. Pathogenic and likely pathogenic variants were detected in 25.6% of ovarian cancer patients' Pap smears, with notably higher detection rates in endometrial aspirates (77.8%) and tumor samples (69.2%), suggesting these variants originate from tumor tissue migrating through the fallopian tubes. The most frequently mutated genes differed between sample types: TP53, ARHGAP35, and CTCF in Pap smears from cancer patients versus TP53, PIK3CA, and JAK1 in controls, with TP53, ARID1A, and KRAS predominating in actual tumors. Detection of variants in cervicovaginal samples was strongly associated with poorer overall and disease-free survival, with higher sensitivity in advanced-stage and high-grade cancers but minimal detection in early-stage disease. While the variants showed limited diagnostic specificity (66.7%) for cancer detection, their presence conveyed significant prognostic value, as 72.7% of variant-positive patients experienced relapse compared to variant-negative patients.

title: Composite proteomic and metabolomic plasma biomarkers for detection of colorectal, lung and ovarian cancers
summary: This study integrated plasma proteomics and metabolomics to identify composite biomarker panels for detecting colorectal, lung, and ovarian cancers. Using affinity proteomics to measure 165 plasma proteins and NMR-based metabolomics to analyze 244 metabolites, researchers identified cancer-specific protein signatures with high diagnostic accuracy: colorectal cancer was distinguished by elevated CEACAM5, FLT1, and IL19 combined with reduced ferritin (AUC 0.89); lung cancer showed elevated FNDC5, MDK, PLAUR, and CEACAM5 (AUC 0.91); and ovarian cancer was characterized by MUC16/CA125 and plasminogen (AUC 0.97). Additionally, metabolite profiles demonstrated superior utility for tumor staging rather than early detection, particularly in lung and ovarian cancers. The diagnostic performance of these composite protein biomarkers matched or exceeded conventional screening tests such as FIT for colorectal cancer, suggesting potential for non-invasive blood-based cancer detection and staging. External validation using the CancerSeek dataset confirmed the robustness of these findings across independent cohorts.
